Answers: 1. liberator  2. metamorphosis  3. voltage  4. censor  5. juror  6. warfare  7. confederation  8. illegal  9. instrument  10. supplied
tables: Not allowed by law or rules
It is illegal to steal someone else's property.
A person who follows the teachings of Jesus Christ
My friend is a Christian and goes to church every Sunday.
The act of going into a place, or the way to get in
You need a ticket for entry to the concert.
A tool or device used to make music or do a specific job
She plays the piano, which is a musical instrument.
Gave or provided something that was needed
The teacher supplied us with new books for our class.
A pleasing combination of things that work well together; peace
The choir sang in harmony, making beautiful music.
A way of looking at or thinking about something
Everyone has a different perspective on what makes a good story.
To remove or hide parts of something considered inappropriate
The teacher had to censor some parts of the film before showing it.
The activity of fighting in a war
The soldiers trained hard for the warfare they would face.
A member of a strict English Protestant group from the 16th and 17th centuries
The Puritans believed in living a simple, strict religious life.
A big change in the form or shape of something
The caterpillar went through a metamorphosis into a butterfly.
A person chosen to help decide a court case
The juror listened carefully to all the evidence before deciding.
A person who creates something new
Thomas Edison was an inventor who created the light bulb.
An action to stop people or goods getting in or out of a place
The army set up a blockade to prevent supplies reaching the city.
To think carefully about something; to show an image
After reading the fable, take a moment to reflect on its meaning.
The amount of electrical energy pushing through wires
The battery has a high voltage to power the toy car.
A room or space used for a special purpose
The king held a meeting in his large chamber.
Showed or bounced back an image or light; thought carefully
The lake reflected the beautiful trees around it.
The official approval of something, like a law or agreement
The ratification of the new school rules made everyone happy.
The act of being set free from control or restrictions
The emancipation of the slaves meant they could live freely.
The quality of trying hard even when something is difficult
Her determination to finish the race helped her overcome every challenge.
The belief that your country is important and worth being proud of
Nationalism can make people feel proud during national celebrations.
A connection or bond between people
My sister and I have a very strong relationship.
A group of states working together while keeping their own governments
The confederation of states helped them cooperate during difficult times.
Supplies or food needed for a journey or purpose
We packed enough provisions for our camping trip.
A group of people who make laws for a country
The parliament met to discuss new rules for schools.
A person who helps free others from control or oppression
The liberator helped the people escape from unfair rules.
A feeling of shame or loss of respect
He felt a lot of disgrace after cheating on the test.

paragraphs: First Conditional
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 2 Grammar

This grammar guide will help you understand and use the First Conditional with confidence. Work through each section carefully, practise the exercises, and check your answers using the answer key at the end.
What Is the First Conditional?

The First Conditional is a way of talking about things that are likely or possible in the future. We use it when we believe there is a real chance that something will happen, and we want to describe what the result will be.
For example:
If you study hard, you will pass your test.
If it rains, we will cancel the picnic.
Each First Conditional sentence has two parts:
The condition (the if-clause): this describes what might happen.
The result (the main clause): this describes what will happen if the condition is met.

The First Conditional is called 'first' because it deals with real, likely possibilities. It is one of the most common and useful grammar structures in English. You will find it in everyday conversations, in news articles, in science writing, and in stories.
Understanding the First Conditional helps you:
Talk about future plans and possibilities
Give advice and warnings
Make predictions based on evidence
Describe cause and effect relationships
How to Form the First Conditional
The Basic Pattern
The First Conditional follows a clear and simple pattern:
If + subject + present simple, subject + will + base verb
Let us break this down step by step:
Start with the word If.
Add a subject (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) and a verb in the present simple tense.
Put a comma after the if-clause.
Write the result using will (or will not / won't) followed by the base form of the verb.
Examples:
If she practises every day, she will improve her speed.
If the team works together, they will win the tournament.
If you eat too much sugar, you will feel tired.
If we protect the forest, the ecosystem will survive.
If he does not sleep well, he will not perform his best.
Swapping the Clauses
You can put the result clause first and the if-clause second. When you do this, you do not need a comma:
If you train hard, you will succeed. (comma after the if-clause)
You will succeed if you train hard. (no comma needed)
Both sentences have exactly the same meaning. Using different orders makes your writing more varied and interesting.
Using 'Unless' in the First Conditional
The word unless is a very useful addition to your First Conditional toolkit. It means if not. Using 'unless' makes your sentences sound more natural and confident.
Compare these two sentences:
If you do not drink enough water, you will feel tired.
Unless you drink enough water, you will feel tired.
Both sentences mean exactly the same thing. The word 'unless' replaces 'if not' and makes the sentence shorter and smoother.
More examples:
Unless you practise, you will not improve. (= If you do not practise...)
The match will be cancelled unless the rain stops. (= ...if the rain does not stop.)
Unless the team cooperates, they will lose. (= If the team does not cooperate...)
She will not finish the race unless she trains harder. (= ...if she does not train harder.)
Important Rule: No Double Negatives with Unless
Because 'unless' already means 'if not', you must not use a negative verb after it. Adding 'not' after 'unless' creates a confusing double negative.
Incorrect: Unless you don't hurry, we will be late.
Correct: Unless you hurry, we will be late.
Incorrect: Unless she doesn't study, she will fail.
Correct: Unless she studies, she will fail.
Variations: Other Modal Verbs
The most common result clause uses will, but you can also use other modal verbs to express different shades of meaning:
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using 'will' in the if-clause
The if-clause must use the present simple tense, not 'will'. This is the most common mistake learners make.
Incorrect: If he will eat too much, he will get sick.
Correct: If he eats too much, he will get sick.
Incorrect: If she will study hard, she will pass.
Correct: If she studies hard, she will pass.
Mistake 2: Forgetting the comma
When the if-clause comes first, you need a comma before the result clause.
Incorrect: If it rains we will stay home.
Correct: If it rains, we will stay home.
Remember: no comma is needed when the result comes first.
We will stay home if it rains. (correct — no comma)
Mistake 3: Using the wrong verb form after 'will'
After 'will', always use the base form of the verb (the infinitive without 'to').
Incorrect: The team will wins the match.
Correct: The team will win the match.
Incorrect: She will to improve her time.
Correct: She will improve her time.
Mistake 4: Double negatives with 'unless'
As we learned earlier, do not use 'not' or 'don't' after 'unless'.
Incorrect: Unless you don't try, you will fail.
Correct: Unless you try, you will fail.
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Complete the Sentences
Fill in the blanks using the correct form of the verb in brackets.
If it __________ (rain), we __________ (stay) indoors.
If she __________ (practise) her speech, she __________ (succeed).
If we __________ (not protect) the environment, the ecosystem __________ (collapse).
If he __________ (eat) healthy food, he __________ (have) more energy.
If the team __________ (cooperate), they __________ (win) the match.
If pollution __________ (increase), the ozone layer __________ (deteriorate).
If you __________ (not drink) enough water, you __________ (feel) tired.
If she __________ (finish) her homework, she __________ (go) to the park.
If the forest __________ (die), many animals __________ (lose) their homes.
If we __________ (plant) more trees, the air __________ (become) cleaner.
Exercise 2: Rewrite Using 'Unless'
Rewrite each sentence using unless instead of if not.
If you do not hurry, you will miss the bus.
If they do not cooperate, the project will fail.
If she does not rest, she will feel exhausted.
If we do not save water, there will be a shortage.
If he does not train harder, he will not make the team.
If the students do not study, they will not pass the exam.
Exercise 3: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence contains a mistake. Find it and rewrite the sentence correctly.
If she will study hard, she will pass the exam.
If we plant more trees the air will become cleaner.
Unless you don't eat breakfast, you will feel hungry.
If the rain will stop, we will go outside.
The team will wins if they practise every day.
If he drinks water, he will feels better.
Unless they doesn't cooperate, the team will succeed.
If she eat healthy food, she will have energy.
Exercise 4: Match the Halves
Match each condition on the left with the correct result on the right.
Conditions:
If you read every day,
If the weather is sunny,
If she eats a balanced diet,
If the players argue,
If we recycle more,
Results (scrambled):
a) she will be healthy and strong.
b) we will help protect the environment.
c) your English will improve.
d) we will play outside.
e) they will lose the match.
Exercise 5: Write Your Own Sentences
Write six First Conditional sentences of your own. Use a different topic for each one:
A sentence about sports or exercise
A sentence about healthy eating
A sentence about the environment
A sentence about school or studying
A sentence using unless
A sentence using might instead of will
Exercise 6: First Conditional Chain
The result of one sentence becomes the condition for the next. Continue the chain by writing at least four sentences.
Start: If I eat a healthy breakfast, I will have more energy.
Continue: If I have more energy, I will...
Exercise 7: Story Connection
Read the story 'When the Leaves Stop Speaking' and find at least five examples of the First Conditional. For each one:
Write out the full sentence.
Underline the condition and circle the result.
Explain in your own words what the sentence means.
Answer Key
Exercise 1 Answers
rains, will stay
practises, will succeed
do not protect, will collapse
eats, will have
cooperate / cooperates, will win
increases, will deteriorate
do not drink, will feel
finishes, will go
dies, will lose
plant, will become
Exercise 2 Answers
Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.
Unless they cooperate, the project will fail.
Unless she rests, she will feel exhausted.
Unless we save water, there will be a shortage.
Unless he trains harder, he will not make the team.
Unless the students study, they will not pass the exam.
Exercise 3 Answers
If she studies hard, she will pass the exam.
If we plant more trees, the air will become cleaner.
Unless you eat breakfast, you will feel hungry.
If the rain stops, we will go outside.
The team will win if they practise every day.
If he drinks water, he will feel better.
Unless they cooperate, the team will not succeed.
If she eats healthy food, she will have energy.
Exercise 4 Answers
If you read every day, c) your English will improve.
If the weather is sunny, d) we will play outside.
If she eats a balanced diet, a) she will be healthy and strong.
If the players argue, e) they will lose the match.
If we recycle more, b) we will help protect the environment.
Exercise 5 and 6 Answers

Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the present simple in the if-clause and will + base verb in the result clause. For the chain, check that each result logically becomes the next condition.
Possible chain:
If I eat a healthy breakfast, I will have more energy.
If I have more energy, I will train harder at football practice.
If I train harder at football practice, the coach will notice my improvement.
If the coach notices my improvement, he will pick me for the team.
If I play for the team, I will learn the value of teamwork.
Exercise 7 Answers
Answers will vary. Examples from the story include:
"If the forest continues to deteriorate, the whole ecosystem will collapse."
"If we lose it, we lose more than trees."
"If pollution increases, the ozone will continue to deteriorate."
"If we do nothing, its silence will grow loud."
"If we act now, we can save it."
"If enough people believe a story, it becomes a reason to act."
"If people choose to change." (implied result: forests return)
tables:
💡  Memory Trick
Think of the First Conditional as an 'If-Then' situation.
If = present simple (the condition). Then = will + base verb (the result).
Never use 'will' in the if-clause!

💡  Top Tip
To check your 'unless' sentence, try replacing 'unless' with 'if not'.
If the sentence still makes sense, you have used 'unless' correctly!
If she trains, she will improve.
If it rains, we might stay indoors.
If she is late, she could miss the start.
If you finish early, you can play outside.
If it snows, we should wear warm clothes.
If pollution increases, we must act.

⭐  Did You Know?
The First Conditional is very common in science writing. Scientists use it to describe cause and effect:
If the temperature rises by 2 degrees, sea levels will increase.
You will find many First Conditional sentences in your unit story too!

💬  Well Done!
You have completed the First Conditional grammar guide.
Keep using the First Conditional in your own writing and speaking.
The more you practise, the more natural it will feel. Your AI Tutor is always here to help!

paragraphs: Unit 2: Sports and Health
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 2: Sports and Health! In this unit, you will explore the exciting world of sports, fitness, and healthy living. You will read about how athletes train, what makes a healthy lifestyle, and how sports bring communities together across East Africa and beyond. Along the way, you will learn powerful new vocabulary, master an important grammar structure called the First Conditional, and develop your writing and speaking skills through creative projects like writing a biography and delivering sports commentary.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use and understand 28 new vocabulary words related to science, the environment, and health
Identify and use the First Conditional to talk about possible future events and their results
Use unless as an alternative to if not in conditional sentences
Read and understand an informative passage about the science behind sports and health
Write a short biography of a sportsperson using the third person
Write and deliver a piece of sports commentary using exciting and descriptive language
Distinguish between facts and opinions in texts about health and fitness
Practise speaking and listening skills through role-play, interviews, and presentations
Evaluate health habits and understand the importance of teamwork
Key Vocabulary

Before you begin reading, take a moment to study the important words you will meet in this unit. These words come from your unit story and from the topics of science, health, and the environment. Knowing these words will help you understand the texts and complete the activities with confidence. Read through the table below, say each word aloud, and try to use it in a short sentence of your own.
Reading: Training Champions — The Science Behind Sports
Read the passage below carefully. Think about the main ideas, notice any new vocabulary, and pay attention to sentences that use the First Conditional structure (if + present simple, will + verb).

In many East African communities, sports have always been more than a game. From the famous long-distance runners of Kenya and Ethiopia to the basketball courts of Mogadishu and the football pitches of Nairobi, physical activity brings people together and keeps them strong. But have you ever wondered what happens inside your body when you exercise? The answer involves some fascinating biological processes that scientists are still studying today.

When you run, swim, or play football, your muscles need more energy. Your heart beats faster to pump blood around your body. This blood carries oxygen to your muscles so they can keep working. If you train regularly, your heart will become stronger and more efficient. It will pump more blood with each beat, which means it will not have to work as hard during rest. This is why athletes often have lower resting heart rates than people who do not exercise.

Nutrition plays an important role in sports performance. If you eat a balanced diet with plenty of fruit, vegetables, and whole grains, your body will have the fuel it needs to perform well. Protein from foods like beans, lentils, eggs, and fish helps your muscles repair and grow stronger after exercise. If you do not eat enough healthy food, your performance will deteriorate over time. Harmful bacteria can also enter your body through unclean food or water, so hygiene is an important part of staying healthy.

Water is just as important as food for any athlete. When you exercise, your body loses water through sweat. If you do not drink enough water, you may feel tired, dizzy, or unwell. Doctors recommend drinking water before, during, and after physical activity. The environmental conditions matter too. If the weather is very hot, athletes must take extra care to stay hydrated and avoid heat exhaustion.

Sleep is another vital part of an athlete's routine. During sleep, your body repairs itself. Growth hormones are released, muscles rebuild, and the brain processes everything it has learned during the day. If a young athlete does not get enough sleep, their reaction times will slow down and their concentration will suffer. Most health experts say that children your age need between nine and eleven hours of sleep every night.

In East Africa, many young athletes dream of competing at the international level. Abdi Bile from Somalia became a world champion in the 1500 metres in 1987. Mo Farah, who was born in Mogadishu, won multiple Olympic gold medals in long-distance running. Tegla Loroupe from Kenya became the first African woman to win a major marathon. These athletes did not succeed by talent alone. They trained with discipline, ate well, rested properly, and listened to their coaches. Their stories show that if you combine hard work with good health habits, you will give yourself the best chance of success.

Sports also teach important life skills. If you play in a team, you will learn to cooperate with others, communicate clearly, and handle both victory and defeat with grace. Team sports like football, basketball, and volleyball require players to trust each other and work towards a shared goal. Individual sports like running and swimming build self-discipline and mental toughness. In a multicultural society, sport is one of the most powerful ways to bring different people together.

The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, encouraged physical activity and strength. Swimming, archery, and horse riding are among the activities recommended in Islamic tradition as beneficial for the body and the mind. Keeping your body healthy is considered a trust and a responsibility.

Whether you dream of becoming a champion or simply want to feel healthy and strong, remember this: if you take care of your body today, it will take care of you tomorrow. Good health is not a gift that arrives by accident. It is the result of daily choices — the food you eat, the exercise you do, the sleep you get, and the way you look after your mind as well as your body.
Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the passage, answer the following questions. Write your answers in full sentences. Try to include vocabulary from the passage where you can.
Comprehension Questions
What happens to your heart when you exercise regularly?
Why is nutrition important for athletes? Name two types of food that help the body.
What can happen if you do not drink enough water during exercise?
Why is sleep important for a young athlete? Give two reasons from the passage.
Name three East African athletes mentioned in the passage. What did each one achieve?
According to the passage, what life skills can sports teach you?
Think Deeper
The passage says that good health is 'the result of daily choices'. What daily choices can you make to stay healthy? List at least three.
The author writes that sport is 'one of the most powerful ways to bring different people together'. Do you agree? Give a reason for your answer and an example from your own life.
Find two examples of the First Conditional in the passage. Write them out and explain what condition and result each one describes.
Find one fact and one opinion in the passage. Explain how you can tell the difference between them.
Vocabulary in Context
Go back to the reading passage and find the words listed below. For each word, explain what it means based on the way it is used in the passage. Write your answer in a full sentence.
efficient (paragraph 2): What does it mean when we say the heart becomes more efficient?
deteriorate (paragraph 3): What will deteriorate if you do not eat healthy food?
bacteria (paragraph 3): Why are bacteria mentioned in a passage about sports and health?
environmental (paragraph 4): What environmental conditions can affect athletes?
multicultural (paragraph 7): How does the word multicultural relate to the role of sport?
Grammar Focus: First Conditional

In the reading passage, you noticed several sentences that followed a special pattern. They described a possible situation in the future and the result that would follow. This pattern is called the First Conditional, and it is one of the most useful grammar structures in English. Mastering it will help you talk and write about plans, warnings, advice, and predictions with real confidence.
What Is the First Conditional?

The First Conditional is used to talk about events that are likely or possible in the future. We use it when we believe there is a real chance that the condition will happen. It connects a condition (something that might happen) to a result (what will happen if the condition is met).
Look at this example from the reading passage:
If you train regularly, your heart will become stronger.
The condition is 'if you train regularly'. This is something that might really happen. The result is 'your heart will become stronger'. This is what will follow if the condition is met.
How to Form the First Conditional
The First Conditional follows a clear pattern:
If + subject + present simple, subject + will + base verb
Here are some more examples:
If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the match.
If she practises every day, she will improve her speed.
If the team works together, they will win the tournament.
If you eat too much sugar, you will feel tired.
Notice that the if-clause (the condition) uses the present simple tense, even though we are talking about the future. This is a very important rule. Do not use 'will' in the if-clause.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Many learners make the mistake of putting 'will' in the if-clause. Here is how to fix it:
Incorrect: If he will eat too much, he will get sick.
Correct: If he eats too much, he will get sick.
Another common mistake is forgetting the comma when the if-clause comes first:
Incorrect: If it rains we will stay home.
Correct: If it rains, we will stay home.
However, if the result comes first, you do not need a comma:
We will stay home if it rains. (no comma needed)
Swapping the Clauses
You can put the result clause first and the if-clause second. The meaning stays the same, but notice that you do not need a comma when the if-clause comes at the end:
If you study hard, you will pass the test.
You will pass the test if you study hard.
Both sentences mean exactly the same thing. Changing the order can make your writing more varied and interesting.
Using 'Unless' Instead of 'If Not'
The word unless means 'if not'. It is used in the First Conditional to express a negative condition. Using 'unless' makes your sentences sound more natural and mature.
If you do not drink enough water, you will feel tired.
Unless you drink enough water, you will feel tired.
Both sentences above mean the same thing. Here are more examples:
Unless you practise, you will not improve. (= If you do not practise, you will not improve.)
The match will be cancelled unless the rain stops. (= ...if the rain does not stop.)
Unless the team cooperates, they will lose. (= If the team does not cooperate, they will lose.)
Important: Do not use a negative verb after 'unless', because 'unless' already carries the negative meaning:
Incorrect: Unless you don't hurry, we will be late.
Correct: Unless you hurry, we will be late.
Using Other Modal Verbs
Instead of 'will', you can sometimes use other modal verbs in the result clause to express different meanings:
If it rains, we might stay indoors. (less certain)
If she is late, she could miss the start. (possible consequence)
If you finish early, you can play outside. (permission)
If it snows, we should wear warm clothes. (advice)
Grammar Practice
Now it is time to practise what you have learned about the First Conditional. Try each activity carefully and check your answers using the answer key at the end of this section.
Activity 1: Complete the Sentences
Fill in the blanks using the correct form of the verb in brackets.
If it __________ (rain) tomorrow, we __________ (cancel) the football match.
If she __________ (practise) her speech, she __________ (succeed) in her presentation.
If we __________ (plant) more trees, the air __________ (become) cleaner.
If you __________ (not eat) breakfast, you __________ (feel) tired at school.
If the forest __________ (continue) to deteriorate, the ecosystem __________ (collapse).
If they __________ (save) enough money, they __________ (buy) new sports equipment.
If the pollution __________ (increase), the ozone layer __________ (deteriorate).
If Amina __________ (study) hard, she __________ (pass) her science exam.
Activity 2: Rewrite Using 'Unless'
Rewrite each sentence using unless instead of if not. The meaning must stay the same.
If you do not hurry, you will miss the bus.
If the players do not cooperate, they will lose the match.
If she does not drink enough water, she will feel dizzy.
If we do not protect the forest, the ecosystem will collapse.
If they do not train every day, they will not win the tournament.
Activity 3: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence below contains a mistake. Rewrite it correctly.
If he will study hard, he will pass the test.
If it rains we will cancel the picnic.
Unless you don't try, you will never succeed.
If she will eat healthy food, she will feel better.
The team will wins if they practise every day.
Activity 4: Write Your Own First Conditional Sentences
Write five sentences of your own using the First Conditional. Each sentence must be about sports, health, or fitness. Try to use at least two vocabulary words from this unit.
Example: If the athlete trains every day, her performance will improve.
Activity 5: First Conditional Chain
In this fun activity, the result of one sentence becomes the condition for the next. Continue the chain by writing at least three more sentences.
Start: If I eat a healthy breakfast, I will have more energy.
Next: If I have more energy, I will...
Answer Key: Grammar Practice
Activity 1:
rains, will cancel
practises, will succeed
plant, will become
do not eat, will feel
continues, will collapse
save, will buy
increases, will deteriorate
studies, will pass
Activity 2:
Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.
Unless the players cooperate, they will lose the match.
Unless she drinks enough water, she will feel dizzy.
Unless we protect the forest, the ecosystem will collapse.
Unless they train every day, they will not win the tournament.
Activity 3:
If he studies hard, he will pass the test. (Remove 'will' from the if-clause.)
If it rains, we will cancel the picnic. (Add a comma after the if-clause.)
Unless you try, you will never succeed. (Remove the double negative.)
If she eats healthy food, she will feel better. (Remove 'will' from the if-clause.)
The team will win if they practise every day. (Use the base form after 'will'.)
Activity 4: Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the present simple in the if-clause and will + base verb in the result clause.
Activity 5: Answers will vary. A possible chain:
If I eat a healthy breakfast, I will have more energy.
If I have more energy, I will run faster during training.
If I run faster during training, the coach will put me in the team.
If the coach puts me in the team, we will have a better chance of winning.
Writing Workshop

In this section, you will use what you have learned about vocabulary, grammar, and reading to produce two pieces of writing. Take your time, plan your ideas before you start, and enjoy the creative process!
Writing Task 1: Biography of a Sportsperson

A biography is the story of a person's life written by someone else. It is written in the third person, using pronouns like he, she, and they. Biographies focus on facts, achievements, and important events in the person's life.

Your task is to write a short biography (150 to 250 words) about a sportsperson you admire. This can be someone famous, someone from your community, or even someone you have invented. Your biography should include:
The person's name, nationality, and sport
When and where they were born or where they grew up
How they started playing their sport
Their main achievements or what makes them special
At least three vocabulary words from this unit (underline them)
At least two First Conditional sentences that describe what might happen in their future career
A closing sentence that explains why this person inspires you
Here is a model biography to help you:
Abdi Bile: Somalia's Running Legend

Abdi Bile was born in Mogadishu, Somalia, in 1962. He grew up in a country where running was part of everyday life, and he showed efficient running form from a very young age. He trained on dusty roads and open fields before earning a place at George Mason University in the United States.

In 1987, Abdi Bile became the World Champion in the 1500 metres at the World Athletics Championships in Rome. His victory was a source of enormous pride for all Somalis. Even during difficult times in his country, he continued to represent Somalia on the world stage. He used his imagination and determination to overcome obstacles that would have stopped many others.

If young Somali athletes follow his example, they will achieve great things. If they train with discipline and dedication, the world will see even more champions from East Africa. Abdi Bile's story reminds us that if you believe in yourself and work hard, your dreams can come true.
Writing Task 2: Sports Commentary

A sports commentary is a spoken or written description of a sporting event as it happens. Sports commentators use exciting, descriptive language to bring the action to life for people who cannot see the event. They use short, energetic sentences and the present tense to make the listener feel as if they are watching the action live.
Your task is to write a short sports commentary (100 to 150 words) describing an exciting moment in a game or race. Choose any sport you like. Your commentary should include:
Short, energetic sentences that create excitement
Present tense verbs to make it feel live (e.g., 'She runs! She scores!')
At least two vocabulary words from this unit
At least one First Conditional sentence (e.g., 'If she gets past the defender, she will score!')
Descriptive adjectives and adverbs to paint a picture (e.g., 'incredible speed', 'brilliantly')
Here is a model commentary to inspire you:
The Final Minute

And we are into the final minute of this incredible match! The crowd is on its feet. Halima receives the ball on the right wing. She is quick, she is efficient, and she means business. She dribbles past one defender, then another! The goalkeeper watches nervously. If Halima shoots now, she will have a clear chance at goal. She pulls her foot back... she strikes! The ball flies through the air like a flash of light. GOAL! What an absolutely brilliant finish! The crowd erupts! If this team continues to play like this, they will be unstoppable! What a moment for the tournament!
Editing Checklist for Both Writing Tasks
After writing your first draft, use this checklist to improve your work:
Have I used vocabulary words from this unit?
Have I included First Conditional sentences?
Is my biography written in the third person?
Is my commentary written in the present tense with short, exciting sentences?
Have I checked my spelling and punctuation?
Does each piece have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Have I read my work aloud to check that it sounds natural?
Listening and Speaking
Good communication is not just about writing. In this section, you will practise your speaking and listening skills through fun activities connected to sports and health.
Activity 1: Sports Interview Role-Play
Work with a partner or ask your AI Tutor to role-play an interview. One person plays a journalist and the other plays a famous athlete who has just won a big competition.
The journalist should ask at least five questions. Here are some ideas:
How did you first become interested in your sport?
What does your daily training routine look like?
What do you eat to stay healthy and strong?
How do you and your teammates cooperate during competitions?
If a young person wants to become an athlete, what advice would you give them?
The athlete should answer in full sentences, using vocabulary from this unit and at least two First Conditional sentences in their answers.
Activity 2: Health Advice Presentation
Prepare a short presentation (two to three minutes) on one of the following topics. Use at least three First Conditional sentences and three vocabulary words.
Why sleep is important for young athletes
How to eat a healthy and balanced diet
The benefits of team sports for your community
How to stay safe and healthy during exercise in hot weather
When presenting, speak clearly, make eye contact with your audience, and use your notes as a guide rather than reading them word for word.
Activity 3: Fact or Opinion? Listening Game

Your teacher or AI Tutor will read out a list of statements about sports and health. After each statement, decide whether it is a fact (something that can be proven true) or an opinion (what someone thinks or believes). Hold up a card or say your answer aloud.
Here are some statements to get you started:
Running helps strengthen the heart.
Football is the best sport in the world.
Children need nine to eleven hours of sleep each night.
Mo Farah won multiple Olympic gold medals.
Everyone should play a team sport.
Drinking water during exercise is important.
Swimming is more fun than running.
Abdi Bile became World Champion in 1987.
After the activity, discuss with your partner: How can you tell the difference between a fact and an opinion? What key words or phrases help you decide?
Activity 4: Sports Commentary Performance
Using the sports commentary you wrote in the Writing Workshop, practise performing it aloud. Imagine you are a real sports commentator on television or radio.
Tips for a great performance:
Speak with energy and enthusiasm — your voice should rise and fall to match the action
Use pauses for dramatic effect before big moments
Change your speed — speak faster during exciting moments and slower during tense build-ups
Record yourself and listen back — does it sound exciting? Would you want to keep listening?
If you can, perform your commentary for a partner or family member. Ask them for feedback on your delivery and expression.
Critical Thinking: Health Habits and Teamwork

This section helps you think more deeply about the themes of this unit. There are no right or wrong answers here — the goal is to develop your thinking skills and learn to support your ideas with reasons and evidence.
Evaluating Health Habits

Think about your own daily habits. For each area below, give yourself a score from 1 (I need to improve a lot) to 5 (I am doing very well). Then write one sentence explaining what you could do better.
Eating: Do I eat a variety of healthy foods, including fruit, vegetables, and protein?
Drinking water: Do I drink enough water throughout the day, especially during physical activity?
Sleep: Do I get nine to eleven hours of sleep each night?
Exercise: Do I do at least thirty minutes of physical activity every day?
Hygiene: Do I wash my hands before eating and after using the bathroom?

After completing your self-assessment, write a short paragraph using First Conditional sentences to describe what will change if you improve one of your habits. For example: If I start eating breakfast every morning, I will have more energy at school. If I have more energy, I will be able to concentrate better in my lessons.
Evaluating Teamwork
Think about a time when you worked as part of a team — in a sport, a school project, or a community activity. Answer these questions in full sentences:
What was the team trying to achieve?
How did the team members cooperate and share responsibilities?
Were there any disagreements or problems? How were they resolved?
What would have happened if one person had not done their part?
What did you learn about teamwork from this experience?

Finally, write three pieces of advice for someone who is about to join a team for the first time. Use First Conditional sentences. For example: If you listen to your teammates, you will build trust and respect.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I do not understand. Can you explain the First Conditional in a different way?"
Practise speaking: "Can you interview me about sports and health?"
Get feedback: "Can you check my biography and tell me what to improve?"
Ask for more practice: "Can you give me more First Conditional exercises?"
Prepare for your teacher: "What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning

You have worked hard throughout this unit! Use the checklist below to see how much you have learned. Tick each statement that you feel confident about. If there are any areas where you are not yet sure, go back and review that section before moving on.
I can use and explain at least 20 vocabulary words from this unit.
I understand what the First Conditional is and can form it correctly.
I can use unless as an alternative to if not in conditional sentences.
I know that the if-clause uses the present simple, not 'will'.
I can read a passage and find the main ideas and supporting details.
I can tell the difference between a fact and an opinion.
I can write a biography in the third person with facts and achievements.
I can write a piece of sports commentary using the present tense and exciting language.
I can use First Conditional sentences in my own writing and speaking.
I can take part in a role-play interview and speak in full sentences.
I understand the importance of health habits: nutrition, hydration, sleep, and exercise.
I can evaluate teamwork and explain why cooperation is important in sports and life.
Getting Ready for Unit 3
Make sure you know all the Unit 2 vocabulary words. Review any you find difficult.
Keep using the First Conditional in your daily conversations and writing.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 3? Can you help me get ready?"
Well done — you have completed Unit 2: Sports and Health! Remember, every step you take towards learning English makes you stronger and more confident. Keep going!
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson for Unit 2. It covers reading, grammar, writing, and speaking.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (First Conditional), and the Story.
Work through a section each day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are unsure about any word or idea in this lesson, ask your AI Tutor.
Try saying: What does the word 'deteriorate' mean? or Can you explain the First Conditional to me?
Related to the physical features of the Earth and how places are arranged
The geographic features of our country include mountains, rivers, and valleys.
At the beginning or in the first place
Originally, the story was written as a book before it became a movie.
The ability to create ideas or pictures in your mind
With her imagination, she can turn a simple box into a spaceship.
The ability to make choices and take action
In our class, we have the agency to choose our own projects.
A person who speaks for God or tells what will happen in the future
The prophet warned the people about the coming storm.
To become worse or to decline in quality
If you do not take care of your bike, it will start to deteriorate and will not work well.
Doing something in a way that saves time and energy
She is very efficient at finishing her homework quickly.
Actions or words that encourage people to go against their government or authority
The group was arrested for sedition because they were trying to make people protest against the government.
The way something feels when you touch it, like rough or smooth
The texture of the sandpaper is rough, while the texture of the silk is smooth.
A three-dimensional artwork made by shaping materials like stone, metal, or clay
The sculpture of a lion was made from marble and looked very realistic.
The art and science of designing and building structures
The architecture of the new school is modern and very beautiful.
A sacred or holy writing, often from a religious book
Many people read scripture to learn about their faith.
A huge explosion that happens when a star dies, making it very bright for a short time
The supernova was so bright that it could be seen from Earth.
A special kind of gas in the air that helps protect us from the sun's harmful rays
The ozone layer is important because it keeps us safe from too much sunlight.
Something that gives off dangerous energy or particles
The scientist explained that the rocks in the mine are radioactive and can be harmful.
Tiny living things that can only be seen with a microscope; some can make you sick, while others are helpful
Bacteria are so small that you need a special tool to see them.
Information that is spread to help or harm a person, group, or idea, often by showing only one side
The government used propaganda to convince people to support their new plan.
Involving or representing different cultures and traditions
Our school is multicultural, with students from many different countries.
Related to the natural world and the impact of human activity on it
We should take care of our environmental resources, like water and trees.
Related to living things and how they work
In science class, we learned about the biological processes that help plants grow.
Something that can be replaced or used again, like energy from the sun or wind
Solar energy is a renewable source because we can use it over and over.
The process where plants lose water through tiny openings in their leaves
When the sun shines, the leaves of the plants release water into the air through transpiration.
The act of coming back to life after being dead
In the story, the hero's resurrection surprised everyone because he came back to help his friends.
Allowing some light to pass through, but not completely clear
The translucent curtains let in soft light but kept the room private.
A community of living things and their environment, working together
The forest is an ecosystem where plants, animals, and insects all help each other.
A very serious disease that spreads quickly and can make many people sick
In history, the plague caused many people to become ill in a short time.
Energy that comes from a source and travels through space, in the form of heat or light
The sun gives off radiation that warms the Earth.
A type of animal that has a soft body and usually a shell, like snails and clams
We found a colourful mollusk on the beach that had a shiny shell.

💡  Vocabulary Tip
When you come across a new word while reading, try to work out its meaning from the words around it.
This is called using context clues. It is one of the best ways to grow your vocabulary!
Keep a vocabulary notebook. For each new word, write the word, its meaning, and your own sentence.

⭐  Did You Know?
East Africa has produced more Olympic medal winners in middle-distance and long-distance running than any other region on Earth.
Scientists believe that the high altitude and geographic features of the East African highlands help train the body to use oxygen more efficiently.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you found any question difficult, try asking your AI Tutor for a hint.
Say: Can you help me find a First Conditional sentence in the reading passage?

💡  Top Tip
Remember: the if-clause always uses the present simple, never 'will'.
Think of it as a rule: If = present simple. Result = will + base verb.

⭐  Did You Know?
The First Conditional is called 'first' because it deals with real, likely possibilities. There are other conditionals too.
The Second Conditional talks about imaginary or unlikely situations: If I had wings, I would fly. You will learn this later!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want more practice? Ask your AI Tutor to give you extra First Conditional exercises.
Try saying: Can you give me five more First Conditional sentences to complete?

💡  Top Tip
Before you start writing, make a list of facts about your chosen sportsperson.
Organise your facts in chronological order (the order they happened in time).
Use the third person (he, she, they) throughout your biography.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Need help starting your biography? Try asking:
Can you help me plan a biography about a famous athlete?
Want feedback on your commentary? Say:
Can you check my sports commentary and tell me how to make it more exciting?

🎙️  Speaking Tip
When you answer a question, try to give a reason or an example.
This makes your response more interesting and shows that you are thinking carefully.
For example, instead of just saying 'I train every day', say 'I train every day because if I miss even one session, my fitness will deteriorate.'

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to practise your interview skills? Try saying:
Can you be a journalist and interview me about my favourite sport?
Want feedback on your presentation? Say:
Can you listen to my health advice presentation and give me tips?

⭐  Did You Know?
Research shows that children who play team sports develop stronger communication skills, higher self-esteem, and better problem-solving abilities.
In many East African communities, sports are also an important way to promote peace and understanding between different groups.

🕑  About 40-50 minutes a day
Morning: review five vocabulary words and say them in sentences. Read a paragraph of the story aloud.
Afternoon: complete one grammar activity OR one writing task from this lesson.
Evening: practise speaking with your AI tutor. Try a role-play or present your commentary.
Twice a week: bring your recordings and writing to your teacher session for feedback.

💬  Well Done!
You have completed Unit 2: Sports and Health. Be proud of what you have achieved!
Remember, learning a language is like training for a sport. It takes daily practice, patience, and determination.
If there is anything you want to revisit, go back and read through the sections again. Your AI Tutor is always here to help!

paragraphs: When the Leaves Stop Speaking
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 2 Story
When the Leaves Stop Speaking

In the year 2083, in the green hills of what had once been northern Uganda, stood a protected grove named Nguvu Wa Asili—"Power of Nature." Around it was a self-sustaining multicultural research village where scientists, farmers, historians, and spiritual leaders lived together.

Amani, age 12, lived in the village. Her parents were both researchers in the local environmental agency, working on soil regeneration and water cycle modelling. Amani loved science—but she also loved listening to the stories of her grandfather, Baba Tano.
"Did you know," Baba would say, "that long ago, a prophet stood under that giant fig tree and predicted the return of light after the world's sky broke?"
Amani giggled. "You mean the supernova of 2081?"
"To me, it was prophecy. To you, it is physics," he smiled. "But do not laugh. Some truths are shared in more than one language."

That year, the village was visited by a small agency of international scientists studying transpiration in old forests. "The plants here release water at incredible rates," said Dr Lin, a biologist from Nairobi. "It may be the reason the surrounding farmland remains so fertile."

The villagers had always cared for the grove. Children were taught not to break branches. Farmers planted food only at the edges. Priests and herbalists performed monthly ceremonies at the fig tree in gratitude for rain. It was a place of peace.
But something was wrong. The outer ring of trees had started to dry out.
"If the forest continues to deteriorate, the whole ecosystem will collapse," warned Dr Lin.
"What's causing it?" asked Amani.
"We're not sure yet," she said. "But it may involve radiation leaking from a forgotten military device buried during the old wars. Or maybe a bacterial plague."
Amani had questions. She asked her parents. She asked the scientists. She even asked Baba Tano.
He stroked his beard. "If the ground grows weak, it may be because the sky grows angry."
"That's not science," Amani said gently.
"It is a way of seeing," he said. "And both ways matter."
The scientists began soil testing. Amani watched them assemble tools and test leaves for bacteria and radioactive particles.
One sample glowed faintly blue.
"It's not dangerous in small amounts," said Dr Lin, "but we should take it seriously."
In the village square, meetings were held. A few leaders believed outsiders were spreading propaganda—spreading fear to control the forest.
"This forest was originally sacred," said one elder. "Now it is filled with machines and measuring sticks!"
"It must be protected," argued Amani's mother. "If we lose it, we lose more than trees—we lose rainfall, clean air, even the ozone above us."
"If pollution increases," another scientist added, "the ozone will continue to deteriorate."
Amani began writing her own report. "The forest," she wrote, "is both memory and machine. We must protect it. If we do nothing, its silence will grow loud."

She collected data. She learned that transpiration had slowed in 25% of the trees. Some leaves had changed texture, becoming dry and rough. Bark had peeled in unusual shapes, like sculpture from a forgotten gallery.
"The change isn't just chemical," she told her mother. "It feels… personal."
Her mother nodded. "Sometimes, places are like people. When they suffer, they give signs."
"I want to make people care," Amani later told Dr Lin.
"Then make them see," she replied. "Use your imagination. Describe what will happen if this forest dies."
Amani drew posters. One showed a mollusk dying near a cracked streambed. Another showed the Earth wrapped in smog, a shrinking ecosystem floating like a translucent bubble in space.
She created a display titled: "If the leaves stop speaking—who will listen?"
That weekend, Baba Tano asked to walk the grove with her.
"I have a story," he said. "Of the day the forest died… and came back."
She smiled. "You just made that up."
"Perhaps," he said. "But if enough people believe a story, it becomes a reason to act."
They reached the tree. He placed his hand on the bark. "I have lived long enough to see forests disappear. But I have also seen them return. If people choose to change."
They walked quietly. Birds shifted in the canopy. The air smelled like damp moss and fallen flowers.
"Isn't it strange," Amani whispered, "how something so alive can also feel fragile?"
"Yes," he said. "Like peace. Like hope."
At the youth assembly the following week, Amani gave a presentation. She began:
"This forest is not just science. It's not just scripture. It's not just an ecosystem. It's a library, a temple, and a memory."
She showed graphs. She read predictions. She described the forest's architecture—its layers, patterns, and connections.
"You mustn't ignore the evidence. If we act now, we can save it."
One student asked, "What if the cause is unknown?"
"Then we keep learning," Amani said. "And we act where we can."
Her display included the phrase: "Renewable hope is planted through action."
A local carpenter had helped her build a model of the forest's root system. Every layer was painted with phrases in Swahili, Arabic, and English.
"Multicultural doesn't just mean people," she explained. "It means ideas. Different worldviews. Like science and spirit."

Students from different schools visited her booth. Some asked questions about the science. Others simply stood in silence, staring at the carved root system, running their fingers over the painted languages.
The mayor of the research village was moved.
"Children," he said, "have reminded us of what we forgot. This forest is not ours to own. It's ours to tend."
The council agreed to ban further clearing near the sacred zone. New funding went to clean-up crews and radiation containment. Village elders and scientists held new dialogues—respectful and equal.
The agency in charge of soil studies also announced a new training programme—for local youth.
Dr Lin approached Amani afterward. "You've done more than observe. You've inspired."
Amani beamed. "I only followed the data."
"No," Baba Tano said. "You followed your vision."
That evening, Amani stood at the base of the fig tree again. The wind rustled the top branches gently.
"Some call it a prophecy," she whispered. "Some call it biology. Either way—it begins with belief."
And the forest rustled softly in return.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Where and when is the story set? Describe the village in your own words.
What is the name of the protected grove, and what does it mean?
Who is Amani, and what do her parents do?
What problem do the scientists discover in the forest?
What are the two possible causes of the problem that Dr Lin suggests?
How do the village leaders react to the scientists' findings? Are they all in agreement?
What does Amani create to make people care about the forest?
What decision does the council make at the end of the story?
Think Deeper
Baba Tano says, 'Some truths are shared in more than one language.' What do you think he means by this?
Amani writes, 'The forest is both memory and machine.' What do you think this means? How can a forest be a memory?
The story shows a conflict between science and tradition. How do Amani and Baba Tano show that both can work together?
Amani says, 'Multicultural does not just mean people. It means ideas.' Do you agree? Give a reason for your answer.
The story ends with Amani saying, 'It begins with belief.' What kind of belief do you think she is talking about?
If you were Amani, what else would you do to protect the forest? Explain your idea.
Vocabulary Hunt
Find these vocabulary words in the story and write the sentence they appear in. Then explain what each word means in that context:
multicultural
environmental
prophet
supernova
transpiration
deteriorate
ecosystem
bacteria
radioactive
propaganda
originally
ozone
texture
sculpture
imagination
mollusk
translucent
scripture
architecture
renewable
Grammar in the Story
The story contains several examples of the First Conditional. Find at least four examples and write them out. For each one, identify the condition (the if-clause) and the result (the will-clause).
Example: 'If the forest continues to deteriorate, the whole ecosystem will collapse.'
Condition: the forest continues to deteriorate
Result: the ecosystem will collapse
Now You Try: Writing

Imagine you are Amani. Write a diary entry (100 to 150 words) about the day you gave your presentation at the youth assembly. Use the first person (I, my, we) and include at least three vocabulary words and two First Conditional sentences.
Write a letter to the mayor (100 to 150 words) from the perspective of a student who visited Amani's booth. Explain why the forest should be protected. Use at least two First Conditional sentences.
Create your own poster or display title that could be used to encourage people to protect the environment in your community. Write three sentences explaining your poster, using the First Conditional.
tables:
📖  About this story
Genre: Realistic science fiction with historical and ecological layers.
Theme: Science, belief, and environmental responsibility.
Setting: A future East African research village built near an ancient sacred forest.
Moral: Truth is not only found in labs or scriptures, but when curiosity, belief, and action meet.
Read it slowly. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, say it out loud!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?
Can you help me find all the First Conditional sentences in the story?
Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood it?

paragraphs: Unit 2: Vocabulary
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Unit 2 Vocabulary

This vocabulary guide contains all 28 key words from Unit 2: Sports and Health. These words appear in your unit story, your reading passage, and your grammar exercises. Study the word table carefully, then complete the practice activities to make sure you understand and can use each word with confidence.
Word List
Practice Activities
Activity 1: Match the Word to Its Meaning
Draw a line to match each word on the left to its correct meaning on the right.
Column A (Words):
deteriorate
efficient
ecosystem
transpiration
bacteria
translucent
multicultural
renewable
Column B (Meanings — scrambled):
a) Tiny living things that can only be seen with a microscope
b) A community of living things and their environment, working together
c) Something that can be replaced or used again
d) To become worse or to decline in quality
e) Allowing some light to pass through, but not completely clear
f) The process where plants lose water through tiny openings in their leaves
g) Involving or representing different cultures and traditions
h) Doing something in a way that saves time and energy
Activity 2: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence.
Word box: geographic, originally, imagination, agency, prophet, sculpture, architecture, scripture, ozone, radioactive
The __________ features of East Africa include mountains, plains, and the Great Rift Valley.
__________, the building was a mosque before it became a museum.
She used her __________ to create a beautiful painting of the forest.
The environmental __________ worked to protect the local rivers and wildlife.
In many religions, a __________ is someone who brings messages from God.
The __________ in the park was carved from a single block of stone.
The __________ of the old town is famous for its beautiful arches and courtyards.
Many people read __________ to find guidance and comfort.
The __________ layer protects us from the sun's harmful rays.
The scientists found __________ material buried underground and had to handle it very carefully.
Activity 3: Use the Word in a Sentence

Write your own sentence for each of the following words. Make sure your sentence clearly shows the meaning of the word. Try to connect your sentences to the themes of sports, health, or the environment.
deteriorate
biological
environmental
propaganda
sedition
plague
radiation
mollusk
Activity 4: Word Families
Some of the words in this unit belong to families of related words. Complete the table below by filling in the missing forms. The first one has been done for you.
Activity 5: Context Clues Challenge
Read each sentence and use the context clues (the words around the unknown word) to work out the meaning of the word in bold. Write what you think it means, then check the word list above.
The forest is an important ecosystem where plants, animals, and insects all depend on each other to survive.
The old bridge began to deteriorate after years of rain and wind, and soon it was no longer safe to cross.
The translucent jellyfish floated near the surface, and you could see its insides through its body.
The government used propaganda to make people believe the new law was good, even though many disagreed.
The scientists studied the transpiration of the plants to understand how much water was being released into the air.
The texture of the fabric was soft and smooth, like silk against the skin.
Activity 6: Odd One Out
In each group of four words, one word does not belong. Circle the odd one out and explain why it does not fit.
bacteria, mollusk, ozone, ecosystem (Which one is not a living thing or part of a living system?)
sculpture, architecture, texture, scripture (Which one is not related to buildings, art, or design?)
radioactive, renewable, translucent, efficient (Which one describes something dangerous?)
propaganda, sedition, agency, imagination (Which two have negative meanings? Which is the odd one out?)
Activity 7: Vocabulary in the Story
Go back to the story 'When the Leaves Stop Speaking' and find ten vocabulary words from this unit. For each word:
Write the sentence from the story that contains the word.
Explain what the word means in that context.
Write a new sentence of your own using the same word.
Answer Key
Activity 1 Answers
deteriorate → d) To become worse or to decline in quality
efficient → h) Doing something in a way that saves time and energy
ecosystem → b) A community of living things and their environment, working together
transpiration → f) The process where plants lose water through tiny openings in their leaves
bacteria → a) Tiny living things that can only be seen with a microscope
translucent → e) Allowing some light to pass through, but not completely clear
multicultural → g) Involving or representing different cultures and traditions
renewable → c) Something that can be replaced or used again
Activity 2 Answers
geographic
Originally
imagination
agency
prophet
sculpture
architecture
scripture
ozone
radioactive
Activity 3 Answers
Answers will vary. Check that each sentence uses the word correctly and clearly shows its meaning.
Activity 4 Answers
Activity 5 Answers
ecosystem — a community of living things that depend on each other (the clue is 'plants, animals, and insects all depend on each other')
deteriorate — to become worse (the clue is 'no longer safe to cross')
translucent — allowing some light to pass through (the clue is 'see its insides through its body')
propaganda — information spread to influence people (the clue is 'make people believe' and 'many disagreed')
transpiration — the process of plants releasing water (the clue is 'how much water was being released')
texture — the way something feels (the clue is 'soft and smooth, like silk')
Activity 6 Answers
ozone is the odd one out — it is a gas, not a living thing or a system of living things.
scripture is the odd one out — it is a religious text, not related to buildings, art, or design.
radioactive is the odd one out — it describes something dangerous, while the others describe positive or neutral qualities.
propaganda and sedition have negative meanings. imagination is the odd one out because it is a positive or neutral quality.
Activity 7 Answers
Answers will vary. Check that each sentence is correctly copied from the story, the meaning is accurate, and the student's own sentence uses the word correctly.
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!
