Our school is multicultural, with students from many different countries.
Related to the natural world and the impact of human activity on it
We should take care of our environmental resources, like water and trees.
Related to living things and how they work
In science class, we learned about the biological processes that help plants grow.
Something that can be replaced or used again, like energy from the sun or wind
Solar energy is a renewable source because we can use it over and over.
The process where plants lose water through tiny openings in their leaves
When the sun shines, the leaves of the plants release water into the air through transpiration.
The act of coming back to life after being dead
In the story, the hero's resurrection surprised everyone because he came back to help his friends.
Allowing some light to pass through, but not completely clear
The translucent curtains let in soft light but kept the room private.
A community of living things and their environment, working together
The forest is an ecosystem where plants, animals, and insects all help each other.
A very serious disease that spreads quickly and can make many people sick
In history, the plague caused many people to become ill in a short time.
Energy that comes from a source and travels through space, in the form of heat or light
The sun gives off radiation that warms the Earth.
A type of animal that has a soft body and usually a shell, like snails and clams
We found a colourful mollusk on the beach that had a shiny shell.

💡  Vocabulary Tip
The best way to learn new words is to use them! After studying the table, try to use at least three new words in conversation today.
You can also write each word on a card with its meaning on the back for quick revision.
When you read the unit story, look for each word and see how it is used in context.

💬  Well Done!
You have completed the Vocabulary practice for Unit 2.
Keep using these words in your writing and conversation.
The more you practise, the stronger your vocabulary becomes!

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 3: Life in the Wild
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar mini-lessons

This file has six grammar mini-lessons covering the two main grammar topics for Unit 3: the Present Simple Tense for stating facts and Relative Clauses (who, which, where). Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: The Present Simple Tense — Stating Facts
What it means and why it matters

The present simple tense is used to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always or generally true. When we write about science, nature, or the world around us, we use this tense to state facts. For example, 'The Earth orbits the Sun' is a fact that is always true, so we use the present simple.
This is important for Unit 3 because when you write about animals, ecosystems, and food chains, you need to state facts clearly and accurately.
How to form it
The present simple is formed using the base form of the verb. For he, she, and it, add -s or -es to the verb.
I / you / we / they + base verb:  "Lions hunt in groups."
He / she / it + verb + s/es:  "A cheetah runs very fast."
Special spelling rules for he/she/it:
Most verbs: add -s (eat → eats, live → lives, play → plays)
Verbs ending in -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: add -es (watch → watches, go → goes, catch → catches)
Verbs ending in consonant + y: change y to i, add -es (fly → flies, carry → carries)
Irregular: have → has, do → does, be → is
Negative form
To make a negative, use do not (don't) or does not (doesn't) + base verb:
"Herbivores do not eat meat."
"A frog does not live only in water."
"Camels don't need much water."
Question form
To ask a question, use Do or Does + subject + base verb:
"Do lions hunt at night?"
"Does the whale shark eat fish?"
"Do amphibians live on land and in water?"
Examples using wildlife facts
"The African elephant is the largest land animal."
"Cheetahs run faster than any other land animal."
"A food chain shows which creature eats which."
"Plants produce energy through photosynthesis."
"The Nile crocodile lives in rivers and lakes across East Africa."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Forgetting the -s for he/she/it.
"The eagle fly high." ✗  →  "The eagle flies high." ✓
Mistake 2: Using the present continuous instead of the present simple for facts.
"A camel is storing fat in its humps." ✗  →  "A camel stores fat in its humps." ✓
Mistake 3: Adding -s with do/does in negatives or questions.
"Does the lion hunts at night?" ✗  →  "Does the lion hunt at night?" ✓
Memory tip
Think: Fact = Present Simple. If it is always true — right now, yesterday, and tomorrow — use the present simple. Ask yourself: "Is this a fact about the world?" If yes, keep the verb simple!
Practice 1
Choose the correct form of the verb in brackets.
A vulture ______ (clean / cleans) the environment by eating dead animals.
Frogs ______ (live / lives) both in water and on land.
The gerenuk ______ (stand / stands) on its hind legs to eat from tall bushes.
Plants ______ (use / uses) sunlight to make food.
An ecosystem ______ (include / includes) all the living things in one area.
Herbivores ______ (do not eat / does not eat) meat.
______ (Do / Does) the whale shark hunt large fish?
The camel ______ (store / stores) fat in its humps.
Lesson 2: Present Simple — The Verb 'to be' for Facts
What it means and why it matters

The verb to be (am, is, are) is one of the most important verbs in English. In the present simple, we use it to describe what something is, to classify things, and to state facts about identity and characteristics.
How to form it
I → am:  "I am a student."
He / she / it → is:  "The cheetah is the fastest land animal."
You / we / they → are:  "Coral reefs are underwater ecosystems."
Negative: add not (or use contractions: isn't, aren't):
"A whale shark is not (isn't) a whale. It is a fish."
"Camels are not (aren't) predators."
Questions: put the verb before the subject:
"Is the gerenuk an antelope?"
"Are amphibians cold-blooded?"
Examples using wildlife facts
"The Somali wild ass is one of the most endangered animals in the world."
"Frogs are amphibians."
"The savannah is a grassland where many animals live."
"Coral reefs are home to thousands of species."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "is" with a plural subject.
"The elephants is large." ✗  →  "The elephants are large." ✓
Mistake: Using "are" with a singular subject.
"The lion are a predator." ✗  →  "The lion is a predator." ✓
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with is, are, isn't, or aren't.
The African elephant ______ the largest land animal.
Frogs ______ mammals. They ______ amphibians.
A food chain ______ a series showing which creature eats which.
Vultures ______ predators that hunt live animals. They eat dead ones.
The Serengeti ______ a place where the great migration takes place.
______ the whale shark a dangerous animal? No, it ______.
Lesson 3: Relative Clauses with 'who' (for people)
What it means and why it matters

A relative clause is a group of words that gives extra information about a noun in a sentence. We use who when we are talking about people. A relative clause with "who" tells us more about a person — what they do, what they believe, or what they are like.
Using relative clauses makes your writing more detailed and interesting. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can combine them into one complex sentence.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about a person. Then add a clause beginning with who to give more information:
"Scientists study ecosystems." + "Scientists are called ecologists."
→ "Scientists who study ecosystems are called ecologists."
More examples:
"The ranger who protects the park is called Amina."
"Farmers who clear forests destroy animal habitats."
"The abolitionist who fought slavery used songs and secret messages."
"People who oppress others are afraid of what they cannot control."
Defining vs. non-defining
A defining relative clause tells us exactly which person we mean. It does not use commas:
"The boy who won the race is my friend." (Which boy? The one who won.)
A non-defining relative clause adds extra information. It uses commas:
"Nia, who was only twelve, showed great courage." (We already know which Nia.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "which" for people.
"The teacher which helped us is kind." ✗  →  "The teacher who helped us is kind." ✓
Mistake: Forgetting commas in non-defining clauses.
"Nia who was twelve showed courage." ✗  →  "Nia, who was twelve, showed courage." ✓
Memory tip
Who = people. If you can replace "who" with a person's name, you are using it correctly.
Practice 3
Combine each pair of sentences into one, using who.
The ranger protects wild animals. She works in a national park.
Scientists discovered a new species of frog. They were studying the cloud forest.
The farmer refused to hand over his harvest. He died from starvation.
Nia showed great courage. She was only twelve years old.
An abolitionist fights against slavery. He or she believes all people should be free.
Lesson 4: Relative Clauses with 'which' and 'that' (for animals and things)
What it means and why it matters

We use which when we are talking about animals, things, and ideas — never for people. We can also use that for animals and things, especially in defining clauses. Using "which" and "that" lets you add important details to your sentences about the natural world.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about an animal or thing. Add a clause beginning with which or that:
"The coral reef provides shelter." + "The coral reef is underwater."
→ "The coral reef, which is underwater, provides shelter."
More examples:
"The camel, which can survive without water for days, is found across East Africa."
"A food chain is a series that shows which creature eats which."
"The lion, which lives in prides, is a famous predator."
"An ecosystem that loses its top predator can fall apart."
When to use 'that' vs. 'which'
That is used in defining clauses (no commas) — it tells us exactly which thing we mean:
"The animal that eats only plants is called a herbivore."
Which can be used in both defining and non-defining clauses. Non-defining clauses (with commas) add extra, non-essential information:
"The elephant, which is the largest land animal, lives in family groups."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "who" for animals or things.
"The tree who grows near the river is an acacia." ✗  →  "The tree which grows near the river is an acacia." ✓
Mistake: Forgetting to include the relative pronoun.
"The animal eats plants is a herbivore." ✗  →  "The animal that eats plants is a herbivore." ✓
Memory tip
Which = things and animals. If you are talking about a creature with four legs, wings, fins, or roots in the ground, use "which" or "that" — never "who".
Practice 4
Fill in each blank with which or that. Add commas where needed for non-defining clauses.
The cheetah is a predator ______ runs faster than any other land animal.
Coral reefs ______ are found off the East African coast support thousands of species.
The gerenuk ______ stands on its hind legs is found in Somalia and Kenya.
An ecosystem ______ loses its key species can collapse.
The Serengeti ______ is located in Tanzania sees a massive migration every year.
A food chain is a diagram ______ shows the flow of energy from one creature to another.
Lesson 5: Relative Clauses with 'where' (for places)
What it means and why it matters

We use where when we are talking about places. A relative clause with "where" tells us more about a location — what happens there, who lives there, or why it is important. This is especially useful when describing habitats and ecosystems.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about a place. Add a clause beginning with where:
"The savannah is a grassland." + "Many animals graze on the savannah."
→ "The savannah is a grassland where many animals graze."
More examples:
"Coral reefs are underwater ecosystems where thousands of species find food and shelter."
"The national park is a protected area where no hunting is allowed."
"The river is a place where frogs, fish, and herons live side by side."
"East Africa is a region where some of the world's most incredible wildlife can be found."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "which" instead of "where" for a place when it acts as a location.
"The park which we play" ✗  →  "The park where we play" ✓
Note: you can use "which" for a place when it is the subject or object of the clause:
"The park, which is very large, has many animals." ✓ ("which" = the park = subject)
But when you mean "in that place", use where:
"The park where many animals live is protected." ✓ ("where" = in the park)
Memory tip
Where = places. If you can replace "where" with "in that place" or "at that place", you are using it correctly.
Practice 5
Complete each sentence with where and your own ending.
The Serengeti is a grassland where ______.
The coral reef is an underwater habitat where ______.
The forest is a place where ______.
The river is a habitat where ______.
East Africa is a region where ______.
Lesson 6: Combining Present Simple with Relative Clauses
What it means and why it matters

The real power of your grammar comes when you combine the present simple tense with relative clauses. This lets you write rich, detailed sentences about the natural world — exactly what you need for your animal research report and your Cambridge Primary English assessments.
How to combine them
Use the present simple for the main fact, and add a relative clause for extra detail:
"The cheetah, which is the fastest land animal, hunts gazelles on the open plains."
"Ecologists are scientists who study how living things interact with their environment."
"The savannah is a grassland where zebras, wildebeest, and gazelles graze."
"A predator is an animal that hunts other animals for food."
Building complex sentences step by step
Start with a simple fact:
"The eagle hunts small mammals."
Add a relative clause for more detail:
"The eagle, which has excellent eyesight, hunts small mammals."
Add a location clause:
"The eagle, which has excellent eyesight, hunts small mammals in the savannah where tall grasses provide cover."
Practice 6
Write five complex sentences about East African wildlife. Each sentence must:
Use the present simple tense for the main verb.
Include at least one relative clause (with who, which, that, or where).
Be factually accurate (ask your AI tutor if you are unsure about a fact).
Here are some animals and places to write about:
The African elephant
The Nile crocodile
The Somali ostrich
The coral reefs of East Africa
The Masai Mara grasslands
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Present simple — verb forms)
1. cleans  2. live  3. stands  4. use  5. includes  6. do not eat  7. Does  8. stores
Practice 2 (Present simple — to be)
1. is  2. aren't / are  3. is  4. aren't  5. is  6. Is / isn't
Practice 3 (Relative clauses with who)
Possible answers:
The ranger who protects wild animals works in a national park.
Scientists who were studying the cloud forest discovered a new species of frog.
The farmer who refused to hand over his harvest died from starvation.
Nia, who was only twelve years old, showed great courage.
An abolitionist is someone who fights against slavery and believes all people should be free.
Practice 4 (Relative clauses with which/that)
1. that/which  2. which (+ commas: Coral reefs, which are found...)  3. which (+ commas: The gerenuk, which stands...)  4. that  5. which (+ commas: The Serengeti, which is located...)  6. that/which
Practice 5 (Relative clauses with where)
Sample answers (your own endings may differ):
The Serengeti is a grassland where millions of wildebeest migrate every year.
The coral reef is an underwater habitat where colourful fish and sea turtles live.
The forest is a place where birds, monkeys, and insects find food and shelter.
The river is a habitat where crocodiles, frogs, and herons can be found.
East Africa is a region where some of the world's most amazing wildlife lives.
tables:
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise relative clauses?
What is the difference between a defining and non-defining relative clause?
Can you help me combine short sentences using who, which, and where?

paragraphs: Unit 3: Life in the Wild
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 3: Life in the Wild! In this unit, you will explore the natural world through the lens of East African ecosystems. You will discover how animals, plants, and their environments are connected in amazing ways. Every living thing depends on something else to survive, and understanding these connections helps us take better care of the world around us.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary.
Use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature.
Build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where.
Write an animal research report with clear paragraphs and accurate information.
Discuss how humans affect wildlife and what can be done to protect endangered species.
Identify and use key vocabulary related to the natural world: habitat, predator, prey, species, ecosystem, and more.
Read, understand, and respond to a text about wildlife and survival.
Speak confidently about the natural world and present your findings to others.
Key Vocabulary
Before you begin reading, make sure you understand these important words. You will meet them again and again throughout the unit. Study the full definitions in your Vocabulary companion file.
Nature and Ecosystems
ecosystem – a community of living things and their environment, all connected together
habitat – the natural home of an animal or plant
food chain – a series showing which creature eats which
predator – an animal that hunts other animals for food
prey – an animal that is hunted by another animal
species – a group of living things that are the same kind
amphibian – an animal that lives both in water and on land
carbon – a basic element found in all living things
Describing Wildlife
observable – something that can be seen or noticed
crimson – a deep red colour
vicinity – the area around a particular place
endure – to keep going through something difficult without giving up
threat – something that can cause harm or danger
treacherous – dangerous and not safe, often not what it seems
starvation – the condition of not having enough food
Reading: The Web of Life in East Africa

Read the following passage carefully. As you read, pay attention to the present simple tense used to state facts, and notice the relative clauses that add extra information about animals and places. The words in bold are from your vocabulary list.

East Africa is a region which stretches from the dry plains of Somalia to the green highlands of Ethiopia and the vast savannahs of Kenya and Tanzania. It is home to one of the richest collections of wildlife on Earth. Every animal, plant, and insect plays a part in the great web of life. Scientists call this web an ecosystem — a community of living things that depend on each other and on the land, water, and air around them.

At the heart of every ecosystem lies the food chain. A food chain shows who eats whom. It begins with plants, which use sunlight and carbon dioxide to make their own food through a process called photosynthesis. These plants are called producers because they produce energy for the entire chain. In the semi-arid grasslands of the Horn of Africa, tough grasses and thorny acacia trees are the main producers. They endure months of dry heat and burst into life when the rains finally come.

The next link in the chain is the herbivore — an animal that eats only plants. On the East African savannah, herds of zebra, wildebeest, and gazelle graze on the grasslands. In the drier regions near Somali towns, the camel — which people sometimes call the “ship of the desert” — feeds on dry shrubs and thorny branches that other animals cannot reach. The gerenuk, a slender antelope which is found in Somalia and Kenya, stands on its hind legs to browse the highest leaves. Each herbivore has its own habitat and its own way of surviving.

Then come the predators — the meat-eaters who hunt other animals for food. The lion, which lives in family groups called prides, is one of East Africa’s most famous predators. Cheetahs, who are the fastest land animals on Earth, chase gazelle across the open plains. The Somali wild dog hunts in packs, working together to catch prey that is larger than any single dog. High above, eagles and vultures circle the sky, watching for small mammals and reptiles below. These birds of prey play an important role because they clean the environment by eating dead animals.

Water creates its own chain. Along the rivers and seasonal pools, frogs and toads are common. These amphibians — animals that live both in water and on land — eat insects and are in turn eaten by snakes and herons. Off the coast of East Africa, the coral reefs are underwater ecosystems where thousands of species of fish, crabs, and sea turtles find food and shelter. Whale sharks, which are the largest fish in the ocean, visit these warm waters to feed on tiny plankton.

Every part of this web matters. Remove one species, and the whole chain can fall apart. When farmers clear land for crops, they destroy the habitat of wild animals. When hunters kill too many predators, the number of herbivores grows too large, and they eat all the grass until the land turns to dust. When plastic waste reaches the ocean, it becomes a threat to sea turtles who mistake it for jellyfish. The balance of nature is treacherous — it can look strong, but it is easily broken.

Understanding food chains and ecosystems is not just science — it is a duty. In Islam, caring for the Earth is called khilafah, stewardship. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, taught that every living creature deserves kindness and that wasting resources is wrong. When we protect the wild lands of East Africa, we protect a gift that belongs to everyone — today and for generations to come.
Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the passage, test your understanding by answering these questions. Write your answers in full sentences.
Comprehension Questions
What is an ecosystem? Use your own words to explain.
What is the first link in every food chain? Why are these called “producers”?
Name two herbivores mentioned in the passage. What do they eat?
How does the gerenuk get its food differently from other antelopes?
Why are vultures important to the ecosystem, according to the passage?
What are amphibians? Give one example from the text.
Name two threats to East African ecosystems mentioned in the passage.
What does the passage say about the Islamic concept of khilafah?
Think Deeper
These questions ask you to think beyond the text. There is no single right answer — explain your reasoning clearly.
The passage says, “Remove one species, and the whole chain can fall apart.” Can you think of an example from your own experience or knowledge? What might happen if bees disappeared?
Why do you think the passage connects science with the Islamic teaching of khilafah? How can understanding ecosystems help us be better stewards of the Earth?
The camel is called the “ship of the desert.” Why is this a good name? Can you think of another animal nickname that describes what the animal does?
If you could protect one animal or habitat in East Africa, which would you choose and why?
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the reading passage. Read the sentence they appear in, then write your own sentence using each word.
ecosystem
food chain
predator
prey
habitat
species
amphibian
carbon
endure
threat
treacherous
Grammar Focus: Present Simple for Facts and Relative Clauses
This unit focuses on two important grammar topics. You will study them in detail in your Grammar companion file, but here is a summary to help you as you work through the lesson.
Grammar Topic 1: Present Simple Tense for Facts

We use the present simple tense when we want to state a fact — something that is always true or generally true. This is different from talking about what is happening right now (present continuous) or what happened in the past (past simple).
Look at these examples from the reading passage:
"East Africa is home to one of the richest collections of wildlife on Earth."
"Plants use sunlight and carbon dioxide to make their own food."
"The lion lives in family groups called prides."
"Whale sharks visit these warm waters to feed on tiny plankton."
Notice the pattern: for he, she, and it, we add -s or -es to the verb. For I, you, we, and they, we use the base form.
The cheetah runs fast. (he/she/it = add -s)
Frogs eat insects. (they = base form)
A food chain shows who eats whom. (it = add -s)
Herbivores graze on the grasslands. (they = base form)
Grammar Topic 2: Relative Clauses (who, which, where)

A relative clause is a group of words that gives extra information about a noun. It starts with a relative pronoun: who, which, or where. Relative clauses help us combine two short sentences into one longer, more interesting sentence.
Use who for people:
"Cheetahs, who are the fastest land animals, chase gazelle."
"The farmer who lives near the river grows maize."
Use which for animals and things:
"The lion, which lives in prides, is a famous predator."
"Acacia trees, which have sharp thorns, survive the dry season."
Use where for places:
"The savannah is a place where many animals graze."
"Coral reefs are underwater ecosystems where thousands of species live."
You can also use that instead of who or which in many sentences:
"An animal that eats only plants is called a herbivore."
"The eagle that circles above is looking for prey."
Grammar Practice
Complete these exercises to practise the grammar you have learned. Check your answers at the bottom of this section.
Exercise 1: Present Simple — Choose the Correct Form
Choose the correct verb form to complete each fact.
A cheetah ______ (run / runs) faster than any other land animal.
Frogs ______ (live / lives) both in water and on land.
The coral reef ______ (provide / provides) shelter for many sea creatures.
Camels ______ (store / stores) fat in their humps for energy.
A vulture ______ (clean / cleans) the environment by eating dead animals.
Plants ______ (produce / produces) energy through photosynthesis.
The gerenuk ______ (stand / stands) on its hind legs to eat leaves.
Herbivores ______ (eat / eats) only plants.
Exercise 2: Relative Clauses — Fill in who, which, or where
Complete each sentence with who, which, or where.
A predator is an animal ______ hunts other animals for food.
The savannah is a grassland ______ zebras and wildebeest roam.
The camel, ______ can survive without water for days, is found across East Africa.
Scientists ______ study ecosystems are called ecologists.
An amphibian is a creature ______ lives both in water and on land.
The ocean is a habitat ______ whale sharks feed on plankton.
The eagle, ______ has excellent eyesight, spots prey from high above.
Farmers ______ clear forests destroy animal habitats.
Exercise 3: Combine the Sentences
Use a relative clause to combine each pair of sentences into one.
The lion is a predator. It lives in family groups. → The lion is a predator ______.
The coral reef is beautiful. It is home to thousands of species. → The coral reef, ______, is beautiful.
The farmer grows maize. He lives near the river. → The farmer ______ grows maize.
The forest is important. Many animals live there. → The forest is a place ______.
Vultures eat dead animals. They keep the environment clean. → Vultures, ______, eat dead animals.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Facts
Write five facts about East African wildlife using the present simple tense. Include at least two relative clauses. Here is an example to get you started:
"The Somali wild ass, which lives in the dry rocky lands of the Horn of Africa, is one of the most endangered animals in the world."
Answers
Exercise 1: 1. runs  2. live  3. provides  4. store  5. cleans  6. produce  7. stands  8. eat
Exercise 2: 1. which/that  2. where  3. which  4. who  5. which/that  6. where  7. which  8. who
Exercise 3: 1. which/that lives in family groups  2. which is home to thousands of species  3. who lives near the river  4. where many animals live  5. who/which keep the environment clean
Writing Workshop: Animal Research Report

In this section, you will plan and write a short research report about an East African animal. This is a key skill for your Cambridge Primary English course. A good report presents facts clearly and uses the present simple tense throughout.
Step 1: Choose Your Animal
Pick one animal from the list below, or choose your own East African animal:
The Somali wild ass
The gerenuk
The whale shark
The African elephant
The Nile crocodile
The East African crowned crane
The cheetah
The Somali ostrich
Step 2: Research Your Animal
Find out the following information about your animal. You can use books, your AI tutor, or reliable websites. Write notes — do not copy full sentences.
What is the animal called? What species group does it belong to?
What does it look like? Describe its size, colour, and any special features.
Where does it live? Describe its habitat.
What does it eat? Is it a predator, a herbivore, or an omnivore?
How does it survive in its environment? What special abilities does it have?
Is it endangered? What threats does it face?
One surprising or interesting fact.
Step 3: Plan Your Report
Use this planning frame to organise your ideas before writing:
Introduction: Name the animal and say why it is interesting. (1–2 sentences)
Appearance: Describe what the animal looks like. (2–3 sentences)
Habitat: Describe where the animal lives. Use where. (2–3 sentences)
Diet and Food Chain: Explain what the animal eats and where it fits in the food chain. (2–3 sentences)
Survival and Threats: Explain how the animal survives and what dangers it faces. (2–3 sentences)
Interesting Fact: Share one amazing fact. (1–2 sentences)
Conclusion: Say why this animal matters and what we can do to protect it. (1–2 sentences)
Step 4: Write Your Report
Now write your report. Use your plan and your notes. Here is a model opening paragraph to help you:

"The African elephant is the largest land animal on Earth. It is a herbivore which lives in the savannahs and forests of East Africa. Elephants are gentle giants who live in family groups led by the oldest female, which scientists call the matriarch. They play a vital role in the ecosystem because they spread seeds and create paths through the forest that other animals use."
Notice how the model paragraph:
Uses the present simple tense for facts (is, lives, are, play, spread, create, use).
Includes relative clauses with which, who, and that.
Starts with an interesting opening fact to catch the reader's attention.
Connects the animal to the wider ecosystem.
Step 5: Check and Improve Your Report
Before you finish, go through this checklist:
Have I used the present simple tense for all my facts? (Check: no “is eating” or “was living”.)
Have I included at least three relative clauses with who, which, or where?
Have I organised my report into clear paragraphs with a logical order?
Have I used vocabulary from this unit (ecosystem, habitat, predator, prey, species, threat)?
Have I checked my spelling, capital letters, and full stops?
Does my report have a strong opening sentence and a thoughtful conclusion?
Listening and Speaking
Listening 1: A Wildlife Documentary Script
Ask your AI tutor to read this documentary narration aloud. Listen carefully, then answer the questions below. Read it yourself afterwards.

“Welcome to the plains of the Serengeti, where one of nature’s greatest events takes place every year. Over one million wildebeest begin their long journey north, searching for fresh grass and water. They travel in enormous herds that stretch as far as the eye can see. Along the way, they face many dangers. Crocodiles wait in the rivers. Lions hide in the tall grass. But the wildebeest keep moving. They endure hunger, thirst, and exhaustion because their survival depends on reaching the green pastures of the Masai Mara. This migration is one of the most observable wonders of the natural world — a reminder that life in the wild is both beautiful and treacherous.”
After listening, answer:
Where does the wildebeest migration take place?
How many wildebeest take part in the migration?
Name two predators the wildebeest face on their journey.
Why do the wildebeest keep moving despite the dangers?
The narrator says life in the wild is “both beautiful and treacherous.” What does this mean?
Listening 2: An Interview with a Wildlife Ranger
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud. Listen, then discuss the questions.
“Interviewer: What does a wildlife ranger do?”

“Ranger Amina: A wildlife ranger is someone who protects animals and their habitats. I work in a national park where elephants, giraffes, and lions live. My job is to make sure no one hunts them illegally. I also teach visitors about the animals which live here and why they are important.”
“Interviewer: What is the biggest threat to the animals?”

“Ranger Amina: The biggest threat is habitat loss. When people cut down trees or build on wild land, the animals have nowhere to go. Poaching is also a serious problem. But I believe that education is the answer. When people understand ecosystems, they want to protect them.”
After listening, discuss:
What is Ranger Amina’s job?
What is the biggest threat to the animals she protects?
Ranger Amina says “education is the answer.” Do you agree? Why or why not?
Would you like to be a wildlife ranger? Explain your reasons.
Speaking 1: Present Your Animal Report
When your animal research report is finished, prepare a short spoken presentation. Follow these steps:
Stand up straight and speak clearly. Imagine you are a nature documentary presenter!
Start with a strong opening: “Today I will tell you about the ______, which is one of the most ______ animals in East Africa.”
Present your key facts: habitat, diet, food chain position, and one interesting fact.
End with a call to action: “We should protect this animal because ______.”
Record yourself and listen back. Did you use the present simple for facts? Did you speak at a good pace?
Speaking 2: Food Chain Challenge

Work with your AI tutor or a partner. One person names a living thing (for example, “grass”). The other person must name an animal that eats it, and then the next person names what eats that animal. Keep the chain going as long as you can! Use full sentences:
“Grass is a producer which grows on the savannah.”
“The zebra is a herbivore that eats grass.”
“The lion is a predator which hunts zebras.”
Speaking Game: Habitat Guessing Game
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of an animal but do not say its name.
Describe its habitat: “This animal lives in a place where ______.”
Describe its diet: “It is a ______ that eats ______.”
Give one more clue: “It is an animal which ______.”
The other person guesses. Then swap turns.
Activities and Exercises
Activity 1: Food Chain Diagram

Draw a food chain for the East African savannah. Include at least four links. Label each one as a producer, herbivore, or predator. Then write one sentence about each link using the present simple tense and a relative clause. For example:
“The acacia tree is a producer which makes food using sunlight.”
“The gazelle is a herbivore that eats acacia leaves.”
“The cheetah is a predator which hunts gazelles.”
Activity 2: True or False
Write True or False. If the statement is false, correct it.
A predator is an animal that eats only plants.
An amphibian lives both in water and on land.
The present simple tense is used for facts that are always true.
We use “who” for things and “which” for people.
The coral reef is an underwater ecosystem.
Camels are predators that hunt other animals.
Vultures help clean the environment.
A relative clause gives extra information about a verb.

Answers: 1. False (a predator hunts other animals)  2. True  3. True  4. False (who for people, which for things)  5. True  6. False (camels are herbivores)  7. True  8. False (a relative clause gives extra information about a noun)
Activity 3: Vocabulary Crossword Clues
Write the correct vocabulary word for each clue.
A community of living things and their environment: e________
The natural home of an animal or plant: h________
An animal that hunts other animals: p________
An animal that is hunted: p________
A group of living things of the same kind: s________
A deep red colour: c________
To keep going through difficulty: e________
The area around a particular place: v________
Answers: 1. ecosystem  2. habitat  3. predator  4. prey  5. species  6. crimson  7. endure  8. vicinity
Activity 4: Critical Thinking — Predicting Animal Survival
Read each situation and predict what might happen. Explain your reasoning using because and the present simple tense.
All the lions in an area disappear. What happens to the zebra population? What happens to the grass?
A factory pollutes the river near a village. What happens to the frogs and fish? What happens to the animals that eat them?
Climate change causes the rains to come later each year. How does this affect the plants? How does it affect the herbivores?
A community decides to create a protected area where no hunting is allowed. What changes might you see after five years?
Activity 5: Compare and Contrast
Choose two East African animals (for example, the cheetah and the Nile crocodile). Write a short paragraph comparing them. Use these sentence starters:
"Both the ______ and the ______ are predators which ______."
"The ______, which lives in ______, hunts by ______."
"Both animals are important to the ecosystem because ______."
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand ecosystems. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my animal presentation and tell me how to improve?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my research report and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more exercises on relative clauses?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
Explore further: “Tell me more about endangered animals in East Africa.”
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning: Self-Assessment
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet” for each statement. For every “Not yet”, go back to that section and ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can explain what an ecosystem and a food chain are.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can name several predators, herbivores, and prey animals from East Africa.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use the present simple tense to state facts about animals.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can build sentences with relative clauses using who, which, and where.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a structured animal research report with clear paragraphs.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can speak about wildlife confidently and present my findings.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can discuss the threats facing East African wildlife and suggest solutions.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I understand the key vocabulary: ecosystem, habitat, predator, prey, species, amphibian, carbon, endure, threat, treacherous.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Getting Ready for Unit 4

Well done — you have finished Unit 3! Next you will study Unit 4: Inventions and Creativity, where you will learn about amazing inventions, robotics, and how to talk about the future. The skills you built in this unit — stating facts, building complex sentences, and writing reports — will help you with everything ahead.
Make sure you know all the Unit 3 words on your Word Wall.
Finish your animal research report and share it with your teacher.
Keep using English every day, even for a few minutes.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 4? Can you get me ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, and celebrate every new word and every new sentence you master! 🌟
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through each section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar, and the Story “The Red Cloth”.
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you need help, and bring your work to your teacher sessions.
Sections marked with a star are especially important for your Cambridge Primary English skills.

🌍  Did You Know?
East Africa is home to some of the most incredible wildlife on the planet. The Serengeti in Tanzania sees over 1.5 million wildebeest migrate every year. The coral reefs along the Somali coast are among the longest in Africa. Even the semi-arid lands of the Horn of Africa support a rich web of life, from the gerenuk that stands on its hind legs to eat from tall bushes, to the Somali wild ass that survives in extreme heat.

💡  Top Tip
Keep a vocabulary notebook. For each new word, write the word, its meaning, and your own example sentence. Review your notebook every evening before bed. Research shows that writing words by hand helps your brain remember them better.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain what a food chain is using an example from my country?
What is the difference between a predator and prey?
Can you help me draw a simple food chain for the East African savannah?

🌍  Did You Know?
The Somali wild ass is one of the most endangered animals in the world. Fewer than 600 remain in the wild. They live in the dry, rocky areas of the Horn of Africa and can survive for days without water. Conservation groups are working to protect them before it is too late.

💡  Top Tip
When you write facts about animals, always use the present simple. Ask yourself: Is this always true? If yes, use present simple. If it happened once in the past, use past simple instead.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you give me five sentences about East African animals that use relative clauses?
Can you check if I am using who, which, and where correctly?
What is the difference between a defining and non-defining relative clause?

💡  Top Tip
When you are not sure whether to use who, which, or where, ask yourself: Am I talking about a person (who), a thing or animal (which), or a place (where)? This simple question will guide you every time.

✍️  Writing rules to remember
Start every sentence with a capital letter and end it with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark.
Use the present simple tense for facts about your animal.
Include at least three relative clauses with who, which, or where.
Organise your report into clear paragraphs: introduction, habitat, diet, interesting facts, and conclusion.
Read your work aloud, or ask your AI tutor to check it, before you hand it in.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor

Can you help me add more relative clauses to my writing?
Can you suggest a better opening sentence for my report?
Can you quiz me on the vocabulary I used in my report?

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you listen to my animal presentation and give me feedback?
Can you play the Food Chain Challenge with me?
Can you help me improve my pronunciation of the vocabulary words?
Can you ask me questions about the wildlife documentary to test my listening?

🌍  Did You Know?
In 2023, researchers discovered a new species of frog in the mountains of Ethiopia. It lives in a tiny area of cloud forest and is already endangered because its habitat is shrinking. Every year, scientists discover new species, but many disappear before they are even found.

🕑  About 30–45 minutes a day
Morning: review your vocabulary words and say one fact about an animal out loud.
Afternoon: do one grammar exercise OR one activity from this file.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 3: Life in the Wild
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Red Cloth”
The Red Cloth
The sun rose slowly over the hills of Kalembo, a village surrounded by dust and silence. Three months ago, everything had changed.

Foreign soldiers had arrived. They wore sharp grey coats and marched in tight rows. Without warning, they had proclaimed that Kalembo now belonged to a land “across the sea.” No one had seen this ruler. People called him a dictator, someone who gave orders no one could question.

The soldiers took control of the grain stores. They set up new laws in an administration centre — a stone building filled with papers and stamps. “This is what they call bureaucracy,” said Bibi Asha, the village elder. “A system where rules matter more than people.”

Twelve-year-old Nia had lived in Kalembo her whole life. She remembered laughter, music, and baskets of mangoes under the fig tree. Now, she heard orders barked in a language she didn’t understand. People whispered more than they spoke. Soldiers watched every corner.
One day, a funeral was held for Mzee Baraka, an old farmer who had refused to hand over his harvest. He had died quietly in his sleep, some said from starvation.
A crowd gathered beside the river to pray and bury him. Women wore shawls. Men stood in silence. Children held hands.
Suddenly, a group of soldiers arrived.
“No gatherings allowed!” one of them shouted. “This is a prohibited event.”
“But it’s a funeral,” someone said.
“All events must be approved,” said the soldier. “Even grief must follow rules.”
The villagers dispersed slowly, heads bowed. But something stayed — an emotion deeper than sorrow. Frustration.
Nia stood beside her mother and whispered, “They fear even our tears.”
That night, Nia asked her grandfather, “Why do they treat us this way?”
“Because people who oppress others are afraid,” he said. “They fear what they cannot control — memory, belief, unity.”
She didn’t fully understand, but she remembered his words.
The next day, the soldiers painted warnings on walls:
“Any acts of resistance will be punished. Anyone who refuses to obey will be treated as treacherous.”
The words were meant to scare. But they lit a fire in Nia’s heart.
“These words,” she told her friend Juma, “are like weapons.”
“Then we need words too,” he said.
That night, they gathered red cloth scraps — bright, crimson, the colour Mzee Baraka had worn when he walked through town. Nia’s mother had once called it “a colour no fear can hide.”
Nia, Juma, and others tied the cloths on trees, water pots, rooftops. On fences, they painted messages in charcoal:
“This land has roots.”
“Grain is not rebellion.”
“We are people, not property.”
They didn’t sign the messages. But the whole vicinity began to feel different. A wind of quiet courage blew through the village.
When the soldiers saw the red cloths, they grew angry.
“Who’s behind this?” they shouted. “These symbols are treacherous.”
But no one spoke. It was a collective silence. The villagers had found power in not revealing the truth.
The next day, all the cloths were torn down. But by sunset, new ones had appeared.
At school, the teacher gave a new writing assignment:
“Choose someone who showed courage. Write how they faced hardship, and how they responded.”
Nia wrote about her grandmother, who had once hidden refugees fleeing the war. “She was quiet,” Nia wrote, “but she acted. That is strength.”
Juma wrote about a man named Kwame, an abolitionist who had fought slavery through song and secret messages.
Others wrote about leaders from the past. One wrote:
“A dictator builds walls. But people build bridges.”
They used relative clauses naturally:
“My grandmother, who raised me, believed in truth.”
“Kwame, whose words became songs, never gave up.”
“A soldier is someone who follows orders, which can destroy or protect.”
One morning, the soldiers arrived at school.
“These books,” they said, “contain propaganda.”
They burned several. “You must only learn what we allow.”
Nia looked down, but something inside her refused to shrink.
That night, she and others replaced the school gate sign with a painted message:
“Knowledge is not treason. Burning pages doesn’t erase truth.”
That week, there were whispers of sickness. A health worker arrived from the capital to test the water. “This is a case of typhoid,” he said. “Caused by unsafe drinking water.”
He handed out carbon filters and gave instructions. “Boil water. Avoid contaminated streams. Illness is not always observable. It hides.”
The lesson stuck with Nia.
Later she told Juma, “Injustice is like typhoid. You don’t always see it, but it poisons everything.”
One day, the red cloths disappeared.
The villagers looked confused. No one had removed them. No one had put up new ones.
That afternoon, a new rule was posted:
“You may bury your dead in groups of five or fewer. All funerals must end before sunset.”
“It’s not freedom,” someone muttered. “But it’s a crack in the wall.”
Nia’s grandfather said, “It shows they feel pressure. They see we endure.”
The teacher asked the class to reflect:
“What happens when people face power they cannot fight?”
A student said, “Some break.”
Another said, “Some bend.”
Nia raised her hand. “Some plant roots. Quiet ones. Ones that reach deep.”
As the months passed, small changes grew.
The soldiers patrolled less. They shouted less. The new bureaucrats were local hires. The cloths returned, not just as symbols, but in clothes, art, and flags.
One day, years later, a traveller came to Kalembo. He had heard of the red cloths. He asked the villagers:
“Who started the movement?”
Nia, now older, pointed to a grave wrapped in crimson. “The man we buried.”
She pointed to a tree with painted bark. “The questions we asked.”
She smiled. “The girl I was.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Recall Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences.
What had happened to the village of Kalembo three months before the story begins?
Who was Mzee Baraka, and what happened to him?
Why were the villagers not allowed to hold the funeral freely?
What did Nia and Juma do with the red cloth scraps?
What messages did the children paint on fences?
What did the soldiers do when they found the red cloths?
What happened at the school when the soldiers arrived?
What did the health worker discover about the water?
How did the situation in Kalembo change over time?
Who did Nia say started the movement, at the end of the story?
Think and Discuss
These questions ask for your own thoughts. There is no single right answer. Explain your reasoning clearly.
Nia’s grandfather says, “People who oppress others are afraid.” What do you think he means? Do you agree?
The villagers used red cloth and painted messages instead of fighting with weapons. Why did they choose this kind of resistance? Was it effective?
Nia says, “Injustice is like typhoid. You don’t always see it, but it poisons everything.” What does she mean by this comparison?
The teacher asked, “What happens when people face power they cannot fight?” Nia answered, “Some plant roots.” What does she mean by “planting roots”?
At the end, Nia points to three things that started the movement: the man they buried, the questions they asked, and the girl she was. Why does she name all three?
The story’s moral is: “When power tries to silence people, truth finds other ways to speak.” Can you think of a real-world example of this?
Find the Vocabulary
Read the story again carefully. Find each of these Unit 3 vocabulary words in the text. Read the full sentence aloud, then write your own sentence using the same word.
proclaimed
dictator
administration
bureaucracy
funeral
starvation
crowd
prohibited
frustration
oppress
treacherous
refuse
crimson
vicinity
collective
hardship
abolitionist
propaganda
typhoid
carbon
observable
endure
Find the Grammar
The story contains many examples of this unit’s grammar. Find and copy out:
Three sentences that use the present simple tense to state a general truth or fact.
Three sentences that contain a relative clause with who.
One sentence that contains a relative clause with which.
One sentence that contains a relative clause with whose.
Now You Try: Writing
Creative Writing

Write a diary entry as Nia. Imagine you are Nia on the night she and Juma tie the first red cloths to the trees. What are you feeling? What are you hoping for? Write 8–10 sentences. Use at least two relative clauses and two vocabulary words from the unit.

Write a letter from Nia to a friend in another village. Explain what has happened in Kalembo and what the red cloths mean. Use the present simple tense for facts about the situation and the past simple for events that already happened.

Write a new ending. What if Nia could speak to the soldiers directly? Write a short scene (6–8 sentences) where she tells them why the village deserves to be free. Use powerful vocabulary from the unit.
Connecting the Story to the Unit Theme
The story is called “The Red Cloth”, but this unit is called “Life in the Wild.” Think about the connection:
How is the struggle of the villagers similar to the struggle of animals in the wild?
The passage in the Reading section talks about how removing one species can break a food chain. In the story, what happens when the soldiers remove people’s freedoms?
Both the reading passage and the story talk about endurance. Compare how animals endure in the wild and how the villagers endure under occupation.

Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 3. They are divided into four groups. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example sentence out loud, and then try the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Power, Control, and Injustice
Practise these words
Use threat and treacherous in two sentences about the natural world. For example: "The rising sea levels are a ______ to coastal habitats."
Explain the difference between oppress and persecute in your own words.

Group 3: Nature and the Environment

My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!

Using Your Vocabulary in Context
The best way to remember new words is to use them in your own writing and speaking. Try these challenges:
Challenge 1: Write a Short Paragraph
Write a paragraph of 5-6 sentences about an East African ecosystem. Use at least six vocabulary words from this unit. Underline each vocabulary word you use.
Challenge 2: Tell a Story

Use at least five vocabulary words to tell a short story (real or made up) about someone who faces a difficult situation and shows courage. Record yourself telling the story and listen back. Did you use the words correctly?
Challenge 3: Word Connections
Some words in this unit are connected to each other. Draw lines between the words that go together, and explain why:
dictator ↔ oppress (Why?)
funeral ↔ starvation (Why?)
refuse ↔ endure (Why?)
threat ↔ treacherous (Why?)
carbon ↔ observable (Why?)
tables: Something that can cause harm or danger.
The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.
Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems.
The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.
A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others.
The dictator did not allow people to speak freely or choose their leaders.
To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
The soldiers tried to oppress the villagers by taking away their rights.
A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble.
After the drought, many families faced hardship because they lost their crops.
To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity.
It is wrong to persecute people just because they are different.
To say that something is not allowed.
The soldiers tried to prohibit gatherings in the village.
To keep going through something difficult without giving up.
The villagers had to endure months of harsh rules and limited food.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the power and control words?
Can you help me use these words in sentences about real situations?
What is the difference between a dictator and a fair leader?
To say something loudly or officially.
The leader proclaimed that the village now belonged to a foreign power.
A ceremony to remember and say goodbye to someone who has died.
The whole community gathered at the funeral to honour the old farmer.
A feeling of being upset or annoyed because you cannot do something you want.
Nia felt frustration when the soldiers banned their gathering.
To say no to something or not accept it.
Mzee Baraka chose to refuse to hand over his harvest to the soldiers.
A system of managing a government or organisation with many rules and officials.
The soldiers set up a bureaucracy with papers and stamps to control the village.
The group of people who manage a government, school, or organisation.
The administration centre was a stone building filled with rules.
A person who wants to end something, especially slavery.
Kwame was an abolitionist who fought slavery through song and secret messages.
Information, often false or misleading, used to influence how people think.
The soldiers called the school books propaganda and burned several of them.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the community and society words?
Can you explain what an abolitionist does using real historical examples?
Can you help me write a paragraph using at least four words from this group?
The condition of not having enough food to eat, causing extreme hunger and weakness.
Without rain, the animals on the savannah face starvation.
A basic element found in all living things and in materials like coal and diamonds.
Plants take in carbon dioxide from the air to help them grow.
A serious illness caused by bacteria that can spread through dirty water or food.
The health worker warned that typhoid was spreading because of unsafe water.
Something that can be seen or noticed.
The migration of wildebeest is one of the most observable events in nature.
A person or thing that comes into a place and takes control without permission.
Invasive plant species can act as invaders in a habitat, pushing out native plants.
The sky turned crimson as the sun set behind the mountains.
The area around a particular place.
There are many different bird species in the vicinity of the lake.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the nature and environment words?
Can you help me understand how carbon moves through an ecosystem?
Can you explain what typhoid is and how it spreads?
Done by or belonging to a whole group acting together.
The silence of the villagers was a collective act of quiet resistance.
A large group of people gathered together.
A crowd gathered beside the river to pray for the old farmer.
To make sure that something happens or is done.
Rangers work hard to ensure that wild animals are protected.
Causing or involving a lot of physical force or harm.
The villagers chose peaceful resistance instead of violent actions.
The news about the burning of school books was very disturbing.
Related to the study of ancient cultures through their buildings, tools, and objects.
The archaeological site near the village revealed tools from thousands of years ago.
