paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 4: Inventions and Creativity
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Grammar Lessons

This file has three grammar lessons. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice activities, and check your answers. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Past Simple Tense (Reviewed)
What it means and why it matters

The past simple tense is used to talk about actions or events that happened in the past and are now finished. It tells us that something occurred at a specific time and is complete. For example, “The explorer claimed a new route” tells us this action happened in the past. Knowing how to use the past simple helps us share stories, describe experiences, write about history, and communicate about things that have already happened.
How to form the Past Simple
Regular verbs: Add -ed to the base form.
play → played
claim → claimed
propose → proposed (drop the silent e, add -d)
travel → travelled (double the final consonant after a short vowel)
carry → carried (change y to i after a consonant, then add -ed)
Irregular verbs: These do not follow the -ed pattern. You must memorise them.
Forming negatives and questions
Negatives: did not (didn’t) + base form
✓  She did not see the machine.
✗  She did not saw the machine. (Never use past form after did not.)
Questions: Did + subject + base form?
Did the physicist propose a theory? — Yes, she did.
Did they find water? — No, they didn’t.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Forgetting to change the verb: “I play football yesterday” → “I played football yesterday.”
Using past form after did not: “She did not went” → “She did not go.”
Using past form in questions: “Did you went?” → “Did you go?”
Memory tip
Think of the past simple as a time machine! For regular verbs, the ticket is -ed. For irregular verbs, you need a special ticket — learn the common ones by heart!
Practice 1: Past Simple
Fill in the blanks with the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
The explorer __________ (claim) a new route.
The physicist __________ (propose) a theory.
Last weekend, I __________ (visit) my grandparents.
They __________ (have) a great time at the party.
We __________ (go) on an excursion to the river.
She __________ (write) a letter to her friend.
The team __________ (find) water at the base of the cliffs.
The nomads __________ (travel) through the desert for weeks.
Answers: 1. claimed  2. proposed  3. visited  4. had  5. went  6. wrote  7. found  8. travelled
Lesson 2: Will and Won’t for Predictions
What it means and why it matters

We use will and won’t (will not) to make predictions about the future. Predictions are guesses about what we think will happen. This is important because it helps us express our thoughts about the future, share ideas, and make plans. For example, “This initiative will change future navigation” is a prediction about the future.
How to form predictions
Positive: Subject + will + base form of the verb
She will play soccer tomorrow.
The invention will help many people.
Negative: Subject + won’t + base form of the verb
He won’t eat broccoli.
They won’t finish if they waste time.
Adding certainty
Use phrases like I think, I believe, or I am sure to show how certain you are:
I think it will be sunny tomorrow. (less certain)
I am sure the project will succeed. (very certain)
I believe they won’t need help. (fairly certain)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Wrong verb form after will: “She will eats the cake” → “She will eat the cake.” Always use the base form after will.
Confusing will and won’t: Think about whether you predict something will happen (will) or will not happen (won’t).
Memory tip
Think of will as a “yes” for the future and won’t as a “no” for the future. Ask yourself: Am I saying yes or no to this happening?
Practice 2: Will and Won’t
Complete the sentences using will or won’t.
I think my brother __________ (clean) his room today.
They __________ (go) to the park if it rains.
This initiative __________ (change) future navigation.
She __________ (like) the new invention.
We __________ (finish) the project by Friday!
If shared peacefully, migration and farming __________ (improve).
Answers: 1. will  2. won’t  3. will  4. will  5. will  6. will
Lesson 3: Prepositional Verbs
What they are and why they matter

Prepositional verbs are verbs that are always combined with a specific preposition. A preposition shows the relationship between parts of a sentence. When we pair a verb with a preposition, it gives the verb a specific meaning. Using the correct preposition is important — saying “rely on” makes sense, but “rely to” does not.
Common prepositional verbs
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Leaving out the preposition: “I rely my friends” → “I rely on my friends.”
Using the wrong preposition: “I travel over the city” → “I travel through the city.”
Memory tip
Think of the verb and its preposition as best friends — they always go together. If you separate them, the meaning changes or disappears.
Practice 3: Prepositional Verbs
Choose the correct preposition to complete each sentence.
I always rely __________ (on / at) my teacher for advice.
We travel __________ (through / over) the mountains every winter.
Can you wait __________ (for / to) me after school?
We set out __________ (from / at) the park at noon.
The cake consists __________ (of / from) flour, sugar, and eggs.
The explorers relied __________ (on / in) the stars for navigation.
Answers: 1. on  2. through  3. for  4. from  5. of  6. on
Bonus: Sequencing Words for Presenting
What they are and why they matter

Sequencing words help us organise our ideas in the order they happen. They make it easier for listeners and readers to follow along. This is especially important when explaining how an invention works or presenting the steps of a procedure.
Common sequencing words
First — the beginning step
Next / Then — the following steps
After that — what comes later
Finally — the last step
Example
First, we explored the straits. Then, we established a research site. After that, we collected samples from the water. Finally, we wrote our report.
Practice 4: Sequencing Words
Fill in the blanks with the correct sequencing word (First, Then, After that, Finally).
__________, we arrived at the beach.
__________, we built sandcastles.
__________, we had a picnic.
__________, we went home.
Answers: 1. First  2. Then  3. After that  4. Finally
tables: I rely on my parents for help.
We travel through the forest every summer.
to stay in one place until something happens
I will wait for my turn.
to begin a journey from a place
We set out from home at 8 AM.
The team consists of eight people.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me more past simple tense sentences to practise?”
“Can you test me on irregular past forms?”
“Can you help me use sequencing words when describing how my invention works?”

paragraphs: Unit 4: Inventions and Creativity
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 4! This unit is all about inventions and creativity — two powerful forces that have shaped the world we live in today. Throughout history, people have looked at problems around them and imagined new ways to solve them. From the simple wheel to the complex machines of the modern age, every invention began as an idea in somebody’s mind. In this unit, you will explore how inventions work, why they matter, and how ordinary people — including young people just like you — have changed the world with creative thinking.

You will also learn about the past simple tense, which is the grammar tool we use to describe events that happened in the past. Since inventions are part of history, the past simple tense is the perfect way to talk about them. You will practise using regular and irregular past forms, forming negative sentences with did not, and asking questions with Did...? You will also review will and won’t for making predictions about future inventions and explore prepositional verbs such as rely on, travel through, and wait for.

By the end of the unit, you will read a passage about inventions that changed everyday life, study vocabulary connected to science, exploration, and technology, and enjoy the full story “The Last Straits” — an adventure about a team of explorers in East Africa who discover a hidden water source and must decide how to share it fairly. You will also design your own invention and present it to others, combining creativity with the English language skills you are building every day.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use vocabulary related to inventions, exploration, science, and technology confidently in speech and writing.
Read and understand an informational passage about inventions and answer comprehension questions about it.
Identify the past simple tense in sentences and explain when and why we use it.
Form regular past simple verbs by adding -ed and recall common irregular past forms (went, saw, made, wrote).
Write negative past simple sentences using did not + base verb and ask past simple questions using Did + subject + base verb.
Use will and won’t to make predictions about future inventions and technology.
Recognise and use prepositional verbs such as rely on, travel through, consist of, and wait for.
Design and describe your own invention using descriptive and persuasive language.
Present your invention idea clearly, using sequencing words (first, next, then, finally) and persuasive language.
Evaluate the positive and negative impact of technology on daily life, thinking critically about inventions.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and use them in sentences.
Work through the Grammar file on the Past Simple Tense (reviewed), including will/won’t and prepositional verbs.
Read the Story file, “The Last Straits”, and answer the reflection questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary

Before you read the passage, make sure you know these important words. You will meet them throughout this unit. Study them carefully, say each example sentence aloud, and try to use each word in a sentence of your own.
Science and Exploration Words
Actions and Processes
Places and People
Reading: “Inventions That Changed Everyday Life”

Read this passage carefully. It describes how inventions have changed the way people live, work, and solve problems. Pay attention to the past simple tense — many sentences use it to describe events that already happened. As you read, underline any vocabulary words from this unit that you recognise.

For thousands of years, human beings have solved problems by inventing new tools and machines. Some inventions were simple, like the wheel or the needle, but they changed life forever. Others were complex, like the printing press or the electric generator, and they opened doors that nobody imagined before.

One of the most important inventions in history was the compass. Before the compass existed, sailors relied on the stars for navigation. If clouds covered the sky, ships lost their way. Chinese inventors created the first magnetic compass over a thousand years ago. Once sailors had this tool, they explored new territories, crossed dangerous straits, and connected civilizations that had never met before. The compass did not just change travel — it changed the way humans understood the whole world.

Another invention that transformed daily life was the printing press. In the year 1440, a German inventor named Johannes Gutenberg proposed a machine that could print books quickly. Before his invention, every book was copied by hand, which took months. The printing press made books cheaper and easier to produce. Within a few decades, millions of people who had never owned a book could finally read and learn. Knowledge was no longer something only the elite controlled — it belonged to everyone.

In the modern era, the electric generator changed how people lived and worked. A generator converts movement into electricity. Once communities had electricity, they could light their homes, power machines, and store food safely. In many parts of East Africa and the wider world, generators still provide power to schools, hospitals, and homes that are not connected to the main electricity grid. A single generator can make the difference between a community that struggles in the dark and one that thrives with light.

Today, young inventors continue to solve real problems. In Kenya, a teenager invented a device that keeps lions away from livestock by using flashing lights. In Somalia, students have built simple solar-powered chargers so families in rural areas can charge their phones. These inventions show that you do not need a big laboratory or a lot of money to make a difference — you need curiosity, creativity, and commitment.

Every great invention started with a question: What if? What if we could fly? What if we could talk to someone on the other side of the world? What if we could make clean water from the sea? The inventors who changed history did not have all the answers. They had the courage to ask questions, try new ideas, and learn from their mistakes. That is the true procedure of invention: observe, question, experiment, and improve. And it is a procedure that anyone — including you — can follow.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage if you need to.
What did sailors use for navigation before the compass was invented?
Who created the first magnetic compass, and how long ago was it made?
What problem did Gutenberg’s printing press solve?
How did the printing press change who could read and learn?
What does a generator do, and why is it important in East Africa?
Name one invention by a young person mentioned in the passage. What problem did it solve?
What does the passage say is the “true procedure of invention”?
In your opinion, which invention from the passage is the most important? Explain why.
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the passage and explain what they mean in your own words. Then write your own sentence using each word.
navigation — Find it in the paragraph about the compass.
territories — Find it in the same paragraph about the compass.
generator — Find it in the paragraph about electricity.
commitment — Find it in the paragraph about young inventors.
procedure — Find it in the final paragraph.
Think Deeper
These questions ask you to think carefully and share your own ideas. There is no single right answer — use evidence from the passage and your own experience.
The passage says knowledge was once controlled by the elite. What does this mean? Do you think knowledge is shared fairly today? Why or why not?
The passage mentions young inventors from Kenya and Somalia. Why do you think the author included these examples? What message is the author trying to send?
The passage ends with the phrase “a procedure that anyone — including you — can follow.” Do you agree? What stops some people from inventing, and what could help?
Think about an invention that is important in your daily life (for example: a phone, a bicycle, a water pump, a solar panel). How would your life be different without it?
Grammar Focus: Past Simple Tense (Reviewed)
What is the Past Simple Tense?

The past simple tense is used to talk about actions or events that happened in the past and are now finished. When we describe inventions and discoveries, we almost always use the past simple because these events happened at a specific time in the past. For example, when we say “The explorer claimed a new route,” we understand that this action is complete — it happened before now and it is finished.

Understanding the past simple tense is essential for telling stories, describing your experiences, writing reports about historical events, and explaining how things were done in the past. It is one of the most commonly used tenses in English, and mastering it will make your speaking and writing much stronger.
How to Form the Past Simple
There are two types of verbs in the past simple: regular verbs and irregular verbs.
Regular verbs: Add -ed to the base form of the verb.
play → played
claim → claimed
propose → proposed (drop the silent e, then add -d)
travel → travelled (double the final consonant after a short vowel, then add -ed)
relocate → relocated
Irregular verbs: These do not follow the -ed pattern. You must learn them by memory.
go → went
see → saw
make → made
write → wrote
have → had
find → found
build → built
run → ran
Negative Sentences
To make a negative sentence in the past simple, use did not (or the short form didn’t) + the base form of the verb. Important: do not use the past form after did not.
✓  She did not see the machine. (correct — base form after did not)
✗  She did not saw the machine. (incorrect — do not use past form after did not)
✓  They didn’t travel through the desert. (correct)
✗  They didn’t travelled through the desert. (incorrect)
Questions
To form a question in the past simple, use Did + subject + base form of the verb.
Did the explorer claim a new route? — Yes, he did. / No, he didn’t.
Did you visit the museum? — Yes, I did.
Did they find water? — No, they didn’t.
Time Expressions Used with Past Simple
The past simple often appears with time expressions that tell us when something happened:
yesterday — I visited the library yesterday.
last week / last month / last year — We travelled to the coast last summer.
in 1440 / in 2010 — Gutenberg proposed his printing press in 1440.
ago — The compass was invented over a thousand years ago.
when I was young — When I was young, I played outside every day.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Forgetting to change the verb: “I play football yesterday” should be “I played football yesterday.” Always check whether you are talking about the past.
Using the past form after did not: “She did not went” should be “She did not go.” After did not, always use the base form.
Using the past form in questions: “Did you went?” should be “Did you go?” After Did, always use the base form.

Spelling errors with regular verbs: Remember the rules: drop silent e (propose → proposed), double the consonant after a short vowel (stop → stopped), change y to i after a consonant (carry → carried).
Memory Tip

Think of the past simple as a time machine. When you use it, you travel back in time to describe something that is already done. For regular verbs, the ticket to the past is -ed. For irregular verbs, you need a special ticket — learn the most common ones by heart, and you will be able to travel through time whenever you write or speak!
Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks (Regular Verbs)
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
The explorer __________ (claim) a new route through the mountains.
The physicist __________ (propose) a bold theory about energy.
Last month, we __________ (visit) the science museum in the city.
The team __________ (relocate) to a safer area after the storm.
The government __________ (regulate) how water was shared between the communities.
She __________ (refuse) to give up on her invention, even when it failed the first time.
The students __________ (monitor) the weather patterns for three weeks.
Our class __________ (travel) through the old part of the city on our excursion.
Answers: 1. claimed  2. proposed  3. visited  4. relocated  5. regulated  6. refused  7. monitored  8. travelled
Exercise 2: Fill in the Blanks (Irregular Verbs)
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
The inventor __________ (make) a machine that could print books.
We __________ (go) on an excursion to the river last Friday.
She __________ (write) a proposal to share the discovery.
The caravan __________ (find) a hidden water source beneath the cliffs.
The physicist __________ (have) a brilliant idea that changed everything.
The explorers __________ (see) ancient rock carvings on the cliff walls.
The nomads __________ (build) shelters from the materials they found.
The children __________ (run) to the generator when the power went out.
Answers: 1. made  2. went  3. wrote  4. found  5. had  6. saw  7. built  8. ran
Exercise 3: Rewrite as Negative
Rewrite each sentence as a negative sentence using did not (didn’t).
The explorer claimed the land. → The explorer __________.
They found the hidden river. → They __________.
She proposed a new plan. → She __________.
We visited the old territory. → We __________.
The generator worked during the storm. → The generator __________.
Answers: 1. did not claim the land  2. did not find the hidden river  3. did not propose a new plan  4. did not visit the old territory  5. did not work during the storm
Exercise 4: Form Questions
Turn each statement into a past simple question.
The caravan travelled through the desert. → __________?
The physicist proposed a new theory. → __________?
The eyewitness saw the whole event. → __________?
They relocated to a new area. → __________?
Answers: 1. Did the caravan travel through the desert?  2. Did the physicist propose a new theory?  3. Did the eyewitness see the whole event?  4. Did they relocate to a new area?
Exercise 5: Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence has one grammar mistake. Find it and write the correct sentence.
She did not visited the laboratory yesterday.
Did they found the hidden straits?
The inventor propose a machine last year.
We did not went to the museum on Friday.
The nomads travel through the desert last month.

Answers: 1. She did not visit the laboratory yesterday.  2. Did they find the hidden straits?  3. The inventor proposed a machine last year.  4. We did not go to the museum on Friday.  5. The nomads travelled through the desert last month.
Writing Workshop: Invent a Gadget
Your Task

In this workshop, you will design your own invention and write about it. Your invention should solve a real problem that you or your community face. It can be simple or complex — what matters is that it is creative, useful, and clearly described.
Step 1: Choose a Problem
Think about a problem in your daily life or in your community. Here are some ideas to get you started, but you can choose any problem you like:
Water is difficult to collect or carry.
There is not enough light for studying in the evening.
Food spoils quickly because there is no refrigeration.
It is hard to communicate with family members who live far away.
Animals eat crops or wander into the village at night.
Dust and sand make it hard to keep things clean.
Step 2: Design Your Invention
Think about what your invention looks like, how it works, and what materials it uses. Draw a simple diagram or picture of your invention. Label the important parts.
Answer these planning questions:
What is your invention called?
What problem does it solve?
What does it look like? What shape and size is it?
What materials is it made from?
How does someone use it? Describe the steps.
Who would benefit from this invention?
Step 3: Write Your Description
Write a description of your invention using three paragraphs. Use the structure below:
Paragraph 1 — Introduction: Name your invention and explain the problem it solves. Use persuasive language to explain why this problem is important.
Paragraph 2 — How it works: Describe what your invention looks like and how someone uses it. Use sequencing words (first, next, then, finally) to explain the steps clearly.
Paragraph 3 — Why it matters: Explain who would benefit from your invention and how it would change their lives. Use will and won’t to make predictions about the future impact of your gadget.
Example: The SunJar
Here is a short example to guide you:

My invention is called the SunJar. Many children in rural areas cannot study in the evening because their homes do not have electricity. This is a serious problem because without light, students fall behind in their schoolwork.

The SunJar is a glass jar with a small solar panel on the lid. During the day, the user places the jar in direct sunlight. The solar panel collects energy and stores it in a tiny battery inside the jar. First, you open the lid and check the battery level. Next, you place the jar on a table or hang it from a hook. Then, you press the button on the side. Finally, the jar fills with bright, warm light that lasts for up to six hours.

If schools and families use the SunJar, children will be able to study after sunset. They won’t need expensive fuel or candles. This simple invention could help thousands of students across the region complete their homework and improve their results.
Writing Checklist
Before you finish, check your writing against this list:
Does your description have three clear paragraphs (introduction, how it works, why it matters)?
Did you use sequencing words (first, next, then, finally) when explaining how the invention works?
Did you use will and won’t to make predictions about your invention’s future impact?
Did you use the past simple tense correctly when describing how you designed or tested your gadget?
Did you include at least five vocabulary words from this unit?
Did you check your spelling and punctuation?
Is your writing persuasive — would someone want to use your invention after reading about it?
Listening & Speaking
Listening: Invention Presentations
Ask your AI tutor to describe an invention to you. Listen carefully and then answer these questions:
What is the name of the invention?
What problem does it solve?
When was it invented?
How does it work?
Do you think this invention is important? Why or why not?
After answering, ask your AI tutor to describe a second invention. This time, take notes as you listen. Compare the two inventions: which one do you think is more useful, and why?
Speaking 1: Present Your Invention
Prepare a short presentation about the invention you designed in the Writing Workshop. Your presentation should be about two minutes long. Use this structure:
Greeting: “Hello, my name is ______ and today I want to tell you about my invention.”
Problem: Explain the problem your invention solves. “Many people face the problem of ______.”
Solution: Describe your invention and how it works. Use sequencing words.
Impact: Explain how your invention will help people. Use will and won’t.
Closing: “Thank you for listening. Do you have any questions?”

Practise your presentation at least three times before you deliver it. You can practise with your AI tutor, a family member, or a friend. Record yourself if possible, then listen back and check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use past simple when talking about how you designed it? Did you use will/won’t when talking about its future impact?
Speaking 2: Discussion — Technology in Our Lives
Discuss these questions with your AI tutor, a classmate, or a family member. Give reasons for your answers and try to use vocabulary from this unit.
What is the most useful invention in your home? Why?
Can you think of an invention that has both good and bad effects? What are they?
If you could invent one thing to help your community, what would it be?
Do you think children can be inventors? Why or why not?
The passage says “you do not need a big laboratory or a lot of money to make a difference.” Do you agree? Explain your answer.
Speaking 3: Role Play — The Invention Fair
Work with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor. One person is the inventor and the other is a judge at an invention fair. The inventor presents their gadget. The judge asks questions like:
“How did you come up with this idea?” (past simple)
“What materials did you use?” (past simple)
“Who will benefit from this invention?” (will/won’t)
“What will happen if people start using it?” (will/won’t)
“Did you test it? What happened?” (past simple)
After the role play, swap roles so both people get to practise.
Extra Activities and Exercises
Activity 1: Timeline of Inventions
Create a timeline showing five important inventions. For each one, write the date, the name of the invention, and one sentence about it using the past simple tense.
For example:
Around 3500 BC — The wheel: Early civilizations invented the wheel to carry heavy loads.
Around 1000 AD — The compass: Chinese inventors created the magnetic compass for navigation.
Add at least three more inventions of your choice. You can research them with your AI tutor or use your own knowledge.
Activity 2: Prepositional Verbs Challenge
Prepositional verbs are verbs that always go with a specific preposition. Complete each sentence with the correct preposition: on, through, for, of, from.
The explorers relied __________ the stars for navigation.
The caravan travelled __________ the desert for three weeks.
We must wait __________ the results of the experiment.
The team consists __________ eight experienced members.
They set out __________ the capital at dawn.
Answers: 1. on  2. through  3. for  4. of  5. from
Activity 3: Will and Won’t Predictions
Make predictions about inventions and the future. Complete each sentence with will or won’t and an idea of your own.
In 50 years, I think cars __________.
I believe schools __________.
I think people __________ use paper books anymore because __________.
Robots __________ do all household tasks because __________.
Clean water __________ be available to everyone if __________.
There are no single right answers for this activity. The important thing is to use will and won’t correctly and give a reason for your prediction.
Activity 4: Vocabulary Crossword Clues
Write a definition or clue for each vocabulary word. Then give your clues to a partner and see if they can guess the word.
A narrow passage of water between two areas of land. (__________)
A person who moves from place to place instead of having one home. (__________)
A machine that produces electricity. (__________)
To say no to something or not accept it. (__________)
The study of weather and how it changes. (__________)
A group of people travelling together, often across a desert. (__________)
To suggest an idea or plan for others to think about. (__________)
A person who sees something happen with their own eyes. (__________)
Answers: 1. straits  2. nomad  3. generator  4. refuse  5. meteorology  6. caravan  7. propose  8. eyewitness
Activity 5: Write a Short Report
Choose one famous inventor or invention and write a short report (8–10 sentences). Use the past simple tense to describe what happened. Include:
Who the inventor was and where they lived.
What problem they wanted to solve.
What they invented and how it worked.
How the invention changed people’s lives.

Here are some suggestions: the telephone (Alexander Graham Bell), the light bulb (Thomas Edison), the astrolabe (Islamic Golden Age scholars), or the printing press (Johannes Gutenberg). You can also choose someone else with the help of your AI tutor.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to me describe my invention and give me feedback?”
Get feedback on writing: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more past simple tense exercises?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet” for each statement. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can use vocabulary about inventions, science, and exploration.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can read a passage about inventions and answer comprehension questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can identify and form the past simple tense (regular and irregular verbs).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write negative past simple sentences (did not + base verb).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can form past simple questions (Did + subject + base verb?).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use will and won’t to make predictions about the future.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use prepositional verbs correctly (rely on, travel through, wait for, consist of).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can design and describe my own invention in writing.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can present my invention clearly using sequencing and persuasive language.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can discuss the impact of technology on daily life and give reasons for my opinions.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 5
Make sure you know all the Unit 4 vocabulary words and can use them in sentences.
Keep practising the past simple tense — it appears in almost every unit from now on.
Our next unit is “Money Matters”. Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 5? Can you get me ready?”

Well done — you explored the world of inventions and creativity, and you grew your English skills at the same time! Be proud of your hard work, and keep asking “What if?” — because every great invention starts with a curious mind.
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson. Work through each section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Past Simple Tense), and the Story “The Last Straits”.
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions.
A person who travels to new places to discover things.
The explorer mapped a route through the desert.
A scientist who studies how things move and work.
The physicist proposed a new theory about energy.
The study of weather and how it changes.
Meteorology helps farmers know when rain will come.
The generator provided power during the storm.
The process of finding your way from one place to another.
Early sailors used the stars for navigation.
A group of people living together with their own culture and laws.
Ancient Egypt was a great civilization known for its pyramids.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 4: Inventions and Creativity
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Last Straits”
The Last Straits

Long ago, before satellites mapped every coastline, explorers relied on wind, stars, and memory. In the heart of East Africa, a young physicist named Omari joined a desert caravan setting out toward the unknown.

Their goal? To cross the Mzingi Straits, a mysterious water passage between two towering cliffs. No one had returned alive, but old maps claimed an extended river system ran beneath the cliffs. If true, it would help small communities relocate and survive years of drought.

Omari’s team consisted of eight people: two experienced explorers, a weather expert in meteorology, a translator, and a group of Tuareg guides from the vicinity. Each had made a serious commitment to the mission — not for glory, but to bring change.
The group’s leader, Zahra, stood before a map made of stitched cloth. Her hands, rough from years of expeditions, smoothed the fabric like it held secrets.
“We will travel through harsh terrain,” she said. “But if we succeed, this discovery will change how people understand the region.”
Omari asked, “What will we do if the riverbed is dry?”
“We’ll adapt,” Zahra replied. “The initiative depends on our resolve.”
Their first camp was near a cluster of acacia trees. Omari set up a generator to power their tools. The machine hummed across the sand, pulsing like a second heart for the team.
“This generator is our lifeline,” he told the group. “Without it, we can’t track weather or charge equipment.”
Each morning, they rose early to record data. Omari explained the procedure to the younger guides.
“First, collect samples. Then label and store them carefully. Finally, we submit our findings to the agency.”
The Tuareg youth nodded. “You are not like other outsiders,” one said. “You explain things.”
Omari smiled. “Good science starts by listening.”
By the third week, the desert turned treacherous. Sandstorms appeared without warning. One storm destroyed their water cart.
“We must extend the trip,” Zahra said. “We’ll stop in the next territory for supplies.”
They relied on nearby nomads, who shared dried meat and sour milk in exchange for medicine and solar batteries.
“These people follow herds and stars,” Zahra explained. “They’ve navigated these sands for generations.”
At night, Omari taught a young boy how to use the compass and read the clouds. In return, the boy sang desert songs that described the stars like friends.
Then came Karim.
One evening, just after dusk, a lone man arrived on camelback. His robe was sun-bleached and torn, but his presence was sharp and calm.
“I am Karim,” he said. “I’ve lived near the straits. I’ve seen what lies beyond.”
“An eyewitness account?” Zahra asked. “Can you describe it?”
“I saw water rushing like thunder,” he said. “But I also saw markers — skulls on posts. Someone refused to let strangers pass.”
The group fell quiet.
“Who controls it now?” Omari asked.
Karim shrugged. “A forgotten civilization, or a violent faction. Maybe both.”
Omari looked again at the fading map. “We’ll proceed carefully. We are not here to fight.”
The caravan reached the cliffs two days later. The wind screamed through narrow passages. Rock carvings lined the stone — figures with long limbs and shields.
“This is a warning,” Karim said. “But it’s also confirmation.”
Using climbing gear, the team descended. At the base, they found smooth black stones, and in the cracks — cool mist rising.
“There’s water,” Omari whispered. “We were right.”
But something else awaited.
Dozens of footprints. A camp, recently abandoned. Fires still warm. Tools half-buried in dust.
“They must have relocated in a hurry,” Zahra said. “But why?”
Among the ashes, they found scraps of paper, notes in code. One word had not burned: Segregation.
Zahra frowned. “This is no longer just a river expedition. Someone tried to control who passed — who drank.”
“Why?” a guide asked.
“Because if you control land, you control people,” Omari said. “And if you control water, you control life.”
They set up base higher on the ridge. Omari reconnected the generator and began measuring water speed, purity, and flow.
“We must regulate how this is used,” he said. “It cannot belong to any one group.”
Zahra added, “We’re not just explorers. We’re stewards of what we find.”
That night, Zahra gathered everyone.
“We’ll write a proposal,” she said. “To explain what this land offers — and why it must remain open.”
The group drafted their report:

“We propose an open science zone. This land should not be claimed by any one nation. Its production of clean water must support all nearby communities. We must resist systems that repeat past segregation.”
They added predictions:
“If this area is claimed by force, violence will follow.”
“If shared peacefully, migration and farming will improve.”
“If ignored, drought will deepen.”
Omari added a final note:
“Real exploration begins with respect. We do not own this place. We study, learn, and serve.”
As the team packed to leave, one member uncovered a rusted metal plate buried in the sand. The letters barely readable:
“Confederacy Outpost – 1927”
Karim pointed. “A failed empire,” he said. “They built walls. They were erased.”
The journey home was long. Omari taught the Tuareg youth to track clouds and draw their own maps. In return, the boys sang to him — songs that had guided travellers for centuries.
Back at the capital, Zahra delivered the report to the agency.
“The initiative is complete,” she said. “But the river is still flowing.”
A year later, schools across East Africa studied the Mzingi discovery. Wells were dug. No flag flew over the cliffs. The water remained free.
Omari gave talks at universities.
“What does science consist of?” he would ask. “It consists of questions, care, and courage.”
He always ended with a photo — Zahra, Karim, the Tuareg youth, and himself, standing together by the straits, with notebooks in hand and red cloths tied to signal peace.
“Explorers may map land,” he’d say. “But true exploration maps the future.”
And deep beneath the cliffs, the river still ran — untouched, unowned, waiting for the next ones who would come, not to take, but to understand.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the story if you need to.
Who is Omari, and what is his role in the team?
What is the goal of the caravan’s journey?
Who is Zahra, and what kind of leader is she? Give evidence from the story.
How did the team’s generator help them during the expedition?
What procedure did Omari teach the younger guides for collecting data?
What happened when a sandstorm destroyed their water cart?
Who is Karim, and what important information did he bring?
What did the team find at the base of the cliffs? Describe the scene.
What does the word “segregation” mean in this story? Why was it found in the abandoned camp?
What did the team write in their proposal, and why was it important?
Think and Discuss
These questions ask for your own thoughts and opinions. Use evidence from the story to support your answers.
Omari says, “Good science starts by listening.” What does he mean? Do you agree?
Zahra says, “We’re not just explorers. We’re stewards of what we find.” What is a steward? Why is this an important idea?
The rusted metal plate said “Confederacy Outpost – 1927.” Karim called it “a failed empire.” What lesson does this detail teach?
Why did the team propose that no single nation should claim the water? Do you think this is fair? Why or why not?
At the end, the story says the water “remained free.” Why do you think the author ended the story this way?
How does Omari’s relationship with the Tuareg youth show mutual respect? Find two examples from the story.
Vocabulary in the Story
Find each of these Unit 4 vocabulary words in the story. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what it means in your own words.
straits
explorer
caravan
territory
nomad
navigation
physicist
meteorology
generator
eyewitness
segregation
relocate
commitment
consists
production
procedure
propose
monitor
refuse
regulate
claimed
extended
civilization
Grammar in the Story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find one example of each:
A sentence in the past simple tense (regular verb with -ed).
A sentence in the past simple tense (irregular verb).
A sentence using will or won’t for a prediction.
A sentence using a prepositional verb (rely on, travel through, wait for, consist of, set out from).
A sentence using sequencing words (first, then, finally).
Now You Try: Writing
Imagine you are Omari. Write a diary entry (8–10 sentences) about the day you discovered water at the base of the cliffs. Use the past simple tense.
The team wrote a proposal for an “open science zone.” Write your own short proposal (5–7 sentences) about something in your school or community that should be shared fairly.
Write a letter from Zahra to the agency, explaining what the team found and why the water must remain free. Use persuasive language and at least four vocabulary words from this unit.

Moral: Those who build walls are forgotten; those who share knowledge are remembered.

Group 1: Science and Exploration

Group 3: Science and Technology

Group 4: Actions and Descriptions
Practise these words

Group 5: Verbs for Persuasion and Change
Practise these words

Group 6: Society and History

Your Unit 4 Word Wall
Copy these words onto a piece of paper or card and put them on your wall. Try to use at least three Unit 4 words in conversation every day.

straits · excursion · explorer · caravan · territory · nomad · navigation · civilization · physicist · meteorology · generator · eyewitness · monitor · production · procedure · claimed · extended · established · commitment · elite · chaos · segregation · relocate · propose · refuse · regulate · consists

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 5: Money Matters
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Six grammar lessons

This file contains six grammar lessons for Unit 5. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice exercises, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Comparative Adverbs
What the rule means and why it matters

Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. They tell us if something is done in a greater or lesser way than something else. This is important because it helps us describe differences in actions, making our conversations and writing clearer and more interesting.
For example, when we say "She adapted more quickly than I did," we are comparing how quickly she adapted to how quickly I adapted. This helps us understand that her action was done at a faster rate.
How to form comparative adverbs (step by step)
Identify the adverb you want to compare. Adverbs often tell us how, when, or where something happens. Examples include *quickly*, *slowly*, *easily*, and *happily*.
Decide how to make it comparative:
For short adverbs (one syllable), add -er:
fast → faster: "He runs faster than his brother."
hard → harder: "She studies harder than before."
late → later: "They arrived later than expected."
For longer adverbs (two or more syllables), use more before the adverb:
quickly → more quickly: "She adapted more quickly than I did."
carefully → more carefully: "He counted the money more carefully this time."
eagerly → more eagerly: "The students waited more eagerly for the results."
For irregular adverbs, learn the special forms:
well → better: "She sings better than her sister."
badly → worse: "He feels worse today than yesterday."
far → farther / further: "We walked farther than planned."
Make the comparison with "than"
Always use than to show what you are comparing:
"She speaks English more confidently than she did last year."
"He runs faster than his sister."
"She dances more gracefully than anyone else."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using "-er" with adverbs that have two or more syllables.
Wrong: "She runs quicklier than me." ✘
Right: "She runs more quickly than me." ✔
Mistake 2: Forgetting to use "than" when making a comparison.
Wrong: "He runs faster I do." ✘
Right: "He runs faster than I do." ✔
Quick memory trick
One syllable? Just add -er (fast → faster). Two or more syllables? Use more (quickly → more quickly). Think of "more" as your helper for longer words!
Practice 1: Fill in the blanks
Write the correct comparative form of the adverb in brackets.
My brother plays video games ______ (badly) than I do.
She solved the maths problem ______ (quickly) than I expected.
He speaks ______ (softly) than his friend.
The bus arrived ______ (late) than usual.
She sings ______ (beautifully) than anyone in the choir.
Lesson 2: Quantifiers
What the rule means and why it matters

Quantifiers are words that tell us how much or how many of something there is. They help us understand the quantity of nouns, which can be really useful in both speaking and writing. For example, instead of saying "There are students," we can say "Both of the students had fabulous ideas." This makes our sentences clearer and more informative.
How to use quantifiers (step by step)
Identify the noun you want to describe. For example, in the sentence "Both of the students had fabulous ideas," the noun is "students."
Choose the right quantifier: Use "both" for two items. Use "neither" for two items to say not one of them. Use "some," "many," "few," and "a lot of" for larger groups.
Place the quantifier before the noun: "both students" or "neither answer."
Make sure it matches: "Both" needs a plural noun and plural verb. "Neither" needs a singular verb.
Examples
"Both of the students had fabulous ideas."
"Neither answer was negative."
"Some children like to play outside."
"Many animals live in the forest."
"A lot of people attended the concert."
"Each student received a book."
"Every family has different traditions."
"Only a few students were absent today."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using singular with "both".
Wrong: "Both student had ideas." ✘
Right: "Both students had ideas." ✔
Mistake 2: Mixing up "both" and "neither".
"Both" means yes, both of them. "Neither" means no, not one of them. Make sure you use the right one for your meaning.
Quick memory trick
Think of "both" as a pair (like shoes) that always comes together, and "neither" as a way to say "not either of two." Remember: both means "yes, both," and neither means "no, neither one."
Practice 2: Fill in the blanks
Use the correct quantifier in the blanks.
______ of the answers were correct. (both / neither)
I saw ______ cats in the garden. (some / many)
______ of the two friends could come to the party. (Both / Neither)
There are ______ apples in the basket. (few / a lot of)
______ student must bring their own pencil. (Each / Many)
Lesson 3: Reflexive Pronouns
What the rule means and why it matters

Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject of a sentence does the action to themselves. In English, we have these reflexive pronouns: *myself*, *yourself*, *himself*, *herself*, *itself*, *ourselves*, *yourselves*, and *themselves*. Understanding reflexive pronouns is important because it helps you express actions in a clear way, especially when you want to emphasise that someone is doing something for themselves.
How to form and use reflexive pronouns (step by step)
Identify the subject: Look for who is doing the action in the sentence.
Choose the right reflexive pronoun: Match the pronoun to the subject: I → myself, you → yourself, he → himself, she → herself, it → itself, we → ourselves, they → themselves.
Place the reflexive pronoun correctly: Put the reflexive pronoun after the verb or at the end of the sentence. Example: "They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Examples
"I taught myself how to ride a bike."
"She looked at herself in the mirror."
"We enjoyed ourselves at the party."
"He made the bed by himself."
"They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Emphasis use
Sometimes reflexive pronouns are used for emphasis — to highlight that someone did something alone or without help:
"I made the cake myself." (This highlights that you did it alone, not with help.)
"She solved the problem herself." (Nobody helped her.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Wrong: "He taught hisself to cook." ✘
Right: "He taught himself to cook." ✔
Mistake 2: Using a reflexive pronoun when it is not needed.
Wrong: "She enjoyed herself the movie." ✘
Right: "She enjoyed the movie." ✔ (The subject and object are different things here.)
Quick memory trick

Think of reflexive pronouns as "self" words! If the action bounces back to the same person who did it, you need a reflexive pronoun. I → myself, you → yourself, he → himself, she → herself, it → itself, we → ourselves, they → themselves.
Practice 3: Fill in the blanks
Choose the correct reflexive pronoun to complete each sentence.
She taught ______ how to dance.
We had a great time by ______ at the park.
He fixed the computer by ______.
The cat cleaned ______ after playing.
I reminded ______ to save my pocket money.
They prepared ______ for the school trip.
Lesson 4: Reported Speech (Introduction)
What the rule means and why it matters

Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is when we tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. For example, instead of saying "I am happy," we can say "She said she was happy." Understanding reported speech is important because it helps us share information while keeping the conversation flowing smoothly.
How to form reported speech (step by step)
Identify the original statement: For example, "I am frustrated by the change."
Change the pronouns: "I" changes to "she" or "he" depending on who is speaking.
Change the tense: Usually, move back one tense. "am" (present) changes to "was" (past).
Remove quotation marks: When we report speech, we do not use quotation marks.
Add a reporting verb: Use a reporting verb like "said" or "told."
Putting it all together: She said she was frustrated by the change.
Examples
Direct: "I love ice cream." → Reported: She said she loved ice cream.
Direct: "We are going to the park." → Reported: They said they were going to the park.
Direct: "Can you help me?" → Reported: He asked if I could help him.
Direct: "Please finish your homework." → Reported: The teacher told us to finish our homework.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Not changing the tense.
Wrong: She said she is tired. ✘
Right: She said she was tired. ✔
Mistake 2: Using quotation marks in reported speech.
Wrong: She said, "she was frustrated." ✘
Right: She said she was frustrated. ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of it like telling a story! When you report speech, you are sharing what someone else said, like you are telling a story. Just remember to change the words a little to make it fit your story.
Practice 4: Transform direct speech into reported speech
Direct: "I will help you." → Reported: She said ________.
Direct: "I am reading a book." → Reported: He said ________.
Direct: "Do you like pizza?" → Reported: She asked ________.
Direct: "We are saving money for Eid." → Reported: They said ________.
Lesson 5: Connecting Words
What the rule means and why it matters

Connecting words help us link ideas together smoothly in our writing and speaking. They make our sentences clearer and our thoughts easier to understand. By using connecting words, we can show how one idea relates to another, which is essential for good communication, especially when writing opinion essays.
The main connecting words
Firstly — to introduce the first point: "Firstly, saving money teaches patience."
However — to show a contrast or exception: "I wanted to save; however, I spent it on snacks."
Therefore — to show a result or conclusion: "She saved every week; therefore, she had enough for Eid."
In addition / Furthermore — to add another point: "In addition, budgeting helps families."
Finally — to indicate the last point: "Finally, I want to say that saving is for everyone."
For example / For instance — to give an example: "For example, you could save five coins a week."
In conclusion — to sum up: "In conclusion, financial literacy is a crucial skill."
How to use them in sentences
Connecting words usually go at the beginning of a sentence or clause, followed by a comma:
"Firstly, we need to gather our materials."
"I wanted to go outside; however, it started to rain."
"She studied hard; therefore, she passed with flying colours."
"Finally, we reached our destination after a long journey."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Forgetting the comma after the connecting word.
Wrong: "However I don't eat it often." ✘
Right: "However, I don't eat it often." ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of connecting words as bridges between two ideas. Just like a bridge helps you cross from one side to another, connecting words help your thoughts flow smoothly!
Practice 5: Fill in the blanks
Fill in the blanks with the correct connecting word: *Firstly, However, Therefore, Finally, In addition*.
I woke up late this morning. ______, I missed the bus.
______, we will go over the rules of the game.
I studied for the test. ______, I felt confident when I took it.
We had a lot of fun at the party. ______, we danced all night!
Saving money is important. ______, it teaches us to plan for the future.
Lesson 6: Tone Awareness
What the rule means and why it matters

Tone awareness is about understanding how the words we choose can change the way our message feels to the reader or listener. Different words can make a sentence sound exciting, serious, or even friendly. When we write persuasively (like in an opinion essay), choosing the right tone helps us communicate our feelings clearly.
How to use tone (step by step)
Identify the emotion: Think about how you want your reader to feel. Do you want them to feel excited, serious, or motivated?
Choose the right words: Use adverbs that match the feeling you want. For example: eagerly (excited), firmly (serious), dramatically (surprising).
Use the words in sentences: Place your chosen words in your sentences. Make sure they fit well.
Revise for tone: After writing, read your sentences out loud. Do they sound like the tone you intended?
Examples from the story
In "The Notice Board," Zanele learns that tone matters:
When she spoke firmly, people listened. (Serious and confident tone.)
When she spoke aggressively, they pushed back. (Harsh and forceful tone.)
When she spoke dramatically, they laughed. (Over-the-top tone.)
This shows that the same message can have very different effects depending on the tone we use.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using a word that does not match the tone.
Wrong: "The puppy firmly wagged its tail." (Too serious for a playful puppy.)
Right: "The puppy happily wagged its tail." ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of tone as the "mood" of your writing. Just like music can be happy or sad, your words can create different feelings. Choose your words like you choose your music!
Practice 6: Fill in the blanks
Complete the sentences using *dramatically*, *firmly*, or *eagerly*.
The chef ______ presented her new dish to the judges, hoping to impress them.
The teacher spoke ______ when explaining the importance of studying.
The puppy bounced ______ when it saw its owner coming home.
The actor ______ dropped to one knee during the play.
The student spoke ______ during the debate, making her point clear.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Comparative Adverbs)
1. worse  2. more quickly  3. more softly  4. later  5. more beautifully
Practice 2 (Quantifiers)
1. Neither  2. some  3. Both  4. a lot of  5. Each
Practice 3 (Reflexive Pronouns)
1. herself  2. ourselves  3. himself  4. itself  5. myself  6. themselves
Practice 4 (Reported Speech)
She said she would help me.
He said he was reading a book.
She asked if I liked pizza.
They said they were saving money for Eid.
Practice 5 (Connecting Words)
1. However  2. Firstly  3. Therefore  4. Finally  5. In addition
Practice 6 (Tone Awareness)
1. dramatically  2. firmly  3. eagerly  4. dramatically  5. firmly

"Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?"
"Can you give me more practice sentences for comparative adverbs?"
"Can you help me practise changing direct speech into reported speech?"
