paragraphs: Unit 5: Money Matters
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 5: Money Matters. Money is a part of everyday life, but understanding how it works is a skill that takes practice. Whether you are saving coins in a jar at home, choosing between two things you want at the market, or hearing adults talk about bills and budgets, money is always around you. In this unit, you will explore the world of financial literacy — the ability to understand and use money wisely.

You will read about spending and saving, think about the value of money, and discuss what it means to be responsible with the things we have. You will learn about comparative adverbs (words that compare how actions are done, such as *more quickly* or *faster*), quantifiers (words that tell us how much or how many, such as *both*, *neither*, *some*, and *many*), and reflexive pronouns (words like *myself*, *herself*, and *themselves* that show when someone does something to or for themselves). These grammar tools will help you write a strong opinion essay about pocket money and saving by the end of the unit.

Alongside this lesson, you will read the story "The Notice Board", set in a boarding school in southern Africa. The story follows a girl named Zanele who is new at school and must find the courage to speak up, adapt to challenges, and stand for fairness. The story explores themes of personal growth, emotional strength, and speaking up for what is right. It will help you think about responsibility, self-awareness, and how our choices shape who we become.

In many Somali and East African families, children learn about money from a young age. You might help count change at a shop, save for Eid, or hear family discussions about halal ways of earning and spending. Islam teaches us to be neither wasteful nor stingy, to give sadaqah (charity), and to be grateful for what we have. These are powerful financial principles. This unit will help you connect those values with the skills of budgeting, saving, and making thoughtful choices about money.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand texts about spending, saving, and financial literacy.
Use comparative adverbs correctly to compare actions (e.g. *more quickly*, *faster*, *better*).
Use quantifiers to talk about amounts (e.g. *both*, *neither*, *some*, *many*, *a lot of*).
Use reflexive pronouns correctly to show when someone does something to or for themselves (e.g. *myself*, *herself*, *themselves*).
Write a structured opinion essay about pocket money, saving, or spending wisely.
Use connecting words to link ideas in your writing (e.g. *firstly*, *however*, *therefore*, *finally*).
Speak clearly when presenting your opinion or discussing money habits.
Think critically about smart spending, the value of money, and the difference between needs and wants.
Understand and enjoy the story "The Notice Board" and explore themes of courage, fairness, and personal growth.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn the unit words a few at a time and say the example sentences aloud.
Work through the Grammar file. Focus especially on comparative adverbs, as they will appear throughout the unit.
Read the Story file, "The Notice Board", and answer the comprehension and discussion questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment to see what you have achieved.
Key Vocabulary

Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find the full word list with definitions and examples in your Vocabulary companion file. For now, read through these words and make sure you can say each one aloud.
Emotion and Behaviour Words
Growth and Change Words
Reading
Before you read

The passage below is about money, saving, and making good financial choices. It is written for young people your age who are starting to think about how money works. As you read, think about these questions:
What is the difference between a need and a want?
Why is saving money important, even when you are young?
How do families in your community talk about and use money?
Money, Markets, and Making Good Choices

Every morning in cities and towns across East Africa, markets come alive. Stalls are set up with colourful fabrics, fresh fruit, and bags of rice and sugar. Shopkeepers call out prices. Customers count their coins carefully. A woman buys enough flour for the week. A boy saves his last few shillings instead of buying sweets. A grandmother bargains firmly for a fair price on vegetables. Money moves from hand to hand, and each decision — big or small — tells a story about how people think about value.

But what is money, really? Money is a tool. It is not good or bad on its own. What matters is how we use it. Financial literacy means understanding how money works and learning to make smart choices with it. This includes knowing the difference between needs and wants. A need is something you must have to live safely and healthily, like food, clean water, shelter, and clothing. A want is something nice to have but not essential, like a new toy, a fancy phone case, or extra sweets after school.

One of the most crucial money skills is saving. Saving means putting aside part of your money for the future instead of spending it all at once. Even small amounts add up over time. If you save just a few coins each week, after several months you might have enough for something substantial — a book you have wanted, a gift for someone you love, or a contribution to a family goal. In Islam, we are taught to be balanced with money: not to waste it, not to hoard it selfishly, and to always share with those who have less. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that the best of people are those who are most beneficial to others. Giving sadaqah is not just generous — it is a way of purifying our wealth and strengthening our community.

A budget is a plan for how to use your money. Even young people can make a simple budget. Imagine you receive some pocket money each week. You could divide it into three parts: one part for saving, one part for spending on things you need or enjoy, and one part for giving to charity or helping your family. This simple plan helps you feel in control and teaches you to think productively about your resources. People who budget well are often more productive with their money than people who spend without thinking.

Another important skill is learning to compare prices. When you go to the market with your family, you might notice that one shop sells rice more cheaply than another, or that one brand of soap lasts longer than a cheaper one. Comparing carefully means you get better value. This is a form of critical thinking — the same skill you use in school when you adapt your ideas based on new information.

Money can sometimes cause stress, especially for families who do not have much. It is important to be sensitive to this. Not everyone has the same amount, and that is not something to feel uncomfortable about. What matters is being humble, grateful for what you have, and wise with whatever resources are available to you. A person who manages a meager income well can be far more successful than someone who wastes a large fortune.

Understanding money is a privilege and a responsibility. The earlier you start learning, the better prepared you will be. Whether you are saving for Eid, helping your family with shopping, or simply choosing between two treats at the corner shop, every choice you make is practice for the bigger decisions ahead.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage to find the evidence.
What happens every morning in markets across East Africa, according to the passage?
The passage says "Money is a tool." What does this mean?
What is the difference between a need and a want? Give one example of each from the text.
Why is saving important, even when you only have a small amount of money?
According to the passage, what three parts could you divide your pocket money into?
What does the passage say about comparing prices at the market?
Why does the passage say that a person with a meager income can be more successful than someone with a large fortune?
What Islamic teaching about money is mentioned in the passage? Explain it in your own words.
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the passage. Use the sentence around them to work out their meaning, then check with your Vocabulary file.
firmly (paragraph 1) — What does the grandmother do firmly? What does this word suggest about her?
crucial (paragraph 3) — "One of the most crucial money skills is saving." Why is this skill described as crucial?
substantial (paragraph 3) — What kind of thing might your savings become? What does substantial tell you about the amount?
sensitive (paragraph 6) — Why does the passage ask you to be sensitive about money?
meager (paragraph 6) — "A person who manages a meager income well..." What does meager tell you about the size of the income?
privilege (paragraph 7) — "Understanding money is a privilege and a responsibility." What does this mean?
Deeper Thinking
These questions ask you to think beyond what the passage says directly. There is no single right answer, but you should explain your thinking clearly.
The passage says that money "is not good or bad on its own." Do you agree? Can you think of times when money causes problems, and times when it helps?
The passage connects saving money with Islamic values. How do your family or community values shape the way you think about spending and saving?

The passage talks about the difference between needs and wants. Sometimes this is not easy to decide. Give an example of something that might be a need for one person but a want for another. Explain your thinking.
The passage says that "every choice you make is practice for the bigger decisions ahead." What bigger financial decisions might you face when you are older? How does practising now help?
Think about the grandmother bargaining at the market. Why is it important to know the fair price of things? What could happen if people did not compare prices?
Grammar Focus: Comparative Adverbs

In the reading passage, you met sentences that compared actions — for example, one shop sells rice *more cheaply* than another, or one person manages money *better* than another. These comparisons use comparative adverbs. Understanding comparative adverbs is one of the most important grammar skills in this unit, because they help you write clear, precise comparisons in your opinion essays and everyday conversations.
What are comparative adverbs?

An adverb is a word that tells us how, when, or where something happens. For example, *quickly*, *slowly*, *firmly*, and *eagerly* are all adverbs. A comparative adverb is the form we use when we want to compare how two actions are done.
For example:
"Zanele spoke more firmly than before." (We are comparing how she spoke at two different times.)
"He runs faster than his brother." (We are comparing the speed of two runners.)
"She adapted more quickly than I did." (We are comparing how quickly two people adapted.)
How to form comparative adverbs
The rule depends on the length of the adverb:
Rule 1: Short adverbs (one syllable) — add -er
fast → faster: "He runs faster than his sister."
hard → harder: "She works harder than anyone in the class."
late → later: "They arrived later than expected."
loud → louder: "The teacher spoke louder so everyone could hear."
Rule 2: Longer adverbs (two or more syllables) — add "more" before the adverb
quickly → more quickly: "She finished her homework more quickly than I did."
carefully → more carefully: "He counted the coins more carefully than before."
eagerly → more eagerly: "The children waited more eagerly for Eid than for any other holiday."
dramatically → more dramatically: "The weather changed more dramatically in the mountains."
firmly → more firmly: "She held the rope more firmly after it nearly slipped."
Rule 3: Irregular comparative adverbs
Some adverbs do not follow the regular rules. You need to learn these by heart:
well → better: "She sings better than her friend."
badly → worse: "He did worse on the second test than the first."
far → farther (or further): "We walked farther than we planned."
little → less: "He complained less after he understood the reason."
much → more: "She practised more than anyone else."
Always use "than"
When you compare two things using a comparative adverb, you almost always use the word than to connect them:
"She speaks English more confidently than she did last year."
"He saves money more wisely than his older brother."
"The market opens earlier than the shops on the high street."
A common mistake and how to avoid it
Mistake: Adding "-er" to a long adverb instead of using "more".
Wrong: "She runs quicklier than me." ✘
Right: "She runs more quickly than me." ✔
Mistake: Forgetting "than" in a comparison.
Wrong: "He works harder I do." ✘
Right: "He works harder than I do." ✔
Memory trick: If the adverb is a short word (one syllable), just add -er. If it is a longer word (two or more syllables), put more in front. Think of "more" as your helper for longer words.
Grammar Focus: Quantifiers

A quantifier is a word that tells us how much or how many of something there is. Quantifiers are useful whenever you need to talk about amounts, whether you are describing people, objects, or ideas. In this unit, you will use quantifiers when writing about money, budgets, and the choices people make.
Common quantifiers and how to use them
Here are the quantifiers you need to know:
both — used for exactly two things or people: "Both students saved their pocket money."
neither — used for two things to say not one of them: "Neither answer was negative."
some — a number of, but not all: "Some children prefer to spend rather than save."
many — a large number of: "Many families in our community give to charity regularly."
few — a small number of: "Only a few students forgot their homework."
a lot of — a large amount: "There are a lot of stalls at the market."
all — every one: "All the coins were counted carefully."
each — every individual one: "Each student received pocket money for the week."
every — all, without exception: "Every choice you make with money is practice."
Both vs. Neither
These two quantifiers are used specifically for groups of two:
"Both of the students had fabulous ideas." (Two students, and each one had ideas.)
"Neither of the two answers was correct." (Two answers, and not one of them was correct.)
Important: Both takes a plural verb ("Both students were ready"), but neither takes a singular verb ("Neither student was ready").
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "both" with a singular noun.
Wrong: "Both student had ideas." ✘
Right: "Both students had ideas." ✔
Mistake: Using "neither" with "not".
Wrong: "Neither of them did not come." ✘ (Double negative.)
Right: "Neither of them came." ✔
Grammar Focus: Reflexive Pronouns

A reflexive pronoun is a special pronoun we use when the person doing the action and the person receiving the action are the same. Reflexive pronouns always end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). They are important for clear, accurate English, and they appear often in the story "The Notice Board" and in everyday conversation.
The reflexive pronouns
Here is the complete list:
I → myself: "I taught myself how to save money."
you (one person) → yourself: "Did you prepare yourself for the test?"
he → himself: "He made the bed by himself."
she → herself: "She reminded herself to be patient."
it → itself: "The cat cleaned itself after playing."
we → ourselves: "We enjoyed ourselves at the Eid celebration."
you (more than one) → yourselves: "Did you all prepare yourselves for the trip?"
they → themselves: "They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Two uses of reflexive pronouns
Use 1: When the subject and object are the same person.
This is the main use. The subject does the action to themselves:
"She looked at herself in the mirror." (She looked at her own reflection.)
"I cut myself while cooking." (I accidentally cut my own hand.)
"They taught themselves to budget." (Nobody else taught them; they learned alone.)
Use 2: For emphasis (to stress that someone did something alone or without help).
"I baked the cake myself." (I did it without anyone helping me.)
"Zanele wrote her name on the board herself." (She did it personally, not someone else.)
"The children organised the event themselves." (No adult helped them.)
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using a reflexive pronoun when the subject and object are different people.
Wrong: "She told myself the news." ✘
Right: "She told me the news." ✔ (She and me are different people.)
Mistake: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Wrong: "He taught hisself to cook." ✘
Right: "He taught himself to cook." ✔
Memory trick: Think of reflexive pronouns as "self words." If the action bounces back to the same person who did it, like a ball bouncing off a wall, you need a reflexive pronoun.
Grammar Practice
Practice 1: Comparative adverbs — choose the correct form
Read each sentence and choose the correct comparative adverb. Write the full sentence in your notebook.
She solved the maths problem (more quickly / quicklier) than her classmate.
He speaks Arabic (more fluently / fluenter) than English.
The bus arrived (later / more lately) than we expected.
Zanele adapted to school life (more easily / easilier) than she thought she would.
He runs (faster / more fast) than anyone in his year group.
My sister saves money (more carefully / carefullier) than I do.
The weather changed (more dramatically / dramaticallier) in the afternoon.
She performed (better / more good) in the second test than the first.
Practice 2: Quantifiers — fill in the blank
Choose the correct quantifier from the options in brackets.
______ of the two brothers saved their pocket money. (Both / All)
______ student in the class received a pencil. (Every / Many)
______ of the answers on the quiz were correct. (Neither / A lot of)
There are ______ reasons to learn about money. (many / every)
______ children like to save, while others prefer to spend. (Some / Each)
She checked ______ item on her shopping list. (every / some)
______ of the two friends could attend the party. (Neither / Few)
There were ______ people at the market this morning. (a lot of / each)
Practice 3: Reflexive pronouns — complete the sentences
Fill in each blank with the correct reflexive pronoun.
She taught ______ how to make a budget.
We organised the charity sale by ______.
He accidentally hurt ______ while cutting the paper.
"I will do it ______," said Zanele.
The cat washed ______ after playing in the mud.
Did you all enjoy ______ at the school fair?
They prepared ______ for the big exam.
I reminded ______ to save part of my pocket money.
Practice 4: Mixed grammar — spot and fix the mistakes
Each sentence has one grammar error. Find it and rewrite the sentence correctly.
She runs more fastly than her brother. (Think: is "fastly" a real word?)
Both student agreed to share the prize. (Think: should this be singular or plural?)
He taught hisself to ride a bicycle. (Think: what is the correct reflexive pronoun?)
Neither of the girls were chosen for the team. (Think: does "neither" take a singular or plural verb?)
I saved money more better than last month. (Think: is "better" already comparative?)
She told myself the answer. (Think: are "she" and "I" the same person?)
Answers
Practice 1: 1. more quickly  2. more fluently  3. later  4. more easily  5. faster  6. more carefully  7. more dramatically  8. better
Practice 2: 1. Both  2. Every  3. Neither  4. many  5. Some  6. every  7. Neither  8. a lot of
Practice 3: 1. herself  2. ourselves  3. himself  4. myself  5. itself  6. yourselves  7. themselves  8. myself

Practice 4: 1. She runs faster than her brother. ("Fast" is already short; just add -er.)  2. Both students agreed to share the prize. ("Both" needs a plural noun.)  3. He taught himself to ride a bicycle. (Not "hisself".)  4. Neither of the girls was chosen for the team. ("Neither" takes a singular verb.)  5. I saved money better than last month. (Do not add "more" before an already irregular comparative.)  6. She told me the answer. ("She" and "I" are different people, so no reflexive pronoun.)
Writing Workshop: Opinion Essay on Pocket Money and Saving

An opinion essay is a piece of writing in which you share what you think about a topic and give reasons to support your view. In this unit, your topic is money — specifically, pocket money and saving. A good opinion essay does not just say "I think saving is good." It explains why you think so, gives examples, and uses connecting words to guide the reader through your argument.
The structure of an opinion essay
A well-organised opinion essay has four parts:
Introduction: State the topic and your opinion clearly. For example: "I believe that every young person should learn to save money, because saving teaches us patience and responsibility."
First reason with evidence: Give your strongest reason. Explain it with an example or detail. Use the connecting word Firstly to begin.
Second reason with evidence: Give another reason. Use In addition or Furthermore to connect it to the first.
Conclusion: Summarise your opinion and leave the reader with a strong final thought. Use In conclusion or Therefore to signal the end.
Useful language for opinion essays
Here are phrases you can use in your essay. Practise saying them aloud.
Introducing your opinion:
"I believe that..."
"In my opinion,..."
"I strongly think that..."
"From my point of view,..."
Giving reasons:
"Firstly, saving money helps you..."
"In addition, learning to budget teaches you..."
"Furthermore, when you save, you can..."
"Another reason is that..."
Giving examples:
"For example, in my family we save money for Eid every year."
"For instance, a student who saves a little each week can buy..."
"I know this because..."
Concluding:
"In conclusion, I believe that..."
"Therefore, it is clear that..."
"To sum up, saving money is..."
Model opinion essay
Read this example essay. Notice how it uses the four-part structure, connecting words, and grammar from this unit.
Your turn: Write your own opinion essay
Choose one of these topics and write an opinion essay of 150–200 words using the four-part structure:
"Should children receive pocket money?"
"Is it better to save money or spend it on things you enjoy?"
"Why is it important to learn about money when you are young?"
Before you write, plan:
Choose your topic and decide your opinion. Are you for or against? Why?
Plan your four paragraphs. Write one sentence for each paragraph before you start the full essay.
Use at least two comparative adverbs (e.g. "Saving teaches you to manage money more wisely than spending does").
Use at least two quantifiers (e.g. "Many young people do not learn about saving", "Both saving and giving are important").
Use at least one reflexive pronoun (e.g. "I taught myself to budget").
Use connecting words to link your paragraphs: *Firstly*, *In addition*, *Furthermore*, *In conclusion*, *Therefore*.
Read your essay aloud and check: Does it make sense? Would a reader agree with your argument?
Writing Extension: A family budget plan
Imagine your family has been given a gift of money to spend wisely over one month. Create a simple budget plan that divides the money into categories.
Your budget should include:
Needs: food, transport, school supplies, bills.
Wants: entertainment, treats, new clothes that are not essential.
Savings: money set aside for the future.
Charity: money given to help others (sadaqah).

Write a short paragraph (4–6 sentences) explaining why you divided the money the way you did. Use at least one comparative adverb ("We spent more carefully on wants than on needs") and one quantifier ("Most of the money went to needs").
Listening & Speaking
Listening: A conversation about saving
Ask your AI tutor to read this conversation aloud, or read it with a partner. Then answer the questions below.
Amina: Yusuf, do you get pocket money from your parents?
Yusuf: Yes, I get a small amount each week. But I do not spend it all. I save most of it in a tin under my bed.
Amina: Really? I find it hard to save. I usually spend mine at the school tuck shop. Sometimes I buy snacks I do not even need!
Yusuf: I used to do that too. But my grandmother told me: "Teach yourself to wait. If you still want it after three days, then buy it." That advice changed how I think about money.
Amina: That is clever! So you save more patiently than before?
Yusuf: Yes, much more patiently. Last Eid, I had saved enough to buy gifts for both my parents. I felt really proud of myself.
Amina: That is lovely. I want to start saving more carefully too. Maybe I will make myself a budget, like we learned in class.
Yusuf: You should! Even a few coins a week add up. Neither of us is too young to learn about money.
Listening questions
How much pocket money does Yusuf get?
What does Yusuf do with his pocket money instead of spending it all?
What does Amina usually spend her money on?
What advice did Yusuf's grandmother give him?
What did Yusuf buy with his savings last Eid?
Find two comparative adverbs in the conversation. What actions are being compared?
Find one reflexive pronoun in the conversation. Who is the pronoun referring to?
Find one quantifier in the conversation and explain what it tells you about the amount.
Speaking 1: Present your opinion essay
Once you have written your opinion essay from the Writing Workshop, practise presenting it aloud. Imagine you are giving a speech to your class about why saving money matters.
Stand or sit up straight. Speak clearly and at a steady pace.
Do not just read your essay word for word. Look up and speak naturally.
Emphasise your opinion words: say "I strongly believe" with confidence.
Use a clear voice for your connecting words: "Firstly... In addition... In conclusion..."
End with your conclusion and a strong closing sentence.
Record yourself if you can. Listen back and ask: Did I sound convincing? Would the listener agree with my argument?
Speaking 2: Debate — Should children get pocket money?
With a partner, your AI tutor, or a family member, have a short debate. One person argues that children should receive pocket money, and the other argues that they should not. Each speaker should:
Give at least two reasons to support their opinion.
Use comparative adverbs ("Children who receive pocket money learn more quickly about responsibility").
Use quantifiers ("Many children waste their pocket money", "Both sides have good points").
Use reflexive pronouns where natural ("Children can teach themselves to budget").
Listen to the other person and respond to their points politely.
Speaking 3: Role-play — At the market
Imagine you are at a local market with a limited amount of money. Work with your AI tutor or a partner. One person is the shopper and the other is the stallholder.
The shopper needs to buy items for the family but does not have enough money for everything. They must decide what to buy and what to leave. Use these prompts:
"How much does the rice cost?" / "How much is the flour?"
"Is there anything cheaper?" (Use a comparative adverb: "Do you have anything less expensive?")
"I need to buy food for both my brothers and myself."
"I will save the rest for next week because saving is more important than spending on snacks."
"Thank you. I chose more carefully this time."
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you at any time. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I do not understand comparative adverbs. Can you explain them a different way?"
Practise grammar: "Can you give me ten sentences where I choose the correct comparative form?"
Get writing feedback: "Can you read my opinion essay and check my grammar and vocabulary?"
Prepare for speaking: "Can you listen to me present my essay and tell me how to improve?"
Review the story: "Can you ask me questions about 'The Notice Board' to check I understood it?"
Practise vocabulary: "Can you test me on the Unit 5 words?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Look at each statement below. Tick "Yes" if you can do it confidently, or "Not yet" if you need more practice. For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for extra help.
I can read a passage about money and financial literacy and answer comprehension questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use comparative adverbs correctly to compare actions (e.g. *more quickly*, *faster*, *better*).   Yes □   Not yet □

Getting Ready for Unit 6: People and Work
Make sure you know all the Unit 5 words on your Word Wall, especially the grammar terms (comparative adverb, quantifier, reflexive pronoun).
In Unit 6 you will explore jobs, careers, and the world of work. Think about what jobs people do in your community and what skills they need.
You will learn about compound nouns, reported speech, and the present continuous for future arrangements.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 6? Can you get me ready?"

Well done for completing Unit 5. Money matters touch every part of our lives. Now you have the skills to think critically about spending and saving, to compare actions clearly using comparative adverbs, and to express your opinions with confidence. Keep practising, keep saving, and keep making good choices.

✍️  Model Essay: "Why Every Young Person Should Learn to Save"
I believe that every young person should learn to save money. Saving is not just about collecting coins in a jar. It is about learning to think carefully about the future and making responsible choices.
Firstly, saving teaches patience. When you save for something you really want, you learn that good things take time. For example, I saved my pocket money for three months to buy a book I had been wanting. The wait made the book feel even more special. I felt proud of myself because I had earned it through my own effort.
In addition, saving helps families. When both children and adults in a family save, they can work towards shared goals more effectively. Neither wasteful spending nor selfish hoarding helps a family grow. In my community, many families save together for Eid celebrations, and this brings everyone closer.
In conclusion, saving money is a crucial skill that every young person should develop. It teaches us patience, helps our families, and prepares us for the bigger financial decisions we will face as adults. Even a small amount saved regularly can make a substantial difference over time.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 5: Money Matters
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "The Notice Board"
The Notice Board
The first day at Mbali Academy was not what Zanele expected.

She had been eagerly waiting to move into the school dormitory for weeks. Her mother said it would help her become more independent. Her father, who rarely shared emotions, simply hugged her and said, "Remember who you are."
Zanele was not sure what that meant. But she promised herself she would try.

Mbali Academy was a massive, stone-walled school perched on a green ridge in southern Africa. It was known for its fabulous science program and its strict rules. Students wore uniforms and ate in long rows. The dorms were clean but cold.
Her roommate was a girl named Kemi, who talked firmly and fast.
"Don't touch my shelf," Kemi said the moment Zanele entered.
Zanele smiled shyly. "I won't."
She unpacked her meager belongings — two books, a tiny photo frame, and a borrowed sweater. Compared to Kemi's colourful bedding and shiny decorations, Zanele's side looked bare.
At dinner, Zanele tried to talk to others. But most were already in their own friend groups. She felt a little uncomfortable, like a chair with one short leg.
"Just give it time," she told herself.
Later that week, a teacher announced the new student council would be formed. "One student will be chosen from each dorm group. They will help represent your voices and ideas."
Zanele's ears perked up. Maybe this was her chance to be part of something.
The next morning, Zanele saw a poster on the school notice board.
"Nominate a student who shows leadership, kindness, and courage."
Kemi and her friends giggled behind her.
"We already know who will win," Kemi said. "Nomusa. Everyone likes her."
"She's nice," said someone.
"And confident," added another.
Zanele looked at the names scribbled on the board. All were from the same friend group. None were new students. None were like her.
That night, she asked herself a question: Should I try anyway?
"I don't belong," she whispered, frustrated.
But something inside her — small but substantial — told her to speak.
She wrote her name in shaky handwriting.
The next day, whispers started.
"Who is that girl?" someone asked.
"Zanele," another replied. "Isn't she the one with the second-hand sweater?"
Kemi rolled her eyes. "That's a questionable choice."
Zanele felt heat in her chest. She thought about removing her name. But a teacher passed by and said, "I saw your name. Good for you."
Zanele smiled, just a little.
The council speeches were set for Friday. Students would gather in the auditorium. Each candidate had to give a short talk.
Zanele felt like her stomach had flipped. She barely slept Thursday night.
"You don't have to do this," Kemi said in the morning. "It's not too late to back out."
Zanele looked at her reflection in the mirror. "I didn't come here to hide," she said quietly.
She remembered what her father had said.
When it was her turn to speak, Zanele walked slowly to the front. Her paper trembled in her hands.
She breathed in.
"My name is Zanele. I'm new here, and I know I don't have a big group of friends yet. But I believe kindness should be a rule, not a suggestion."
She paused.
"Sometimes rules feel unavoidable, but that doesn't make them right. Some are even unconstitutional — they go against fairness."
The room was silent.
"I've felt frustrated and small here. But I've also learned that even small voices can grow. I will do my best to be fair, hospitable, and honest."
She stepped back. Her knees felt wobbly.
The applause was quiet — but real.
Later, someone tapped her shoulder. A younger girl from another dorm.
"Thank you," the girl said. "I felt sensitive when I arrived too. You made me feel seen."
Zanele smiled wide. It was not a massive moment, but it felt crucial.
Voting happened the next day. When the results were posted, Zanele was not the winner. Nomusa had the most votes.
But below her name, another line had been added:
"Zanele: assistant rep for dorm 2."
Kemi blinked. "That's new."
Zanele nodded. "Maybe the rules adapted."
In the weeks that followed, she helped organise weekend activities, sorted complaints, and reminded teachers when supplies were unavailable. She took the job seriously and learned quickly.
She noticed that when she spoke firmly, people listened.
When she spoke aggressively, they pushed back.
When she spoke dramatically, they laughed.
She started choosing her tone carefully.
One day, a rule was posted on the notice board: "Only students with full uniforms may attend weekend outings."
Several students couldn't afford new shoes. Zanele raised her hand in the council meeting.
"This rule," she said, "feels unfortunate and unfair. Some students try their best but don't have the means."
"It's about order," said a teacher.
"But order should never come before compassion," Zanele replied.
Another rep whispered, "That rule sounds… unconstitutional."
They voted. The rule was changed.
Zanele felt her heart flutter. Change could be slow — but it was real.
One evening, she and Kemi sat on their beds, both doing homework.
"You've changed," Kemi said. "You used to be so quiet."
"I had to adapt," Zanele replied. "And you?"
Kemi shrugged. "I'm learning too. Being right all the time doesn't mean being respected."
They shared a laugh. It was small — but felt honest.
During essay week, Zanele wrote a piece called "Growing From the Middle."
She used words like productive, symbiotic, and fragile.
"A school is like a tree," she wrote.

"It needs deep roots of respect, branches of growth, and leaves that fall and come again. Every person here, whether popular or unpopular, makes it grow. Sometimes a fragile moment becomes the start of strength."
Her teacher wrote at the bottom:
"Your voice is becoming more noticeable."
At the end of the term, Zanele called home.
"I didn't win," she told her father.
"But you changed something," he said. "That's better than winning."
She smiled. "I guess I remembered who I was after all."
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension questions
What kind of school is Mbali Academy? Describe it in two or three sentences.
How did Zanele feel when she first arrived at the school? What details tell you this?
What was the student council, and why did Zanele want to be part of it?
How did other students react when Zanele put her name on the notice board?
What did Zanele say in her council speech that made the room go silent?
Why did Zanele not win the election? What role did she get instead?
What unfair rule was posted on the notice board later in the story?
How did Zanele convince the council to change the rule?
What did Zanele's father mean when he said "Remember who you are"?
What did Zanele learn about the way she spoke? How did her tone affect people?
Vocabulary in the story
Find these words in the story. For each one, write the sentence it appears in and explain what it means in that context.
eagerly
massive
meager
uncomfortable
frustrated
substantial
questionable
hospitable
unavoidable
unconstitutional
adapt
productive
symbiotic
fragile
Think and discuss
These questions ask you to think more deeply about the story. There is no single right answer. Explain your thinking carefully.
Zanele says "kindness should be a rule, not a suggestion." What does she mean by this? Do you agree?
Kemi says at the end: "Being right all the time doesn't mean being respected." What lesson has Kemi learned? How has her character changed?
Zanele describes her school as like a tree. What does this comparison (metaphor) mean? Can you think of another comparison that describes a school?
The story shows that Zanele lost the election but still made a difference. What does this tell us about success? Is winning always the most important thing?
How does the story connect to the theme of Money Matters? Think about value, fairness, what people "can't afford," and the idea that worth is not always about money.
Grammar in the story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find examples of each:
Reflexive pronouns: Find at least two sentences where a character uses a reflexive pronoun (e.g. "she told herself..."). Write them down.
Comparative structures: Zanele's voice becomes "more noticeable." Find another place in the story where two things are compared.
Quantifiers: Find a sentence that uses a quantifier like "both", "neither", or "some".
Tone and adverbs: The story says Zanele spoke "firmly", "aggressively", and "dramatically." How did each tone affect the people around her?
Now You Try: Writing
Writing task 1: A letter from Zanele
Imagine you are Zanele. Write a letter home to your family after your first month at Mbali Academy. Include:
How you felt when you arrived (use at least one reflexive pronoun).
What happened with the student council election.
What you learned about speaking up and about yourself.
At least one comparative adverb (e.g. "I speak more confidently now than when I arrived").
At least one quantifier (e.g. "Some students were unkind at first, but many have become friendly").
Writing task 2: An opinion paragraph
Write a short paragraph (6–8 sentences) giving your opinion on this question:
"Should schools have student councils? Why or why not?"
Use connecting words (Firstly, In addition, In conclusion) and at least two vocabulary words from this unit.

Group 3: Fairness and Rights
Word-building note: the prefix "un-"
Notice that many words in this group start with the prefix un-. This prefix means "not" or "the opposite of." When you see un- at the start of a word, it usually reverses the meaning:
available → unavailable (not available)
fortunate → unfortunate (not fortunate)
popular → unpopular (not popular)
constitutional → unconstitutional (not constitutional)
avoidable → unavoidable (not avoidable)
Knowing this prefix helps you understand many new words, even if you have never seen them before!

paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 6: People and Work
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Compound Nouns

This grammar file focuses on Compound Nouns — the main grammar target for Unit 6. You will also find a section on Reported Speech, which is the second grammar skill for this unit. Read the explanations, study the examples, complete the exercises, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
What Are Compound Nouns?

A compound noun is a noun made up of two or more words that come together to create a new word with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns is important because they help us express complex ideas in a simple way. For example, instead of saying “the fees for tuition,” we can say tuition fees. Instead of saying “the room where the court meets,” we can say courtroom. Compound nouns make our language clearer, more precise, and more efficient.
Why Do Compound Nouns Matter?
Compound nouns are everywhere in English, especially in the language of work, law, science, and daily life. You will find them in:
News reports: police officer, crime scene, press conference, news headline
Schools: school uniform, classroom, lesson plan, text book, head teacher
Law and government: legal penalties, courtroom, law court, human rights
Health: test results, blood pressure, check-up, health worker
Work and careers: job interview, business owner, fire station, bus driver
The more compound nouns you know, the more precisely you can describe the world around you.
The Three Forms of Compound Nouns
1. Closed Form (One Word)
In the closed form, the two words are joined together as a single word with no space and no hyphen.
toothbrush = tooth + brush
football = foot + ball
classroom = class + room
courtroom = court + room
headmaster = head + master
sunlight = sun + light
newspaper = news + paper
bedroom = bed + room
Closed compound nouns are the easiest to recognise because they look like a single word. You can often find them in a dictionary as one entry.
2. Hyphenated Form
In the hyphenated form, the words are connected with a hyphen (-). Hyphens are used when the compound might be confusing without them or when it follows a specific English spelling rule.
mother-in-law = mother + in + law
well-being = well + being
check-up = check + up
passer-by = passer + by
six-year-old = six + year + old
self-control = self + control
Hyphenated compound nouns are less common than the other two forms, but you should know how to read and write them correctly.
3. Open Form (Two Separate Words)
In the open form, the words are written separately but are understood as a single unit of meaning.
tuition fees = tuition + fees
legal penalties = legal + penalties
bus stop = bus + stop
swimming pool = swimming + pool
ice cream = ice + cream
post office = post + office
fire station = fire + station
test results = test + results
food vendor = food + vendor
script writing = script + writing
migration path = migration + path
police officer = police + officer

Open compound nouns can be tricky because the words look separate, but together they mean something specific. A swimming pool is not just any pool that happens to be swimming — it is a specific type of pool designed for swimming.
How to Form Compound Nouns
To form a compound noun, follow these steps:
Identify the two nouns (or noun + adjective/verb). Think about what you want to name. For example, you want to talk about fees related to tuition.
Put the describing word first. The first word usually tells us what kind or what type. In tuition fees, tuition tells us what kind of fees.

Check the form. Look in a dictionary or ask your AI tutor whether the compound is closed, hyphenated, or open. There is no simple rule for this — you learn the correct form through reading and practice.
Use it in a sentence. Make sure the compound noun fits naturally. Example: My parents saved for years to pay the tuition fees.
Common Patterns
Here are some common ways compound nouns are built:
Noun + noun: toothbrush, courtroom, bus stop, football, sunlight
Adjective + noun: greenhouse, blackboard, full moon, high court
Verb + noun: swimming pool, driving licence, washing machine
Noun + verb: sunrise, rainfall, haircut
Verb + preposition: check-up, break-in, workout
Compound Nouns in the World of Work
Here are compound nouns that relate specifically to jobs, careers, and the working world. These are useful for this unit and for your writing tasks.
Reported Speech — Review and Practice
Reported speech is the second grammar focus for this unit. Here is a quick review of the key rules. For a full explanation, see the Grammar Focus section in your Lesson file.
Quick Reference: Tense Shifts
Quick Reference: Time and Place Changes

well-being — hyphenated

Exercise 2: Build Compound Nouns
fire station — The fire station is on the next street.
headmaster — The headmaster welcomed the new students.
script writing — She wants to study script writing at university.
legal penalties — There are legal penalties for fraud.
ice cream — We bought ice cream after school.
Exercise 3: Fill in the Blanks
tuition fees
check-up
test results
food vendor
courtroom
migration path
Exercise 4: Direct to Reported Speech
The doctor said that he needed to see the test results.
Mama Jalia said that she had never given her consent.
The teacher said that they were visiting the courthouse that day.
Yusuf said that he would write the script the next day.
The judge said that the evidence had been incomplete.
Hawa said that she could help me with my legal problem.
The student asked what a compound noun was.
Imani asked if the officer had had her consent.
Exercise 7: Spot the Errors
The tuition fees are very expensive this year. (Use the compound noun form.)
She said that she was working at the hospital. (Shift present to past continuous.)
He told me that he would come the next day. (‘Told’ needs a person; ‘tomorrow’ changes.)
The swimming pool was closed for cleaning. (Open form, no hyphen.)
She asked where I worked. (Statement order in reported questions; no ‘did.’)

💡  Top Tip
When you are not sure whether a compound noun is one word, two words, or hyphenated, look it up in a dictionary. English spelling is not always consistent, and even native speakers sometimes need to check!
She saved money to pay her tuition fees.
There are legal penalties for driving too fast.
the room where a court meets

paragraphs: Unit 6: People and Work
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit the focus is on people and work. You will explore the many different jobs and professions that keep communities running, from physicians and lawyers to food vendors and commentators. You will learn how people choose their careers, what skills different jobs require, and how the world of work connects to ideas of fairness, justice, and community responsibility.

Along the way, you will strengthen your grammar by learning about compound nouns, the combinations of two or more words that create a single noun with a specific meaning. You will also practise reported speech, which allows you to tell someone what another person said without using their exact words. Both of these grammar skills are essential for clear, mature writing and speaking.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Identify and describe a range of professions and career paths, explaining the skills and training each one requires
Explain how personal interests and strengths can guide career choices
Read a passage about careers and jobs and answer comprehension questions at different thinking levels
Recognise and form compound nouns in their three forms: closed (e.g. toothbrush), hyphenated (e.g. mother-in-law), and open (e.g. tuition fees)
Change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words
Use reported speech accurately in both writing and conversation
Use the present continuous to describe future arrangements (e.g. She is starting her new job on Monday)
Identify profession suffixes such as -ist, -ian, -or, and -er and use them to build job titles
Plan and write a job advertisement or a career presentation using persuasive and informative language
Take part in speaking and listening activities including a career interview role-play and a job description guessing game
Evaluate your own learning by completing a set of self-assessment statements
Key Vocabulary

Before you begin reading, study the words below. These words will appear throughout the unit. Make sure you understand each one so that you can follow the texts and use the words in your own writing. You will find the complete list with practice activities in your Vocabulary file.

You will also use: script, text, caption, figures, criticism, ammunition, integration, segregation, frustration, tuition, migration, commodity, centuries, allies, presence, vacuole, heredity, item, refuse. See your Vocabulary file for the complete list.
Reading: Careers That Build Communities

Read the following passage carefully. It describes several different careers and explains how each one helps to build and protect a community. As you read, think about the skills each profession requires and how compound nouns are used to describe parts of the working world.
The People Behind the Work

Every morning in the East African city of Hargeisa, hundreds of people set off to work. Some carry briefcases, some carry toolboxes, and some carry nothing but their knowledge and determination. Each person has a profession, a role that helps keep the community running smoothly. Understanding these roles can help you start thinking about your own future career.

At the local hospital, a physician named Dr Khalid begins his shift at seven o’clock. His profession requires years of study. He completed medical school, passed difficult exams, and paid tuition fees that his family saved for over many years. Now he treats patients, reads test results, and gives careful observation reports to his team. Being a physician demands patience, scientific knowledge, and the ability to communicate clearly with people who are worried or in pain. Dr Khalid always says that the most important quality in medicine is the wisdom to listen before you speak.

Across the road from the hospital, a young woman named Hawa works as a legal advisor at a small law office. Her job involves studying legal documents, understanding the country’s laws, and giving people advice about their rights. She helps families settle disputes, assists business owners with contracts, and sometimes appears in courtrooms to defend her clients. Hawa knows that legal penalties can be serious, so she works carefully and pays attention to every detail. She often reminds her younger siblings that a career in law requires strong reading skills, clear thinking, and a deep sense of fairness.

Not far away, in the busy Waheen market, a food vendor named Mama Nura runs a popular stall. She has been selling freshly made sambusas and canjeero for over twenty years. Although she did not attend a university, Mama Nura is one of the most efficient workers in the market. She wakes before dawn to prepare her ingredients, keeps her cooking area spotlessly clean to avoid any contaminated food, and manages her money carefully. Her business skills are the result of decades of experience and wisdom passed down through her family. Mama Nura proves that a valuable profession does not always require a formal qualification but always requires dedication and hard work.

At the local radio station, a commentator named Yusuf records his evening programme. He writes a script for each broadcast, choosing his words carefully so that listeners can understand the day’s events. He reads figures and facts aloud, adds his own commentary, and invites guests to share their views. A commentator must be well-informed, confident, and able to explain complicated ideas in simple language. Yusuf also writes captions for the station’s social media pages, making sure every post is accurate and clear.

These four people have very different jobs, yet they all share important qualities: responsibility, dedication, and a desire to serve their community. Whether you dream of becoming a historian, an inventor, a teacher, or something entirely different, the key is to discover what you love, develop the skills you need, and never stop learning. Your profession is not just what you do for money — it is how you contribute to the world around you.
Understanding the Text

Answer the following questions about the passage you have just read. The questions are arranged in three levels. Level 1 questions check that you understood the basic facts. Level 2 questions ask you to think more deeply. Level 3 questions challenge you to analyse, evaluate, or create.
Level 1 — Finding Information
What city is the passage set in?
What is Dr Khalid’s profession?
What does Hawa do at the law office? Name two of her tasks.
How long has Mama Nura been selling food in the market?
What does Yusuf do at the radio station?
According to Dr Khalid, what is the most important quality in medicine?
Level 2 — Thinking Deeper

The passage says that Mama Nura’s profession “does not always require a formal qualification but always requires dedication and hard work.” Do you agree with this statement? Use evidence from the passage to explain your answer.
Why does the passage describe four different professions instead of just one? What effect does this have on the reader?
Find the compound noun tuition fees in the passage. Explain what it means and why it is important for Dr Khalid’s story.
How does Hawa’s career connect to the idea of fairness? Use your own words to explain.
Level 3 — Analysing and Evaluating
If you could interview one of the four people in the passage, who would you choose and what three questions would you ask them? Explain your choices.

The passage ends by saying that a profession “is how you contribute to the world around you.” Think of a profession that is not mentioned in the passage. Write a short paragraph explaining how that profession contributes to the community.
Compare two of the professions described in the passage. What skills do they share, and how are they different? Present your comparison in a table with two columns.
Text Feature Analysis
Find at least three compound nouns used in the passage. Write them down and explain what each one means.
The passage uses a topic sentence at the start of each paragraph. Choose one paragraph and explain how the topic sentence introduces the main idea.
Look at the final paragraph. Why do you think the writer chose to end the passage with advice to the reader? How does this affect the tone of the passage?
Grammar Focus: Compound Nouns and Reported Speech

In this section you will study two important grammar topics that appear throughout this unit. Compound nouns help you name things precisely by combining words, and reported speech helps you share what other people have said. Mastering both of these will make your writing more detailed, more accurate, and more grown-up.
Part A: Compound Nouns

A compound noun is a noun made of two or more words that join together to create a single noun with its own specific meaning. Instead of saying “the fees for tuition,” we can simply say tuition fees. Instead of saying “the path that birds follow when they move to a new place,” we can say migration path. Compound nouns make our language clearer and more efficient.
Compound nouns appear in three different forms:

Closed form: The two words are written as one word with no space. Examples include toothbrush, football, classroom, headmaster, and courtroom. These are the easiest to recognise because they look like a single word.

Hyphenated form: The two words are connected by a hyphen. Examples include mother-in-law, passer-by, well-being, and check-up. Hyphens are used when the compound might be confusing without them or when it follows a specific English spelling rule.

Open form: The two words are written separately but are understood as a single unit of meaning. Examples include tuition fees, legal penalties, swimming pool, bus stop, ice cream, and post office. Open compound nouns can be tricky because the two words look separate, but together they mean something specific.

To form a compound noun, think about which two words you are combining. The first word usually describes or modifies the second word. In tuition fees, the word tuition tells us what kind of fees. In migration path, the word migration tells us what kind of path. In courtroom, the word court tells us what kind of room.
Here are more examples of compound nouns that relate to people and work:
job interview — an interview for a job
police officer — an officer who works in the police
fire station — a station for firefighters
business owner — a person who owns a business
school uniform — a uniform worn at school
test results — the results of a test
food vendor — a vendor who sells food
script writing — the activity of writing scripts
Part B: Reported Speech

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is a way of telling someone what another person said without using their exact words. We use reported speech every day. For example, instead of saying, “My teacher said, ‘Read chapter three,’” you might say, “My teacher told us to read chapter three.” The meaning is the same, but you are reporting what was said rather than quoting it directly.
To change direct speech into reported speech, follow these steps:
Step 1 — Remove the quotation marks. Direct speech uses quotation marks around the exact words. Reported speech does not.
Step 2 — Change the pronouns. The pronouns must match the person who is reporting. Direct: “I love my job,” said Dr Khalid. Reported: Dr Khalid said that he loved his job.

Step 3 — Shift the tense back by one step. This is the most important change. Present simple becomes past simple. Present continuous becomes past continuous. Past simple becomes past perfect. “Will” becomes “would.” Direct: “I work at the hospital.” Reported: He said that he worked at the hospital. Direct: “I am writing a script.” Reported: He said that he was writing a script. Direct: “I will finish by Friday.” Reported: He said that he would finish by Friday.

Step 4 — Change time and place words. Some words change to fit the new context. “Today” becomes “that day.” “Tomorrow” becomes “the next day.” “Here” becomes “there.” “This” becomes “that.” “Now” becomes “then.”

Step 5 — Use “said” or “told.” Use “said” when you do not mention the listener. Use “told” when you name who was spoken to. He said that he loved his job. He told me that he loved his job. You can add the word “that” after “said” or “told,” but it is optional.

Reporting questions: When reporting a question, the word order changes to statement order and the question mark disappears. Direct: “Where do you work?” Reported: She asked where I worked. For yes/no questions, use if or whether. Direct: “Do you like your job?” Reported: She asked if I liked my job.

Reporting commands and requests: Use “told” + person + to + base verb. Direct: “Close the door!” Reported: She told him to close the door. For negative commands, add “not”: She told him not to open the window.
Grammar Practice
Complete the exercises below. Each one focuses on a different aspect of the grammar you have just studied. Write your answers in your exercise book, then check them against the answer keys provided.
Exercise 1 — Identify the Compound Nouns
Read each sentence and find the compound noun. Write it down and say whether it is closed, hyphenated, or open form.
My parents are worried about my tuition fees this year.
The headmaster called a meeting after school.
We watched the football match on television.
Mama Nura keeps her food stall very clean.
The check-up at the hospital went well.
The courtroom was full of people waiting for the verdict.
She wants to learn about script writing for films.
A police officer helped us find our way home.
Answer Key — Exercise 1
tuition fees (open)
headmaster (closed)
football (closed)
food stall (open)
check-up (hyphenated)
courtroom (closed)
script writing (open)
police officer (open)
Exercise 2 — Build Compound Nouns
Match a word from Column A with a word from Column B to form a compound noun. Write the compound noun and use it in a sentence.
Column A: bus, tooth, legal, swimming, migration
Column B: path, stop, brush, pool, penalties
Answer Key — Exercise 2
bus stop — We waited at the bus stop for twenty minutes.
toothbrush — I forgot to pack my toothbrush for the trip.
legal penalties — There are legal penalties for breaking the law.
swimming pool — The children played in the swimming pool all afternoon.
migration path — The birds follow the same migration path every year.
Exercise 3 — Change to Reported Speech
Rewrite each sentence in reported speech. Remember to shift the tense, change the pronouns, and adjust time words where needed.
Dr Khalid said, “I treat patients every day.”
Hawa said, “I am studying legal documents this week.”
Mama Nura said, “I will prepare the sambusas early tomorrow.”
Yusuf said, “I wrote the script yesterday.”
The teacher said, “We are going on a field trip today.”
Answer Key — Exercise 3
Dr Khalid said that he treated patients every day.
Hawa said that she was studying legal documents that week.
Mama Nura said that she would prepare the sambusas early the next day.
Yusuf said that he had written the script the day before.
The teacher said that they were going on a field trip that day.
Exercise 4 — Report the Questions
Change each direct question into reported speech:
The student asked, “What is a compound noun?”
Imani asked, “Was food served that day?”
The lawyer asked, “Did the officer have her consent?”
My friend asked, “Where do you want to work when you grow up?”
Answer Key — Exercise 4
The student asked what a compound noun was.
Imani asked if food had been served that day.
The lawyer asked if the officer had had her consent.
My friend asked where I wanted to work when I grew up.
Exercise 5 — Spot the Mistake
Each sentence below has a mistake in either the compound noun or the reported speech. Rewrite it correctly and explain the error.
She said that she works at the hospital. (Hint: tense shift needed.)
He told that he was tired. (Hint: something is missing after “told.”)
The fees of tuition are very expensive. (Hint: compound noun form.)
She said, “I finished the report yesterday.” He said she finished the report yesterday. (Hint: tense and time word.)
Answer Key — Exercise 5
She said that she worked at the hospital. (Present becomes past.)
He told me that he was tired. (‘Told’ needs a person after it.)
The tuition fees are very expensive. (Use the compound noun form.)
He said she had finished the report the day before. (Past simple becomes past perfect; ‘yesterday’ becomes ‘the day before.’)
Exercise 6 — Write Your Own

Write five sentences about jobs and careers. In each sentence, use at least one compound noun and at least one example of reported speech. Underline the compound nouns and circle the reported speech. Here is an example:
Example: The bus driver told me that the bus stop had been moved to the other side of the road.
Writing Workshop: Job Advertisement and Career Presentation

Now it is time to put everything you have learned into practice. In this workshop you will choose one of two writing tasks. Both tasks ask you to use the vocabulary, compound nouns, and persuasive language you have been studying. Choose the task that interests you most.
Option A — Write a Job Advertisement

Imagine you are the manager of a business and you need to hire a new worker. Write a job advertisement that describes the role and encourages the right person to apply. Your advertisement should be between 150 and 200 words.
Your advertisement should include:
A clear job title (use a compound noun if possible, such as project manager, food inspector, or radio presenter)
A short description of the company or organisation
A list of responsibilities (what the person will do each day)
A list of requirements (what qualifications, skills, or experience the person needs)
Information about the salary, working hours, or other benefits
A final sentence that encourages people to apply, using persuasive language
Here is a model to guide you:
Option B — Prepare a Career Presentation

Choose a profession that interests you. It can be any job: doctor, teacher, engineer, artist, farmer, pilot, journalist, or anything else. Prepare a short presentation of about 150 to 200 words that you could give to your class. Your presentation should teach your audience about the profession and explain why you find it interesting.
Your presentation should include:
An introduction that names the profession and explains why you chose it
A description of what a person in this profession does every day
The skills and qualifications needed for the job
At least one compound noun related to the profession (e.g. flight simulator, lesson plan, operating theatre)
At least one sentence using reported speech (e.g. My uncle told me that being a pilot requires years of training.)
A conclusion that explains how this profession helps the community
Writing Checklist
Before you share your work, check it against this list:
My writing has a clear title and is well organised into paragraphs.
I have used at least three compound nouns correctly.
I have included at least one example of reported speech.
My language is clear and persuasive — it makes the reader interested in the job.
I have used correct spelling, punctuation, and grammar throughout.
My writing is the right length (150 to 200 words).
I have used vocabulary from this unit where it fits naturally.
Peer Feedback
Exchange your writing with a partner. Read their work carefully and give feedback using these questions:
Is the job or profession clearly described? Could you picture what the person does?
Can you find at least two compound nouns? Write them down.
Is there an example of reported speech? Is it formed correctly?
What is the strongest part of the writing? Tell your partner what impressed you.
Is there anything that could be improved? Offer one specific suggestion.
After receiving feedback, revise your work. Good writers always improve their first drafts. Even professional authors rewrite their work several times before publishing.
Listening and Speaking Activities

Good communicators are strong in both speaking and listening. The activities below give you opportunities to practise these skills while exploring the world of work. You can do these activities with a family member, a classmate, or your AI tutor.
Activity 1 — Career Interview Role-Play

In this activity, one person plays the role of a worker and the other plays the role of a journalist. The journalist interviews the worker about their profession. Here is how to play. First, the worker chooses a profession, either one from this unit or any other job they know about. Second, the journalist prepares at least five questions to ask, such as: What does your job involve? What skills do you need? What is the most challenging part of your work? What advice would you give to a young person who wants to do this job? Third, the journalist asks the questions and listens carefully to the answers. Fourth, after the interview, the journalist reports what the worker said using reported speech. For example: She told me that her job involved reading legal documents. He said that the most challenging part was working long hours. Swap roles and play again with a different profession.
Activity 2 — Job Description Guessing Game

In this game, one person describes a job without saying the name of the profession. The listener must guess which job it is. Here is how to play. The speaker describes three things about the job: what the person does, where they work, and one tool or item they use. For example: This person writes scripts, speaks into a microphone, and works at a radio station. The listener guesses: A commentator! Try to play at least five rounds, using different jobs each time. After each round, the speaker should say one compound noun related to the job.
Activity 3 — Career Day Speech

Imagine your school is holding a Career Day and you have been asked to give a short talk about a profession. Prepare a one-to-two-minute speech. Your speech should include the name of the profession, what it involves, why it matters to the community, and why you think young people should consider it. Practise your speech before you deliver it. Speak clearly, make eye contact, and try to sound confident and enthusiastic. If you are working with a partner, ask them to listen and then report back to you what you said, using reported speech. This is a great way to practise both speaking and grammar at the same time.
Activity 4 — Listen and Report

Ask your AI tutor or a family member to tell you something about their job or their daily routine. Listen carefully without interrupting. When they have finished, write down three things they said using reported speech. For example, if your mother says, “I wake up at five o’clock every morning,” you would write: She said that she woke up at five o’clock every morning. This activity trains both your listening skills and your grammar. After writing your three sentences, share them with the person and check if you reported their words accurately.
Check Your Learning

Now that you have completed this unit, read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it. If you can, put a tick. If you are not sure, go back and review the relevant section before moving on. Being honest with yourself is the best way to make real progress.
I can name and describe at least four different professions and explain what skills each one requires.
I can explain how personal interests and strengths can guide someone’s career choices.
I can identify compound nouns in a text and say whether they are closed, hyphenated, or open form.
I can form my own compound nouns and use them correctly in sentences.
I can change direct speech into reported speech by adjusting pronouns, tenses, and time words.
I can report questions using asked + if/whether or asked + question word + statement order.
I can report commands using told + person + to + base verb.
I can use profession suffixes (-ist, -ian, -or, -er) to build job titles such as historian, physician, inventor, and commentator.
I can write a clear job advertisement or career presentation using compound nouns and reported speech.
I can give constructive peer feedback on a partner’s writing using specific comments.
I can take part in speaking and listening activities, including a career interview role-play and a job description guessing game.
I can use key vocabulary from this unit, including wisdom, profession, consent, legal, observation, commentary, penalties, efficient, contaminated, and defense, in my own writing and speech.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you anytime. Here are some ways to use it:
Ask for another explanation: “I do not understand reported speech. Can you explain the tense shift again with different examples?”
Practise speaking: “Can you interview me about a job, and I will answer in character?”
Get feedback: “Can you read my job advertisement and tell me what to improve?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences to change from direct to reported speech?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher from this unit?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Getting Ready for Unit 7: Nature’s Power
Make sure you know all the Unit 6 vocabulary words.
Keep practising compound nouns and reported speech — the more you use them, the more natural they become.
In Unit 7, you will explore natural disasters and the forces of nature. Ask your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 7? Can you get me ready?”
Well done for completing this unit! You have learned about the world of work and how people contribute to their communities.
tables:
🧭  How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through it section by section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar, and the Story “Voices in the Courtroom”.
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions.
the ability to make good choices based on knowledge and experience
My grandmother has great wisdom from years of helping her community.
a job that requires special training or education
Teaching is a noble profession that shapes the future.
You need your parents’ consent before joining the school trip.
related to the law or rules people must follow
The legal system protects people’s rights.
the act of watching something carefully to learn about it
Careful observation helped the scientist discover the pattern.
a spoken or written explanation about an event
The sports commentary made the match more exciting.
punishments for breaking a rule or law
There are penalties for littering in the park.
a noun made of two or more words combined to create a new meaning
Tuition fees and legal penalties are both compound nouns.
telling what someone said without quoting their exact words
She said that the test results were incomplete.
doing something well without wasting time or energy
An efficient worker finishes tasks quickly and carefully.
made dirty or unsafe by harmful substances
The water was contaminated, so we could not drink it.
the action of protecting someone or something from harm
The lawyer prepared a strong defense for her client.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between a job and a profession?
What compound nouns can you find in the reading passage?
Which of the four careers described interests you most, and why?

📖  Did You Know?
In Somalia and across East Africa, many skilled workers learn their professions through apprenticeships, where an experienced person teaches a younger person on the job. This tradition has been passing knowledge from one generation to the next for centuries.
The word profession comes from the Latin word professio, meaning a public declaration. Originally, it referred to taking a public vow or oath, such as a doctor's promise to help the sick.

💡  Top Tip: How to Spot a Compound Noun
Ask yourself: Do these two words together mean something different from what each word means alone?
If yes, you have found a compound noun!
Example: a swimming pool is not just any pool that happens to be swimming. It is a specific type of pool made for swimming.

💡  Memory Trick
Think of reported speech as telling a story about what someone said.
You are the storyteller. Shift everything one step into the past, change the pronouns to fit your perspective, and remove the quotation marks.
Pattern: Person + said/told + that + shifted sentence.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my answers for Exercises 3 and 4?
Can you give me more practice sentences for changing direct speech to reported speech?
I am confused about when to use said and when to use told. Can you explain again?

📝  Model Job Advertisement
Job Title: Community Health Worker
Location: Mogadishu Health Centre
We are looking for a dedicated community health worker to join our team. You will visit families, check on patients, and help people understand how to stay healthy.
Responsibilities: Visit homes in the local area. Record observations and test results. Report to the head physician. Teach families about hygiene and nutrition.
Requirements: A diploma in health studies or equivalent. Good communication skills. At least two years of experience in community health work.
Salary: Competitive. Working hours: Sunday to Thursday, 8 am to 3 pm.
If you are passionate about helping others, we encourage you to apply today!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my job advertisement and tell me if it is clear and persuasive?
Can you suggest compound nouns I could add to my career presentation?
Can you help me turn my direct quotes into reported speech?
Can you read my writing and check for grammar mistakes?

📖  Did You Know?
In many Somali communities, elders pass down career wisdom through stories and advice. A young person learning a trade often listens to an experienced elder for months before starting to practise the skill themselves. This tradition of listening before doing builds patience, respect, and deep understanding.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
I am not sure about some of my Check Your Learning answers. Can you quiz me on compound nouns and reported speech?
Can you give me a short passage and ask me to find all the compound nouns?
Can you test me by giving me direct speech sentences to change into reported speech?
