Morning: Review your vocabulary words and read a short section of the lesson.
Afternoon: Do one grammar exercise OR one writing activity from this file.
Evening: Practise speaking with your AI tutor and read part of the story.
Twice a week: Bring your writing, vocabulary notes, and speaking recordings to your teacher session.

paragraphs: Story — Unit 6: People and Work
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · “Voices in the Courtroom”
Voices in the Courtroom
The day of the trial felt different from every other in the town of Luyanda.
Shops opened more slowly. Motorbikes moved more quietly. Even the sky, usually so loud with birds, felt still.
Twelve-year-old Imani waited outside the school gate, clutching a folder full of papers. Her class was going on a field trip — not to the museum, or the zoo, but to the courtroom.
“We are currently observing a real case,” their teacher had said. “You’ll learn how rights, laws, and profession roles work in society.”
Imani had never stepped foot in a courthouse before. But she knew this was not a normal case.
It involved her neighbour, Mama Jalia.

Mama Jalia was a local food vendor who often gave Imani extra cassava or banana when she passed by. She was kind, wise, and popular. But last month, officials came to her stall and accused her of selling contaminated food.
They said her cooking water was unsafe. She was arrested, fined, and told she could no longer sell.
“I refuse to believe she did anything wrong,” Imani had told her parents.
“They say the test showed bacteria,” her mother had replied.
“She said the officer didn’t have her consent to test her pots,” Imani insisted.
“She told us,” her father added, “that the food wasn’t even served that day.”
Imani had listened to every word — and written it down. Now she carried those notes in her folder like evidence.

Inside the courthouse, rows of benches lined the walls. The room was full of murmurs. A judge in a black robe sat at the front. A commentator from the local radio station recorded notes for a short evening script.
The teacher handed out a sheet.
“Today, you will identify: the defense and prosecution; any compound nouns (such as tuition fees and legal penalties); verbs in present continuous; and new profession titles based on suffixes.”
Imani’s eyes danced across the list.
The case began.
“The State v. Jalia Mtebe,” the bailiff announced.
A young physician testified first. “The test showed bacteria in the container,” he said. “Food from this water could cause illness.”
“But was food served that day?” asked the defense lawyer.
The physician hesitated. “We’re not currently sure.”
Imani scribbled: “Observation unclear. Present continuous used.”
Mama Jalia’s commentary came next. She stood tall, her voice calm.

“The officer entered without asking. I never gave my consent. He touched my pots with gloves still dirty from the last stall. I told him: ‘That’s not allowed.’ But he wrote down his criticism anyway.”
Her lawyer nodded. “That’s reported speech. He said, she said.”
The teacher leaned over to Imani. “You’re doing good observation,” she whispered.
The prosecution argued that penalties must be applied equally. “All vendors must be inspected. Legal standards are there for a reason.”
“But,” Mama Jalia’s lawyer said, “the process was flawed. If the action was unfair, the penalty is unconstitutional.”
Imani whispered to her classmate: “That means it goes against the rules of the country.”
The judge listened quietly, taking notes with a blue pen. His face revealed nothing.
After a break, the students gathered under a tree beside the courthouse.
“I still don’t understand,” one boy said. “If there were figures, like test results, doesn’t that mean she’s guilty?”
“But the figures might be wrong,” Imani argued. “And even good data needs good methods.”
“It’s like when you study heredity in science,” said a girl. “You can’t just assume something based on one test.”
Their teacher nodded. “Good thinking. Scientific wisdom applies in court too. Look at all sides.”
Back inside, a final witness spoke: a food safety commentator from the city.
“She is efficient, yes,” the woman said. “But during food checks, many women vendors are targeted more. We need more fair integration, not more bias or segregation.”
Some murmured.
“Please avoid political speech,” said the prosecution.
But the judge replied, “Injustice is always political. Proceed.”
Imani sat up straighter.
The trial ended, and the judge said he would return a verdict the next day.
As the students filed out, the teacher asked them to write a reflection.
Imani wrote:

“Today we saw how laws are made and used. But rules must be fair. When someone like Mama Jalia is punished unfairly, people lose trust. Good observation is like science. It takes patience and care. I want to be a commentator, a legal advisor, or maybe a historian one day — someone who helps stories stay true.”
That night, Imani told her parents everything.
“She said the officer never washed his gloves. She said she never served that day.”
“She said the criticism was false,” her father added.
Her mother nodded. “She said the penalty wasn’t fair.”
“Exactly!” Imani smiled. “We all used reported speech. We all noticed the same things.”
The next morning, the town was waiting.
The judge read aloud: “The evidence was incomplete. The officer’s observation lacked protocol. The penalty is dropped. Mama Jalia is free to return to her profession.”
The room clapped softly.
Imani did not jump or cheer. She just grinned, wide and quiet.
Outside, Mama Jalia spotted her.
“You were in the courtroom?”
Imani nodded.
The woman touched her shoulder. “Thank you. Your presence mattered.”
Imani felt her heart fill. It was a small moment — but powerful.
The next week, the students wrote scripts of the event for a class radio play. Imani helped add captions under printed photos, and even wrote the commentary for the narrator.
Her script began:
“In the town of Luyanda, one woman defended her name. The story could’ve ended badly. But justice — when observed properly — can shift the course of centuries.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences:
Where is the story set? Name the town and the key locations.
Why did Imani’s class go on a field trip to the courthouse?
What was Mama Jalia accused of doing?
What did Mama Jalia say about the officer who tested her pots?
What is the role of the physician in the trial?
What does the word unconstitutional mean as Imani explains it?
What did the food safety commentator say about how women vendors are treated?
What was the judge’s verdict? What reasons did he give?
What did Imani write in her reflection at the end of the school day?
What project did the students complete the following week?
Think and Discuss
Talk about these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor:
Do you think the trial was conducted fairly? Use evidence from the story to support your opinion.
Imani says, “Even good data needs good methods.” What does she mean? Can you think of a real-life example?
The food safety commentator says that women vendors are targeted more than others. Why is this important to mention in a court?
Mama Jalia tells Imani, “Your presence mattered.” Why do you think it mattered that the students were there?
Imani wants to be “someone who helps stories stay true.” What professions could help her achieve this goal?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 6 vocabulary words and write the sentence they appear in:
wisdom
script
consent
legal
criticism
figures
defense
caption
integration
segregation
observation
profession
commentary
contaminated
penalties
efficient
centuries
presence
heredity
unconstitutional
Grammar in the Story
Find examples of these grammar features in the story:
Find three examples of compound nouns (e.g. tuition fees, legal penalties, radio station).
Find three examples of reported speech (e.g. She said the officer didn’t ask...).
Find one example of the present continuous used in the story.
Find two profession titles that use suffixes: -ian, -or, or -ist (e.g. physician, commentator).
Now You Try: Creative Response

Court Reporter: Imagine you are a journalist who was in the courtroom during Mama Jalia’s trial. Write a short newspaper report (eight to ten sentences) about what happened. Use reported speech to share what different people said.
Letter to Imani: Write a letter to Imani (six to eight sentences). Tell her what you thought about her actions during the trial and share your own thoughts about fairness and justice.

Career Reflection: Imani says she wants to be a commentator, a legal advisor, or a historian. Choose one of these professions and write a paragraph explaining what it involves, what skills it requires, and how it helps the community. Use at least two compound nouns.
tables:
📖  About this story
Genre: Realistic fiction with legal and social themes.
Theme: Education, justice, and the power of shared knowledge.
Setting: Modern-day East African town, centred around a school and courthouse.
Value: True justice begins with asking the right questions and listening to every voice.
Read it slowly. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, pay attention to how it is used!

✅  Answers — Grammar in the Story
1. Compound nouns: tuition fees, legal penalties, food vendor, radio station, test results, legal advisor, court room (courtroom).
2. Reported speech: She said the officer didn't have her consent. / She told us that the food wasn't even served. / She said the criticism was false. / She said the penalty wasn't fair.
3. Present continuous: We are currently observing a real case. / We're not currently sure.
4. Profession suffixes: physician (-ian), commentator (-or), historian (-ian).

paragraphs: Vocabulary — Unit 6: People and Work
Year 6 English · Ehel Academy · Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 6. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then do the practice activity. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Knowledge and Language
Practise These Words
Write a sentence using wisdom. Who is the wisest person you know, and why?
Have you ever seen a script for a play or a film? Write a short script with two characters and three lines of dialogue.
Use commentary in a sentence about a sports match or a school event.
Write two sentences: one using observation about nature, and one about observing people.
Group 2: Rights, Law, and Society
Practise These Words
Write a sentence using consent. When do young people need to ask for consent?
Explain the difference between segregation and integration in your own words.
Use legal and penalties together in one sentence.
What is your favourite profession? Write a sentence about it using the word correctly.
Group 3: Economy, Science, and Environment
Practise These Words
Name a commodity that is important in your country. Write a sentence about it.
Use contaminated in a sentence about food safety or the environment.
Write a sentence using efficient. What does it mean to be an efficient worker?
What is heredity? Write a sentence explaining one trait you share with a family member.
Group 4: Conflict, Resolution, and Other Important Words
Practise These Words
Write a sentence using frustration about a time you felt frustrated.
Use presence in a sentence about someone who makes a room feel different when they walk in.
Write two sentences: one using refuse as a verb, and one using allies.
What does it mean to be someone’s ally? Why are allies important?
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!

Vocabulary Review Activities
Activity 1: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence:
My grandmother’s ______ has helped our family make good decisions for many years.
The river was ______, so the authorities warned people not to drink from it.

The sports ______ on the radio made the match exciting to follow.
There are serious ______ for people who break the law.
Careful ______ helped the scientist discover something new.
Being a doctor is a ______ that requires many years of training.

Activity 2: Synonyms and Antonyms
Write a synonym (a word with a similar meaning) and an antonym (a word with the opposite meaning) for each word:
efficient — synonym: ______   antonym: ______
consent — synonym: ______   antonym: ______
frustration — synonym: ______   antonym: ______
integration — synonym: ______   antonym: ______

Activity 3: Use It in Context
Write a short paragraph (six to eight sentences) about a person you admire and their job. Use at least six vocabulary words from this unit. Underline the vocabulary words.

tables: The ability to make good choices and decisions based on knowledge and experience.
My grandmother has a lot of wisdom because she has lived for many years and learned many things.
A written text for a play, movie, or show that tells what the characters say and do.
The actors read their lines from the script during the rehearsal.
The written words in a book, article, or message.
I read the text in my English book to learn new words.
A spoken or written explanation or discussion about something.
The teacher gave a commentary on the story we read in class.
The act of watching something carefully to learn more about it.
During our science class, we made an observation about how plants grow in sunlight.
A short description or title that explains a picture or a video.
The caption under the photo tells us what is happening in the picture.
Numbers or shapes that represent something.
In maths class, we learned how to add and subtract different figures.

You need your parents’ consent to go on the school trip.
The action of protecting someone or something from harm or danger.
The team worked hard on their defense to stop the other team from scoring.
Related to the law or rules that people must follow.
It is legal to ride a bike on the road if you follow the rules.
Saying what is wrong or bad about something.
The teacher gave me some criticism on my essay to help me improve.
The act of keeping different groups of people apart from each other.
In the past, segregation meant that children of different races could not go to the same school.
The process of combining different things to make a whole.
The integration of new students into our class helps everyone learn together.
Punishments or consequences for breaking a rule or law.
If you break the school rules, there will be penalties like losing recess time.
A job that requires special training or education.
My father’s profession is a doctor, and he helps people stay healthy.
The money you pay to go to school or college.
My parents pay my tuition so I can learn at my school.

A basic item that people buy and sell, like food or materials.

Made dirty or unsafe by harmful substances.

Doing something in a way that saves time and energy.

The movement of people or animals from one place to another.
Every year, many birds make a long migration to warmer places for the winter.

A small space inside a cell that holds water, nutrients, or waste.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 7: Nature’s Power
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Five grammar mini-lessons

This file has five small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Past Simple vs Past Continuous
What it means and why it matters

The Past Simple describes actions that are finished. The Past Continuous describes actions that were happening — still in progress — at a certain moment in the past. When we use them together, we can tell vivid stories about events that happened at the same time or interrupted each other. This is especially useful when describing natural disasters, emergencies, or any event where many things were happening at once.
How to form it
Past Simple:
Regular verbs: add -ed → walk → walked, prepare → prepared.
Irregular verbs: learn special forms → go → went, see → saw, break → broke, send → sent.
Past Continuous:
I / he / she / it + was + verb-ing: “She was cooking dinner.”
you / we / they + were + verb-ing: “They were running to the shelter.”
Combined pattern: Past Continuous (background action) + when + Past Simple (interruption).
“I was reading when the earthquake struck.”
“They were sleeping when the alarm went off.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “I was eat lunch when she called.” Fix: “I was eating lunch.” (Always use the -ing form.)
Mistake: “They playing soccer.” Fix: “They were playing soccer.” (Do not forget was/were.)
Mistake: “She were cooking.” Fix: “She was cooking.” (Use was with he/she/it.)
Memory tip
Past Simple = a photograph (one finished moment). Past Continuous = a video (an action still playing). Together they make a film: the video is playing, and then the photograph is taken.
Practice 1
Fill in each blank with the Past Simple or Past Continuous form of the verb in brackets.
I ______ (watch) the news when the earthquake ______ (hit).
While they ______ (build) the shelter, the rain ______ (start).
She ______ (walk) to school when she ______ (hear) the warning siren.
The volunteers ______ (distribute) food when more supplies ______ (arrive).
We ______ (study) in the classroom while the wind ______ (blow) outside.
Lesson 2: Connecting Words for Time (while, when, until)
What it means and why it matters
Connecting words for time — also called time conjunctions — help us show how two events relate to each other in time. Using them correctly makes our stories clearer and our writing more interesting.
How to form and use it
while — two actions happening at the same time:
“I listened to the radio while I studied.”
“While the wind was blowing, trees fell to the ground.”
when — a specific moment, often an interruption:
“I was sleeping when the alarm rang.”
“When the flood came, the village was not ready.”
until — something continues up to a point and then stops:
“We waited until the storm passed.”
“The volunteers worked until every family had food.”
You may also use after (something that happens later) and before (something that happens earlier):
“After the earthquake, the community began to rebuild.”
“Before the storm arrived, the families moved inland.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: using while for a sudden event: “I was reading while the lights went out.” Fix: use when: “I was reading when the lights went out.”
Mistake: mixing up until and when: “We waited when help arrived.” Fix: “We waited until help arrived.”
Mistake: forgetting the comma: “While the rain was falling the river flooded.” Fix: “While the rain was falling, the river flooded.”
Memory tip
Think of three pictures: while = two people walking side by side (same time). when = a camera flash (one moment). until = a road that ends at a wall (up to a point).
Practice 2
Choose while, when, or until to complete each sentence.
______ the storm was approaching, the radio played warnings.
I was eating breakfast ______ the ground began to shake.
We stayed in the shelter ______ the rescue team arrived.
______ the adults were clearing the roads, the children helped carry water.
The fishermen did not go out ______ the waves calmed down.
She was writing in her notebook ______ the teacher called her name.
Lesson 3: Imperatives for Giving Instructions
What it means and why it matters

Imperatives are command words that tell someone what to do. They are very important during emergencies and natural disasters, when instructions must be clear, short, and direct. For example: “Move to higher ground.” “Do not walk through floodwater.”
How to form it
Start with the base form of the verb (no subject needed): “Prepare your emergency bag.”
Add “Do not” or “Don’t” for a negative command: “Do not go near the river.”
Add “Please” for a polite command: “Please stay inside until the wind stops.”
You can use “Let’s” to include yourself: “Let’s work together to clear the road.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: adding a subject: “You prepare your bag.” Fix: “Prepare your bag.”
Mistake: using the wrong verb form: “Preparing your bag.” Fix: “Prepare your bag.”
Mistake: adding “s”: “Moves to higher ground.” Fix: “Move to higher ground.”
Memory tip
Think of imperatives as your emergency voice — short, clear, and direct. No time for extra words!
Practice 3
Write the correct imperative for each situation.
Tell people to leave the building. (Use: leave)
Tell children not to play near the river. (Use: do not / play)
Ask a neighbour to bring clean water. (Use: please / bring)
Tell your family to prepare food for three days. (Use: prepare)
Tell everyone to stay calm. (Use: stay)
Lesson 4: Adjectives + Prepositions for Feelings
What it means and why it matters

In English, we often describe our feelings using an adjective followed by a preposition. This helps us say exactly what we feel and what caused the feeling. For example: “I am amazed by the restoration.” “She is interested in how cyclones form.”
How to form it
Subject + be + adjective + preposition + object.
amazed by: “We were amazed by the power of the storm.”
interested in: “He is interested in studying natural disasters.”
worried about: “The farmers were worried about the drought.”
proud of: “The community is proud of its commitment to rebuilding.”
surprised by: “I was surprised by how quickly the allies arrived.”
excited about: “The students were excited about presenting their illustrations.”
scared of: “Some children were scared of the thunder.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “I am interested by science.” Fix: “I am interested in science.”
Mistake: leaving out the preposition: “She is worried the exam.” Fix: “She is worried about the exam.”
Memory tip
Learn adjective-preposition pairs as mini phrases. Write them on cards: one side says “amazed”, the other says “by”. Test yourself every day.
Practice 4
Fill in the correct preposition: by, in, about, or of.
She was amazed ______ the size of the waves.
We are worried ______ the next storm season.
He is interested ______ learning about volcanoes.
The town is proud ______ its strong community spirit.
They were surprised ______ how much damage the flood caused.
Lesson 5: Could + Verb (Possibility)
What it means and why it matters

The modal verb could helps us talk about things that are possible but not certain. This is useful when we discuss what might happen during or after a natural disaster, or when we suggest ideas. For example: “This could cause serious flooding.” “Allies could send food and medicine.”
How to form it
Subject + could + base form of the verb (no -s, no -ing, no -ed).
“The drought could last for several more months.”
“This policy could improve the distribution of food.”
“It could cause inflation if supplies run out.”
“Could we write our own script?” (from the story)
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She could to go to the shelter.” Fix: “She could go to the shelter.” (No “to” after could.)
Mistake: “They could goes to help.” Fix: “They could go to help.” (Always use the base form.)
Memory tip

Think of could as a door that is open but you have not walked through yet. The action is possible, but it has not happened. “I could help” means the possibility is there — I just have not done it yet.
Practice 5
Complete each sentence using could and the verb in brackets.
The rising river ______ (flood) the farmland.
This plan ______ (save) many lives.
The earthquake ______ (damage) older buildings.
______ we ______ (start) a collection for the flood victims?
The strong wind ______ (knock) down power lines.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Past Simple vs Past Continuous)
1. was watching, hit   2. were building, started   3. was walking, heard   4. were distributing, arrived   5. were studying, was blowing
Practice 2 (Connecting words)
1. While   2. when   3. until   4. While   5. until   6. when
Practice 3 (Imperatives)
1. Leave the building.   2. Do not play near the river.   3. Please bring clean water.   4. Prepare food for three days.   5. Stay calm.
Practice 4 (Adjectives + prepositions)
1. by   2. about   3. in   4. of   5. by
Practice 5 (Could + verb)
1. could flood   2. could save   3. could damage   4. Could, start   5. could knock

Can you check my grammar answers?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise Past Simple vs Past Continuous?
Can you explain the difference between while and when with more examples?

paragraphs: Unit 7: Nature’s Power
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 7: Nature’s Power! In this unit, you will explore the incredible and sometimes dangerous forces of the natural world. You will learn about natural disasters such as cyclones, earthquakes, floods, droughts, and volcanic eruptions. You will discover how communities in East Africa and around the world prepare for these events, respond when they strike, and rebuild afterwards. Along the way, you will build a strong set of vocabulary words related to planning, science, government, and community action. You will also study an important grammar topic — how to use the Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses together, joined by connecting words like while, when, and until — to describe events that happened at the same time in the past. These skills will help you write powerful safety broadcasts, disaster reports, and opinion pieces about how people can work together when nature shows its strength.

By the end of Unit 7, you will also read this unit’s story, “The Speech Under the Baobab,” about a brave girl named Tendo who stands up at a community forum to speak for the young people of her village. Although the story explores leadership and fair systems, it connects to our theme because real leaders are often tested during times of crisis — including natural disasters. Tendo’s courage shows us that young voices matter, especially when communities must come together to face difficult challenges.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use and understand key vocabulary about natural forces, science, planning, government, and community.
Read and discuss a text about natural disasters and how communities respond to them.
Identify the Past Simple tense (for completed actions) and the Past Continuous tense (for actions that were happening when something else occurred).
Use connecting words — while, when, and until — to link events that happened at the same time or one after another.
Give clear safety instructions using imperatives (command verbs).
Express possibility using could + verb.
Describe feelings about events using adjectives + prepositions (for example, amazed by, interested in).
Write a safety advice broadcast and a natural disaster report.
Speak clearly when presenting a news report or sharing an opinion about how communities should prepare for natural events.
Think critically about cause and effect in nature and evaluate how different systems help communities.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day, grouped by theme.
Work through the five mini-lessons in the Grammar file, one or two per day.
Read the Story file, “The Speech Under the Baobab,” and answer the comprehension questions.
Work through this main lesson: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Activities → Self-Assessment.
Finish with the Check Your Learning section to see how much you have improved.
Key Vocabulary

Before you start reading, let us preview some of the most important words in this unit. You will find the full list with definitions, examples, and practice tasks in your separate Vocabulary file. Here, we will look at four groups of words and think about how they connect to nature’s power and the way communities respond to it.
Group A: Nature, Science, and Technology

Nature is full of powerful forces. Conduction is the way heat moves through materials — for example, the warmth of the sun heating the ocean surface, which can feed energy into storms. Pollination is the natural process by which bees and other insects move pollen between flowers, helping plants produce seeds and fruit. After a disaster, teams may carry out an excavation — digging carefully through rubble to find survivors or important objects buried underground. High above the earth, the stratosphere is the layer of the atmosphere where the ozone layer sits and where the tops of the most powerful storms can reach. Mechanization — the use of machines instead of human hands — helps farmers grow food more quickly, and it also helps rescue teams clear roads after floods. Finally, an illustration is a picture or drawing that helps explain something, like a diagram of the water cycle or a map of an earthquake zone.
Group B: Government, Economy, and Systems

When a natural disaster strikes, government and economic systems play a big role in how quickly a community recovers. Administration means the group of people who manage or organise a school, company, or government. A declaration is a formal statement or announcement — a government might make a declaration of emergency after an earthquake. Copyright protects creative work, such as the designs of buildings that engineers rebuild after a disaster. This unit also introduces three systems of government and economy: capitalism (where people own businesses and compete in markets), socialism (where the government shares important resources fairly), and communism (where the government owns all property and shares it equally). The people who believe in these systems are called a capitalist, a socialist, or a communist. Understanding these systems helps you think about how societies decide to share money, food, and help when a crisis happens.
Group C: Planning, Change, and Action

Preparing for and recovering from a natural disaster involves careful planning. Preparation is the process of getting ready — filling sandbags, storing water, and making a safety plan. Calculation is using maths to work out answers, such as how much food a shelter needs for one hundred people for three days. Distribution is the fair sharing of supplies so that every family receives what it needs. Inflation is when prices rise, which often happens after a disaster because goods become scarce. Conversion is changing something from one form to another — for example, converting a school hall into an emergency shelter. Restoration is the process of bringing something back to its original condition, like repairing broken roads and rebuilding damaged homes. An initiative is when someone starts something new without being told — perhaps a student who organises a collection of blankets for flood victims. And commitment is a promise to keep going, even when the work is hard.
Group D: Community, Voice, and Expression

Natural disasters test the strength of a community, and strong communities are built on communication and trust. A congregation is a group of people who come together, often for prayer or a meeting — after a storm, the whole congregation might gather to discuss what to do next. A declaration (introduced above) is one way leaders communicate important news. Representation means showing or speaking on behalf of others, such as a student representative who shares the class’s ideas at a community meeting. Expression is a way of showing your thoughts or feelings, whether through words, art, or actions. Presence means being there — sometimes the most helpful thing is simply having the presence of a friend by your side during a difficult time. And allies are friends or partners who help each other. After a disaster, neighbouring towns often become allies, sending supplies and volunteers to support one another.
Reading: “When Nature Speaks”

Read this passage carefully. It is about a fictional coastal community in East Africa that faces a powerful cyclone. As you read, look for vocabulary words from this unit and think about how the community works together. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.

Last March, the weather changed quickly along the east coast of Africa. Dark clouds gathered above the Indian Ocean, and the wind grew stronger every hour. The local administration issued a declaration: a powerful cyclone was heading toward the coastal town of Mabari.

The preparation began at once. Families moved inland. Fishermen pulled their boats far up the beach. The school was converted into a shelter — a quick conversion that saved many lives. A congregation of neighbours gathered in the town hall to hear the safety plan.
“We were watching the sky when the radio announcement came,” said twelve-year-old Idris. “My mother was cooking dinner, and my father was mending our fence. Everything stopped.”

While the adults were loading supplies into trucks, children helped carry water bottles and blankets. The distribution of food and clean water was organised by volunteers who worked through the night. They knew that fair distribution could save lives.

The cyclone arrived before dawn. Rain hammered the rooftops while the wind was howling like a wild animal. Trees bent and snapped. The sea pushed inland, flooding streets and farms. In one hour, the water level rose by more than a metre.

By morning, the wind had died down, but the damage was clear. Houses were broken. Crops were destroyed. Roads were covered in mud and fallen branches. Scientists later explained that heat conduction from the warm ocean surface had fed energy into the storm. When the warm, moist air rose high into the stratosphere, it created the spinning winds that made the cyclone so powerful.

“We knew the storm was coming because of modern mechanization,” explained a local teacher. “Weather stations and satellite images gave the administration enough time to act. Without that technology, many more people could have been hurt.”

After the storm, restoration work began. Allies from neighbouring towns sent building materials and medicine. Engineers used careful calculation to check which roads and bridges were safe. An excavation team cleared mud from the school so that children could return to class.

The town held a public meeting. One elder made a declaration: “Nature has great power. We cannot stop a cyclone, but we can prepare, and we can rebuild.” His words were a clear expression of the community’s commitment to recovery.

Students from Mabari’s school created illustrations showing the water cycle and how cyclones form. They wrote captions explaining each stage, and their teacher displayed the work on the school notice board as a representation of what the children had learned.
Idris added his own drawing: a picture of neighbours carrying sandbags side by side. Underneath, he wrote: “Preparation saves lives. Restoration rebuilds hope.”

The experience taught everyone in Mabari that nature’s power is real and enormous. Earthquakes shake the ground beneath our feet. Volcanoes send ash into the sky. Droughts dry the rivers for months. Floods sweep away everything in their path. But when communities work together — sharing resources, making fair distribution of supplies, and keeping a strong commitment to one another — they can face even the fiercest storm.
Nature speaks loudly. The question is whether we listen, prepare, and stand together when it does.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage if you need to.
What natural disaster was heading toward the town of Mabari?
How did the community prepare before the cyclone arrived? Name at least three things they did.
What was Idris doing when the radio announcement came? What were his parents doing?
How did the children help during the preparation?
According to the scientists, what caused the cyclone to become so powerful? Mention conduction and the stratosphere in your answer.
How did modern mechanization help the community?
What did the allies from neighbouring towns do after the storm?
What did the engineers use calculation for?
What did the elder say at the public meeting? What does his statement tell us about the community’s attitude?
What did the students create to show what they had learned?
Think and Discuss
Talk about these questions with your AI tutor, a partner, or your family. There is no single right answer — explain your ideas clearly.
The passage says, “Nature speaks loudly.” What do you think this means? How does nature “speak”?
Idris wrote, “Preparation saves lives. Restoration rebuilds hope.” Do you agree? Why or why not?
Imagine you live in Mabari. What would you do to help your community after the cyclone? Think of one initiative you could start.
The passage mentions several natural disasters: cyclones, earthquakes, volcanoes, droughts, and floods. Which one do you think is the most dangerous, and why?
Why is fair distribution of supplies so important during a crisis? What could happen if the distribution is not fair?
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the passage. Read the sentence each word appears in, then write your own sentence using the same word. Try to connect your sentence to the theme of nature’s power.
administration
declaration
preparation
distribution
conduction
stratosphere
mechanization
restoration
allies
calculation
excavation
expression
commitment
illustration
representation
congregation
conversion
Grammar Focus A: Past Simple vs Past Continuous

In this unit, we are going to learn how two important past tenses work together to describe events. You already know the Past Simple tense from earlier units. Now you will learn the Past Continuous tense and discover how to use them together to tell vivid stories about things that happened in the past.
What Is the Past Simple?

The Past Simple tense describes actions that started and finished in the past. The action is complete — it is done. We use it to talk about things that happened at a definite time, such as yesterday, last week, or an hour ago.

For regular verbs, we form the Past Simple by adding -ed to the base form of the verb. For example: play → played, help → helped, prepare → prepared. For irregular verbs, we must learn special past forms: go → went, see → saw, come → came, break → broke, send → sent.
Examples:
The cyclone arrived before dawn.
The elder made a declaration.
Engineers checked which bridges were safe.
Idris added his own drawing.
What Is the Past Continuous?
The Past Continuous tense describes an action that was happening — it was in progress — at a certain moment in the past. The action had started but had not yet finished when something else happened.
We form the Past Continuous with was or were + the -ing form of the main verb:
I / he / she / it + was + verb-ing:  “Idris was watching the sky.”
you / we / they + were + verb-ing:  “The adults were loading supplies.”
Examples:
My mother was cooking dinner when the announcement came.
The children were carrying blankets to the shelter.
The wind was howling while the rain hammered the roof.
Using Both Tenses Together

The real power of these two tenses appears when you use them together in one sentence. The Past Continuous sets the scene — it tells us what was already happening. The Past Simple then introduces a shorter, completed action that interrupted or happened during the longer one.
Think of it like a film: the camera is showing a scene (Past Continuous), and then something suddenly happens (Past Simple). Here are examples from our reading passage:
“We were watching the sky when the radio announcement came.” — The watching was already happening; then the announcement interrupted it.
“My mother was cooking dinner, and my father was mending our fence.” — Both actions were in progress at the same time.
“Rain hammered the rooftops while the wind was howling.” — The howling was the ongoing background; the hammering happened during it.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Learners sometimes make these mistakes with the Past Continuous:
Mistake: “I was eat lunch when she called.” — Fix: always use the -ing form: “I was eating lunch when she called.”
Mistake: “They playing football when it started to rain.” — Fix: do not forget was or were: “They were playing football.”
Mistake: “She were cooking.” — Fix: use was with he/she/it: “She was cooking.”
Mistake: using Past Continuous for a completed action: “I was finishing my homework yesterday.” — Fix: use Past Simple for a completed action: “I finished my homework yesterday.”
Memory Tip

Think of Past Simple as a photograph — it captures one finished moment. Think of Past Continuous as a video — it shows an action that was still happening. When we combine them, we get a story: the video is playing (Past Continuous), and then the photograph is taken (Past Simple). For example: “I was reading (video) when the lights went out (photograph).”
Grammar Focus B: Connecting Words for Time (while, when, until)

Connecting words for time help us show how two events are related. They tell the reader or listener exactly when one thing happened compared to another. In this unit, we focus on three important connecting words: while, when, and until. We will also briefly mention after and before, which you may already know.
While
Use while when two actions are happening at the same time. It often pairs with the Past Continuous tense because both actions are in progress together.
“While the adults were loading supplies, children helped carry blankets.”
“The wind was howling while rain hammered the rooftops.”
“While some were talking, others were planting.” (from the story)
Notice that while can come at the beginning or in the middle of the sentence. When it starts the sentence, use a comma after the first clause.
When
Use when to show that one action happened at the moment another action occurred. The clause with when often uses Past Simple, because it marks a specific point in time.
“We were watching the sky when the radio announcement came.”
“When the cyclone arrived, the streets flooded within an hour.”
“I was sleeping when the earthquake shook the building.”
A useful pattern: Past Continuous + when + Past Simple. This tells us what was already happening (background) and what suddenly happened (interruption).
Until
Use until to show that something continues up to a certain point and then stops. It answers the question: how long did the action last?
“The volunteers worked until the sun came up.”
“We waited at the shelter until the storm passed.”
“The children did not return to school until the excavation team cleared the mud.”
Notice that until can also appear in the middle of a sentence: “They kept digging until they found the lost supplies.”
Quick Summary Table
Here is a simple way to remember the three words:
while = at the same time (two actions happening together)
when = at that moment (one action interrupts another)
until = up to the point that (an action continues, then stops)
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Mistake: “I was sleeping while the alarm rang.” — Fix: the alarm is a sudden, short action, so use when: “I was sleeping when the alarm rang.”
Mistake: mixing up until and when: “We waited when help arrived.” — Fix: we were waiting for a period of time, so use until: “We waited until help arrived.”
Mistake: forgetting the comma after the time clause at the start: “While the wind was blowing the trees fell.” — Fix: “While the wind was blowing, the trees fell.”
Grammar Practice
Complete these exercises. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the bottom of this section, or ask your AI tutor to check them for you.
Exercise 1: Past Simple or Past Continuous?
Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb in brackets.
The earthquake ______ (strike) while we ______ (eat) breakfast.
I ______ (read) a book when the lights ______ (go) out.
They ______ (run) to the shelter when the alarm ______ (sound).
While the teacher ______ (explain) the lesson, a strong wind ______ (blow) the door open.
The children ______ (sleep) peacefully when the first wave ______ (hit) the shore.
We ______ (walk) home from school when it ______ (start) to rain heavily.
My sister ______ (write) in her notebook while I ______ (listen) to the radio.
The rescue team ______ (arrive) while the villagers ______ (wait) at the community hall.
Exercise 2: Choose the Connecting Word
Fill in the blank with while, when, or until.
We stayed inside ______ the storm passed.
______ the rain was falling, the river kept rising.
I was carrying water bottles ______ the truck arrived.
The volunteers worked ______ every family had food.
______ we were clearing the mud, the engineers checked the bridge.
The fishermen did not go back to the sea ______ the waves calmed down.
The elder was speaking ______ a loud crack of thunder interrupted him.
______ the cyclone was approaching, the administration made a declaration.
Exercise 3: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one sentence for each prompt. Use the tense and connecting word shown.
Two actions happening at the same time during a storm. (Use while + Past Continuous.)
An action that was interrupted by another event. (Use when + Past Simple / Past Continuous.)
An action that continued up to a certain point. (Use until.)
Something that happened in your own life during bad weather. (Use any tense and connecting word.)
Answer Key
Exercise 1:

1. struck, were eating   2. was reading, went   3. were running, sounded   4. was explaining, blew   5. were sleeping, hit   6. were walking, started   7. was writing, was listening   8. arrived, were waiting
Exercise 2:
1. until   2. While   3. when   4. until   5. While   6. until   7. when   8. While
Writing Workshop
Writing Task 1: Safety Advice Broadcast

Imagine you are a radio presenter in the town of Mabari. A new storm is approaching, and you must read a safety broadcast to warn the people. Your broadcast should be between 80 and 120 words long. Follow this plan:
Opening: Greet your listeners and state the emergency clearly. For example: “Good evening, Mabari. This is an urgent safety message.”
What is happening: Describe the approaching danger using one or two Past Continuous sentences to set the scene, and Past Simple to say what the administration has declared.

Safety instructions: Give at least four clear instructions using imperatives. For example: “Move away from the coast.” “Stay indoors until the wind stops.” “Do not walk through floodwater.” “Prepare food and clean water for two days.”
Community message: End with a message about working together. Mention commitment, allies, or preparation.
Closing: Sign off politely. For example: “Stay safe, Mabari. We are in this together.”
Here is a sample opening to help you start:

“Good evening, Mabari. This is Radio Mabari with an urgent safety message. The administration has declared that a powerful storm is heading our way. While we were enjoying a calm afternoon, weather stations detected strong winds over the Indian Ocean. Please listen carefully to the following instructions.”
Writing Task 2: Natural Disaster Report
Now imagine you are a news reporter writing about a natural disaster that has already happened. Choose one of the following events, or invent your own:
A flood that covered farmland near a river in Somalia.
An earthquake that shook buildings in a small town in Kenya.
A drought that dried up wells in a village in Ethiopia.
A volcanic eruption that sent ash into the sky near the Rift Valley.
Your report should be between 100 and 150 words. Follow this plan:
Headline: Write a short, clear headline. For example: “Flood Hits Farmland Near Jubba River.”
What happened: Describe the event using Past Simple. Say when it happened and where.
What was happening before: Set the scene using Past Continuous. For example: “Farmers were working in the fields when the river burst its banks.”
The response: Describe how the community and the administration responded. Use vocabulary from this unit: distribution, restoration, allies, calculation, preparation.
Looking ahead: End with one or two sentences about what will happen next. You may use could to express possibility: “This could lead to food shortages unless allies send help quickly.”
After writing, read your report aloud as if you are presenting it on the radio. Ask your AI tutor to listen and give you feedback on your grammar and vocabulary.
Writing Task 3: Opinion Paragraph

Write a short paragraph (60–100 words) answering this question: “What is the most important thing a community can do to prepare for a natural disaster?” Give your opinion and support it with at least two reasons. Use vocabulary from this unit and try to include one sentence with while, when, or until. Here is a sentence starter to help you:
“I believe the most important thing a community can do to prepare for a natural disaster is …”
Listening & Speaking
Listening 1: A Survivor’s Account
Ask your AI tutor to read the following survivor’s account aloud to you. Listen carefully, then answer the questions below. After that, read it yourself.

“My name is Amina. I am thirteen years old, and I live in a small fishing village on the coast. Last year, a terrible cyclone hit our area. I was studying for my exams when the wind started. My mother was preparing dinner while my little brother was playing outside. We heard the warning on the radio and ran to the school shelter. We waited there until the storm passed. The next morning, our house was damaged, but we were safe. The whole congregation helped each other. People shared food, and allies from the next town brought medicine. I learned that preparation really does save lives.”
After listening, answer:
What was Amina doing when the wind started?
What were her mother and brother doing?
Where did the family go for safety?
How long did they stay at the shelter?
How did the community and its allies help?
What lesson did Amina learn?
Listening 2: A Nature Report
Ask your AI tutor to read this short report. Listen, then answer.

“Today, scientists confirmed that the earthquake measured 5.2 on the Richter scale. While people were going about their normal day, the ground began to shake at exactly 2:14 in the afternoon. Buildings trembled. Glass fell from windows. The administration issued a declaration within minutes, telling people to move to open spaces. Volunteers began a careful excavation of one collapsed building. When rescuers arrived, they found all fifteen people inside safe and alive. The restoration of damaged roads will begin next week.”
After listening, answer:
How strong was the earthquake?
What were people doing when it struck?
What did the administration do?
What did the volunteers do?
What will happen next week?
Speaking 1: News Presenter Role-Play
Read your disaster report (from the Writing Workshop) aloud, as if you are a real news presenter on television or radio. Follow these tips:
Speak clearly and slowly. Pause at full stops.
Use a serious but calm tone. You are informing people, not scaring them.
Emphasise important words: the name of the disaster, the location, and the key numbers.
Record yourself if you can, then listen back and look for ways to improve.
Speaking 2: Discussion — How Should We Prepare?
With a partner, your family, or your AI tutor, discuss the following questions. Speak in full sentences and use vocabulary from this unit.
What are the most common natural disasters in your area or country? Have you ever experienced one?
What should a community do to prepare for a flood or a drought? Who should be in charge of the preparation?
The reading passage says, “Nature speaks loudly.” What does this mean to you? Do you think humans listen to nature enough?
How can young people like you help during a natural disaster? Think of one initiative you could take.
Speaking Game: Disaster Timeline
Play this game with your AI tutor or a friend:
One person describes a scene using the Past Continuous: “People were sleeping peacefully.”
The other person adds an interrupting event using when + Past Simple: “… when the earthquake shook the ground.”
Take turns. Try to make five complete timeline sentences together.
Challenge: add a sentence with until to each pair. For example: “They waited in the shelter until the shaking stopped.”
Activities & Exercises
Activity 1: Match the Cause and Effect
Draw a line from the cause to the correct effect. Write the matching letter next to the number.
Warm ocean water feeds energy into the air.
A drought lasts for many months.
An earthquake shakes a town.
The administration issues a declaration.
Allies send food and medicine.
a) Rivers dry up and crops die.   b) A powerful cyclone forms.   c) Families who were hurt receive help.   d) Buildings crack and roads break.   e) People know what to do and where to go.
Answers: 1. b   2. a   3. d   4. e   5. c
Activity 2: Fill in the Vocabulary Word
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence.
Word box: preparation, distribution, restoration, excavation, declaration, allies, commitment, administration
The ______ of the old bridge took three months, but it looks wonderful now.
After the flood, the fair ______ of food made sure every family received a share.
The ______ issued a ______ telling people to move to higher ground.
Our neighbours became our ______ and helped us clear the mud from our house.
Good ______ before a storm can save many lives.
The rescue team began an ______ to find supplies buried under the rubble.
The whole village showed great ______ by working together until every house was repaired.
Answers: 1. restoration   2. distribution   3. administration, declaration   4. allies   5. preparation   6. excavation   7. commitment
Activity 3: Rewrite Using Past Continuous
Rewrite each sentence so that one action uses the Past Continuous and the other uses the Past Simple. The first one is done for you.
The rain fell. The children ran to the shelter.  →  The rain was falling when the children ran to the shelter.
The teacher explained the lesson. The earthquake shook the building.
We ate breakfast. The warning siren sounded.
Fishermen mended their nets. The cyclone approached the coast.
Volunteers distributed food. More help arrived from the next town.
Activity 4: Critical Thinking
Think carefully about these questions. There is no single correct answer. Write or talk about your ideas, using vocabulary from this unit and explaining your reasons with because and so that.
Some people say that natural disasters are becoming more frequent because of climate change. What do you think, and why?
In the reading passage, the elder said, “We cannot stop a cyclone, but we can prepare.” Is preparation always enough? What else do communities need?
Compare how two different systems (for example, capitalism and socialism) might handle the distribution of food after a flood. Which system might be fairer? Why?
Imagine you are the head of the administration in your town. A drought has lasted for three months. What would you do? Think about calculation, distribution, and allies.
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it wisely:
Ask for another explanation: “I do not understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you read this passage aloud and then listen to me read it back?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my disaster report and tell me what to improve?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me five more sentences to practise while, when, and until?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet” for each statement. For every “Not yet,” ask your AI tutor for more practice on that topic.
I can use key vocabulary about natural forces, planning, systems, and community.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can read a text about a natural disaster and answer comprehension questions.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I understand the difference between Past Simple and Past Continuous.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use while, when, and until to connect events in the past.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can give safety instructions using imperative verbs.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can express possibility using could + verb.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a safety broadcast and a disaster report.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can present a news report or share an opinion clearly when speaking.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can think critically about cause and effect in natural disasters.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Getting Ready for Unit 8

Well done — you have finished Unit 7! In the next unit, Unit 8: Entertainment and Media, you will explore the world of films, video games, and media. You will learn new vocabulary about entertainment, study participle adjectives and the past simple passive, and write your own film review.
Make sure you know all the Unit 7 vocabulary words on your Word Wall.
Keep practising your Past Simple and Past Continuous sentences every day.
Tell your AI tutor: “What will I learn in Unit 8? Can you help me get ready?”
Well done — learning a language takes time and practice. Be patient, keep trying, and celebrate every new word and sentence you master!

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 7: Nature’s Power
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Speech Under the Baobab”
The Speech Under the Baobab

It was the dry season in Mbuma, and the dust hung low over the hills like memory refusing to settle. A small congregation of villagers gathered beneath the baobab tree in the central square. Children whispered. Elders shifted their weight. Something important was coming.
Twelve-year-old Tendo stood beside her grandfather, holding a folded sheet of paper in her hand.
The administration had announced that students could now attend the weekly community forum. Tendo’s school had chosen her to represent them.
“You will speak on behalf of the youth,” her teacher said. “It’s called representation.”
Tendo’s hands trembled. Her voice had never been heard by more than a classroom.
Her grandfather placed a calm hand on her shoulder. “Speak truth. Speak clearly. The baobab remembers.”

Just days earlier, a big event had taken place in Mbuma. The local leader had made a public declaration that the economy would change. The country was leaving a capitalist model and adopting socialism — a system that promised fair distribution of wealth and public resources.
“We must shift toward equality,” the leader had said. “Let us restore balance after years of unfair trade.”
Many villagers had clapped. But others were worried.
“What does socialism mean?” someone had asked.
“Will the shops close?” another whispered.
“Could it cause inflation?” muttered one merchant. “My prices will have to rise.”
Even Tendo’s parents were unsure.
“Before we had capitalism — competition, ownership, private markets,” her mother said. “Now they say we must share everything. It sounds better, but…”
“But systems are not just words,” her father added. “They need care, not only promises.”
The school had held a lesson on different systems.
“Our current situation,” said the teacher, “comes from a capitalist model. But others include communism, where all resources are owned equally, and socialism, where things are shared fairly by law.”
One boy raised his hand. “Which is better?”
“That depends,” the teacher replied. “Each system has its values — and its failures.”
She drew three illustrations on the board: a market with coins, a factory with one large owner, and a communal field with many hands.
“Your task,” she said, “is to write about which system could best help our village.”
Tendo’s group chose to write about restoration — rebuilding after long hardship. They included a photo and caption that said: “Shared power grows deep roots.”
On forum day, Tendo arrived early. Her classmate Biko joined her, carrying a chart with calculations showing how food could be distributed more fairly.
“We used real data,” he said. “Even with basic conduction of food supplies, we could feed 40 more families per week.”
Tendo nodded. “But ideas must be spoken. Let’s make sure we’re heard.”
The forum began with the head of the administration. He wore a beige suit and dark sunglasses.

“Citizens,” he began, “we have started an initiative for change. This includes new trade agreements, updated copyright protections for local artists, and expansion of mechanical farming tools — also known as mechanization.”
Tendo listened carefully. She scribbled notes: copyright = protect ideas; mechanization = machines help farms.
“But who benefits?” whispered Biko. “He didn’t say.”
Then came the floor for community voices.

An elder stood up. “I have farmed for 40 years. I don’t understand these changes. Last week I tried to sell cassava and was told I needed a permit. My son says it’s part of the new conversion system. What is that?”
The official responded, “We are converting from open selling to registered trade. It ensures fairer taxes.”
“But we are losing freedom,” the elder said.
The official looked away.
Next, a young woman spoke. “The new school fees are too high. I had to drop out. I can’t afford the tuition.”
A soft murmur spread through the crowd.
Then another voice spoke up — this time, sharp and direct.
“The new system has potential, yes,” said the town merchant. “But it also creates confusion. We used to know the rules. Now we’re guessing.”
“It’s a time of preparation,” replied the official. “Change takes effort.”
Finally, the moderator turned to Tendo. “We welcome our student speaker.”
Tendo stepped forward. She felt the weight of every eye in the square.
“My name is Tendo. I come as part of a new generation, but I speak with an old voice.”
She opened her folded paper.
“While some were talking, others were planting. While systems changed, people still needed to eat. While the declaration was made, some children missed school.”
The crowd was quiet.
“We have studied capitalism, socialism, and communism. Each system speaks of justice. But justice is not automatic. It must be lived.”
She looked at the official.
“You say there will be restoration. But we also ask: who decides what is restored? Who speaks for the ones without a microphone?”
Biko held up the chart.
“With better calculation and careful distribution, no one needs to go hungry. The students have created models. We ask that you review them.”
There was a pause.
Then a boy in the front row called out, “Go Tendo!”
A few claps followed. Then more.
The official adjusted his tie.
“We are currently revising plans,” he said slowly. “Perhaps we could meet with the students next week.”
After the forum, Tendo sat beneath the baobab tree.
“You did well,” her grandfather said.
“I spoke with my true expression,” she replied.
“And the baobab remembers,” he said, smiling.
Later that month, new proposals were posted on the school notice board.

The initiative would now include: a congregation of student and teacher representatives; a review of calculation methods for food sharing; a declaration of student voices in community meetings; and a pilot programme for pollinator gardens and local pollination studies.
Tendo’s class created illustrations and wrote captions for each proposal. Biko added a note: “Representation is more than attendance. It is presence.”
The administration even added a line: “Thanks to student commitment, the system grows stronger.”
That weekend, a guest visited their classroom — a researcher from the city.
“I’ve read your work,” she told them. “I’m preparing a historical excavation of local economies. You’ve made an impact.”
Biko blinked. “We’re only twelve.”
“Age doesn’t limit insight,” she said. “Just as systems don’t limit dreams.”
Tendo raised her hand. “Could we write our own script? Like a declaration of values for the next generation?”
The researcher nodded. “With your permission, I’ll even help you share it.”
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Where does the story take place, and what season is it?
Why was Tendo chosen to attend the community forum?
What big change did the local leader announce? What system was the country moving to?
Why were some villagers worried about the change? Give at least two reasons.
What did Tendo’s school teach the class about capitalism, socialism, and communism?
What did Tendo’s group choose to write about for their school task?
What did Biko bring to the forum, and what did his data show?
In her speech, Tendo said, “While some were talking, others were planting.” What grammar tenses is she using, and what point is she making?
How did the official respond to Tendo’s speech?
What changes were made after the forum? List at least three new proposals.
Think and Discuss
Tendo said, “Justice is not automatic. It must be lived.” What do you think she means? Do you agree?
Biko said, “Representation is more than attendance. It is presence.” What is the difference between just being there and truly having presence?
The elder said he was losing freedom under the new system. The official said the system ensures fairer taxes. Who do you think has a stronger argument, and why?
Do you think young people should be allowed to speak at community meetings? Why or why not?
The researcher said, “Age doesn’t limit insight.” Can you think of a time when a young person had an idea that was better than an adult’s?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find each of these Unit 7 vocabulary words in the text. Read the sentence they appear in, then write your own sentence using the same word.
congregation
administration
representation
declaration
capitalism / capitalist
socialism / socialist
communism / communist
distribution
inflation
illustration
restoration
calculation
conduction
initiative
copyright
mechanization
conversion
preparation
expression
presence
commitment
excavation
pollination
allies
Grammar Spotlight: Past Simple vs Past Continuous in the Story
Look at these sentences from the story. Identify which verbs are in the Past Simple and which are in the Past Continuous:
“While some were talking, others were planting.”
“While systems changed, people still needed to eat.”
“While the declaration was made, some children missed school.”
“Tendo listened carefully. She scribbled notes.”
“Biko joined her, carrying a chart with calculations.”
Which sentences show two actions happening at the same time? Which show completed actions? Discuss with your AI tutor or write your answers.
Now You Try: Writing

Write a short speech (5–8 sentences) that you would give at a community meeting. Choose a topic you care about: fairness at school, helping the environment, or supporting your community during a natural disaster. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit.
Imagine you are Biko. Write a letter to the administration, asking them to review the students’ food-sharing plan. Use formal language and at least two sentences with while, when, or until.

Write a diary entry from Tendo’s point of view on the evening after the forum. How does she feel? What does she hope will happen next? Use the Past Simple and Past Continuous to describe the day’s events.

Read slowly. When you meet a vocabulary word (shown in bold), say it out loud and think about what it means.

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 7: Nature’s Power
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 7. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example sentence out loud, and then do the quick practice. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you pronounce the words correctly.
Group 1: Nature, Science, and Technology
Practise these words
What is conduction? Explain it in your own words, then give an example from the kitchen.
Why is pollination important for plants? Write one sentence about bees and pollination.
Draw a simple illustration of the water cycle. Label it and write one sentence explaining your drawing.
Say this sentence aloud: “The mechanization of rescue work helps teams clear roads more quickly after a flood.”
Group 2: Government, Economy, and Systems
Practise these words
In your own words, explain the difference between capitalism and socialism.
What does the administration do at your school? Write one sentence.
Why is copyright important for artists and writers? Finish this sentence: “Copyright protects…”
If you were in charge of a country after a big flood, which system (capitalism, socialism, or communism) would you use to share food? Why? Write two sentences.
Group 3: Planning, Change, and Action
Practise these words
What kind of preparation would your family do before a big storm? Write two sentences.
Give an example of a calculation you use in everyday life.
Write a sentence using the word distribution about sharing food after a flood.
What is inflation? Can you think of something that costs more now than it did a few years ago?
Describe a conversion: “The ______ of ______ into ______ was…”
Show initiative! Write one sentence about something helpful you could start at school without being asked.
Group 4: Community, Voice, and Expression
Practise these words
Describe a time when a congregation of people came together in your community. What were they there for?
Write a declaration for your class. For example: “We declare that every student has the right to…”
Who are your allies? Write one sentence about someone who helps you.
What kind of expression do you show when you are happy? When you are nervous? Write two sentences.
Why is presence important? Finish: “Sometimes the most helpful thing is…”

tables: The way heat or electricity moves through a material.
When you touch a metal spoon in a hot pot, the heat travels through the spoon by conduction.
The process of transferring pollen from one flower to another to help plants make seeds.
Bees help with pollination by moving pollen between flowers.
The layer of air above the weather layer, where planes fly and the ozone layer is found.
The airplane flew high in the stratosphere, above the clouds.
The process of digging in the ground to find things like fossils or old buildings.
The workers started the excavation to find ancient artefacts buried in the ground.
The process of using machines to do work instead of people.
The mechanization of farming helps farmers grow more food with less effort.
A picture or drawing that helps explain something.
The book has many illustrations that show what the story is about.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the nature and science words?

Can you help me draw and label a water cycle illustration?
An economic system where people own businesses and make money by selling goods and services.
In capitalism, people can start their own shops and sell things to earn money.
A person who uses money to make more money by starting businesses or investing.
The capitalist opened a new store to sell toys and make a profit.
A system where the government controls important things like schools and hospitals to help everyone equally.
In a socialism system, everyone shares resources so that no one is too poor or too rich.
A person who believes that everyone should share things equally and that the government should help make this happen.
My uncle is a socialist, and he thinks everyone should have the same chance to succeed.
A system where the government owns all property and everyone shares what they need.
In a country with communism, people work together and share things equally.
A person who believes in a system where the government owns everything and shares it equally among people.
In history class, we learned about a communist leader who wanted to make sure everyone had the same amount of food.
The group of people who manage or organise a school, company, or government.
The administration at our school helps to make important decisions.
A law that protects someone’s creative work so that others cannot use it without permission.
The artist has copyright on her paintings, so no one can copy them without asking.

The process of getting ready for something.
We need to do some preparation before the school play starts.
A way to find out a number or answer by using maths.
We did a calculation to find out how many apples we had in total.
The way something is shared or spread out among a group of people or things.
The distribution of the toys was fair, so every child got one.
When prices for things go up, so you need more money to buy the same things.
Because of inflation, the price of a candy bar is higher than it was last year.
The process of changing something from one form to another.
The conversion of the old school into a library made it a great place to read.
The process of bringing something back to its original condition or making it better.
The restoration of the old building made it look beautiful again.
The ability to start something on your own without being told.
She showed great initiative by starting a recycling programme at school.
A promise to do something or to be loyal to someone or something.
She made a commitment to practise the piano every day.

A way of showing or describing something, like a picture or a model; also, speaking on behalf of others.
