paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 8: Entertainment and Media
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Four grammar lessons

This file contains four grammar lessons for Unit 8. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice exercises, and then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Participle Adjectives (-ed and -ing)
What the rule means and why it matters

Participle adjectives are words that come from verbs and work as describing words (adjectives). They help us add detail and emotion to our sentences. Understanding the difference between the -ed form and the -ing form is one of the most important grammar skills at this level, because getting it wrong can change the meaning of your sentence completely.

For example, there is a big difference between saying "I am bored" (I feel boredom) and "I am boring" (I cause boredom in other people). The -ed form describes the person who feels the emotion. The -ing form describes the thing that causes the emotion.
How to form and use it (step by step)
Follow these steps:
Identify the verb: Start with a verb that describes a feeling or state. For example: excite, bore, frighten, inspire, amaze, confuse, tire, surprise, disappoint, interest.
Choose the form: If you are describing the person who feels, add -ed (or the past participle for irregular verbs). If you are describing the thing that causes the feeling, add -ing.
Place the adjective: Put it either before the noun ("an exciting film") or after a linking verb like *be*, *feel*, *seem*, *look* ("The film was exciting").
Examples — see the pattern
Study these pairs carefully. The first sentence uses -ing (the thing that causes the feeling). The second uses -ed (the person who feels).
The documentary was fascinating. → Jabari was fascinated by the documentary.
The news was surprising. → Everyone was surprised by the news.
The commercial was annoying. → The viewers were annoyed by the commercial.
The story of the Monk was inspiring. → The students felt inspired by the story.
The ritual description was confusing. → The historians were confused by the description.
The long lecture was tiring. → The audience felt tired after the lecture.
The ending of the film was disappointing. → We were disappointed by the ending.
The scene in the cemetery was frightening. → The children were frightened by the scene.
Variations and exceptions
Most participle adjectives follow the -ed / -ing pattern, but be aware of these points:
Some past participles are irregular: the past participle of "break" is "broken" (not "breaked"), so we say "a broken vase". Other common irregular forms: written, spoken, stolen, forgotten, chosen.
Some -ed adjectives do not have a matching -ing form in common use. For example, we say "a crowded room" but we do not usually say "a crowding room".
Do not confuse participle adjectives with ordinary adjectives that happen to end in -ed or -ing (e.g. "a painted wall" describes a wall that someone painted, not a wall that feels paint).
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using -ing when you mean -ed.
Wrong: "I was boring during the lesson." (This says you are a boring person!)
Right: "I was bored during the lesson." (You felt boredom.)
Mistake 2: Using -ed when you mean -ing.
Wrong: "The film was very interested." (Films cannot feel interest.)
Right: "The film was very interesting." (The film causes interest.)
Mistake 3: Wrong placement.
Wrong: "It was a ride thrilling."
Right: "It was a thrilling ride." (Adjective before the noun.)
Quick memory trick
Ask yourself: "Who or what has the feeling?"
If it is a person (I, he, she, they, the children), use -ed: "The children were excited."
If it is a thing (the film, the news, the game, the book), use -ing: "The film was exciting."
Practice 1: Fill in the blank
Choose the correct form of the word in brackets.
The ______ (excite) news made everyone smile.
The children looked ______ (bore) during the long meeting.
The ______ (inspire) teacher encouraged her students to dream big.
After the scary movie, I felt ______ (frighten).
The documentary about the dynasty was very ______ (interest).
We were completely ______ (amaze) by the special effects.
The ______ (confuse) instructions made the task harder.
She felt ______ (disappoint) when the show was cancelled.
Lesson 2: Past Simple Passive
What the rule means and why it matters

The past simple passive is a way to talk about actions that happened in the past, focusing on what happened rather than who did it. This is important because in many situations — especially in historical writing, news reports, and film reviews — we care more about the action itself than the person who performed it.
For example, in the sentence "The admiral was honoured in a public ritual," we focus on the admiral and the honour he received, not on who organised the ceremony.
How to form it (step by step)
Identify the verb in the active voice: "The committee honoured the admiral."
Change the subject to the receiver of the action: The admiral becomes the subject.
Use the past simple form of "to be": "was" for singular, "were" for plural.
Add the past participle of the main verb: honoured, discovered, directed, filmed.
Combine: "The admiral was honoured."
If you want to say who did the action, add by: "The admiral was honoured by the committee."
Examples
Active: The team discovered the scroll. → Passive: The scroll was discovered by the team.
Active: They filmed the documentary in Nairobi. → Passive: The documentary was filmed in Nairobi.
Active: The Confederacy arrested the Monk. → Passive: The Monk was arrested by the Confederacy.
Active: People watched the film around the world. → Passive: The film was watched around the world.
Active: The scholars chose the disciples. → Passive: The disciples were chosen by the scholars.
Variations
Singular vs. plural: "The scroll was found." / "The scrolls were found."
Negative: Add not after was/were: "The admiral was not honoured."
Questions: Switch the order: "Was the admiral honoured?"
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Forgetting the past participle.
Wrong: "The film was direct by a young filmmaker."
Right: "The film was directed by a young filmmaker."
Mistake 2: Confusing was and were.
Wrong: "The letters was sent yesterday."
Right: "The letters were sent yesterday."
Quick memory trick
Think of the past simple passive as: "It happened to someone." Ask yourself: "Who received the action?" That person or thing becomes the subject of your passive sentence.
Practice 2: Rewrite in the passive
Rewrite each sentence in the past simple passive.
The team discovered the ancient cemetery.
The curator displayed the scroll at the exhibit.
Millions of people watched the documentary.
The Confederacy destroyed the Monk's teachings.
A young filmmaker directed the award-winning film.
Lesson 3: Adverbs of Time and Frequency
What the rule means and why it matters

Adverbs of time and frequency tell us when or how often something happens. They are essential for describing routines, habits, and schedules clearly. When you write about media habits (how often you watch films, when you read) or describe historical events (rituals that happened daily), you need these adverbs.
The main adverbs of frequency
From most to least frequent:
always (100%): "They always pray before each ritual."
usually (about 80%): "We usually watch a film on Fridays."
often (about 60%): "She often reads before bed."
sometimes (about 40%): "I sometimes listen to podcasts."
rarely (about 10%): "He rarely watches cartoons now."
never (0%): "He never missed a revival."
Where to place them
The position of the adverb depends on the verb:
Before the main verb: "They usually pray." / "She often reads."
After the verb "to be": "She is always happy." / "The film was never shown."
At the beginning or end for emphasis: "Sometimes, we go to the cinema." / "We go to the cinema sometimes."
Adverbs of time
These tell us when something happens:
yesterday, today, tomorrow: "We watched a documentary yesterday."
last week / month / year: "The film was released last year."
already, still, yet: "I have already seen that film."
then, soon, later: "Then the credits rolled."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Wrong position.
Wrong: "He plays football often."
Right: "He often plays football."
Mistake 2: Using "never" with a negative verb.
Wrong: "I don't never watch horror films." (Double negative.)
Right: "I never watch horror films." OR "I don't ever watch horror films."
Quick memory trick
Remember the order with the phrase "A.U.O.S.R.N." — think of it as "Aunty Usually Offers Some Really Nice" food:
Always, Usually, Often, Sometimes, Rarely, Never
Practice 3: Fill in the blank
Choose the correct adverb from the box: *always, sometimes, usually, never, often*
She ______ goes to the library after school. (most of the time)
They ______ celebrate their birthdays together. (every single time)
I ______ forget my homework. (0% of the time)
He ______ watches documentaries on weekends. (frequently)
We ______ have pizza on Fridays. (about half the time)
Lesson 4: Imperatives in Instructions
What the rule means and why it matters

An imperative is a sentence that gives a command, instruction, or request. Imperatives are important for writing clear directions — in recipes, instructions, safety notices, and even in archeological reports like the one Jabari's class wrote. When you write instructions, you use the base form of the verb without a subject.
How to form imperatives
Start with the base form of the verb: add, show, draw, write, read, document.
Do not use a subject: Just say the verb directly. Instead of "You add symbols," say "Add symbols."
For negative imperatives, add "don't" before the verb: "Don't forget to add captions."
For polite imperatives, add "please": "Please show the timeline."
Examples
"Add symbols to your map."
"Show the dynasty timeline."
"Document the Monk's characteristics."
"Don't touch the artefacts."
"Please close the door quietly."
"Compare spiritual and political causes of conflict."
"Include symbols from both Catholic and pagan tradition."
"Write a public report using a historical tone."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the subject "you".
Wrong: "You add symbols to your map."
Right: "Add symbols to your map."
Mistake 2: Using the -ing form instead of the base form.
Wrong: "Adding symbols to your map."
Right: "Add symbols to your map."
Quick memory trick
Imperatives are like a coach giving directions to a team. The coach does not say "You run!" — the coach just says "Run!" Short, clear, direct.
Practice 4: Write imperative instructions
You are writing instructions for a classmate who is visiting an archeological exhibit. Write five imperative sentences. The first one is done for you.
Look at the map on the wall and find the burial site.
(Write your own: tell them to read something.)
(Write your own: tell them NOT to do something.)
(Write your own: tell them to draw or write something.)
(Write your own: tell them to compare two things at the exhibit.)
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Participle Adjectives)
1. exciting  2. bored  3. inspiring  4. frightened  5. interesting  6. amazed  7. confusing  8. disappointed
Practice 2 (Past Simple Passive)
The ancient cemetery was discovered by the team.
The scroll was displayed at the exhibit by the curator.
The documentary was watched by millions of people.
The Monk's teachings were destroyed by the Confederacy.
The award-winning film was directed by a young filmmaker.
Practice 3 (Adverbs of Time and Frequency)
1. usually  2. always  3. never  4. often  5. sometimes
Practice 4 (Imperatives)
Answers will vary. Check that each sentence starts with the base form of a verb and does not include the subject "you". Negative imperatives should start with "Don't" or "Do not".

paragraphs: Unit 8: Entertainment and Media
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 8: Entertainment and Media. In today's world, entertainment is everywhere. We watch films and documentaries on screens, listen to podcasts and radio shows, follow stories through books and graphic novels, and even create our own content. But how much do we think about the media we consume? How does a film tell a story? What makes a documentary different from a drama? Why do some commercials stay in our minds long after we have seen them?

In this unit, you will explore the world of entertainment and media through reading, writing, speaking, and listening. You will read about how stories are told through film and television, and you will think critically about the media around you. You will learn important grammar skills, including participle adjectives (words like *excited* and *exciting*, *bored* and *boring*), the past simple passive (sentences like "The film was directed by a young Somali filmmaker"), and adverbs of time and frequency (words like *usually*, *often*, *always*, and *never*). These grammar tools will help you write a confident film review by the end of the unit.

Alongside this lesson, you will read the story "The Scroll of Nuru", a mystery about a young boy named Jabari who discovers an ancient scroll buried in the highlands of a fictional East African region. The story explores themes of belief, legacy, memory, and how people choose to remember the past. It will challenge you to think about power, truth, and the stories we tell about history.

Entertainment is not only about relaxation. It shapes how we see the world. A good film, a powerful documentary, or even a well-made commercial can change how people think and feel. In many East African and Somali communities, storytelling has always been at the heart of culture, from poetry and oral history to modern cinema. This unit helps you become a more thoughtful viewer, reader, and creator of media.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand texts about entertainment and media, including film, television, and storytelling traditions.
Use participle adjectives correctly, choosing between the -ed form (how someone feels) and the -ing form (what causes the feeling).
Form sentences in the past simple passive to describe events in film and history (e.g. "The documentary was filmed in Mogadishu").
Use adverbs of time and frequency to describe how often things happen (e.g. *usually*, *always*, *never*, *often*).
Write a structured film review with an introduction, summary, opinion, and recommendation.
Use imperatives to give clear instructions (e.g. "Watch this film", "Do not miss the final scene").
Speak clearly when presenting a film review or discussing media habits.
Think critically about screen time, media messages, and storytelling techniques.
Understand and enjoy the story "The Scroll of Nuru" and explore themes of legacy, belief, and memory.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn the unit words a few at a time and say the example sentences aloud.
Work through the Grammar file. Focus especially on participle adjectives, as they will appear throughout the unit.
Read the Story file, "The Scroll of Nuru", and answer the comprehension and discussion questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment to see what you have achieved.
Key Vocabulary

Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find the full word list with definitions and examples in your Vocabulary companion file. For now, read through these words and make sure you can say each one aloud.
Entertainment and Media Words
History and Society Words
Reading
Before you read
The passage below is about how storytelling has changed over time, from ancient oral traditions to modern films and digital media. As you read, think about these questions:
How is storytelling in films similar to storytelling around a fire?
What role does media play in your own life?
What makes a film or documentary interesting rather than boring?
Screens, Stories, and the Art of Telling Tales

Long before anyone owned a television or a smartphone, stories were told around fires. In the suburbs of ancient cities and in the open deserts of East Africa, people gathered to listen. A storyteller would stand, clear her voice, and begin. The audience was captivated. No one moved. No one spoke. The story held them like a spell.

Those early stories were the first form of entertainment. They taught lessons, preserved history, and brought communities together. A tale about a clever fox might teach children about honesty. A poem about a brave admiral might remind a whole town of its legacy. These were not just idle pastimes — they were the glue that held societies together.

Today, our stories come through screens. A film director is, in many ways, a modern storyteller. She decides how the tale unfolds, which characters we care about, and what feelings we carry away. Think of the last film you watched. Were you excited by the action? Were you moved by a sad moment? Were you frightened by a sudden twist? Those feelings did not happen by accident. The director, the actors, the music composer, and even the cartoonist who designed the opening titles all worked together to make you feel that way.

Documentaries tell true stories. A documentary filmmaker might travel to an archeological site in the highlands, or follow a family through Ramadan, or show how a school in a spartan building with few resources produces inspiring students. Unlike a drama, a documentary does not invent characters. Instead, it uses real people, real places, and real events to make us think.

But not all media is created equally. Commercials, for example, are designed to persuade. They use bright colours, catchy music, and exciting images to make us want to buy something. A discerning viewer — someone who thinks carefully about what they watch — can spot the difference between a story that informs and a message that tries to sell.

In East Africa and across the Somali diaspora, a revival of local filmmaking has brought new voices to the screen. Young directors are telling stories about their own communities, their faith, their families, and their dreams. Some of these films have been shown at festivals in Paris and other international cities. They prove that the ancient tradition of storytelling is not dying. It is simply finding new forms.

Whether it is a grandmother telling a tale by lamplight, a cartoonist drawing a comic strip for a newspaper, or a filmmaker recording a documentary about the characteristics of a vanishing craft, the human need to tell and hear stories remains eternal. The tools change, but the heart of entertainment stays the same: a good story, well told, that makes us feel something real.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage to find the evidence.
What was the earliest form of entertainment described in the passage?
According to the passage, what are three things that early stories did for communities?
The passage says a film director is "a modern storyteller." In what ways is a director like an ancient storyteller?
What is the difference between a documentary and a drama, according to the text?
Why does the passage say that commercials are different from other forms of media?
What is happening with filmmaking in East Africa and the Somali diaspora?
The passage ends by saying "the heart of entertainment stays the same." What does this mean?
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the passage. Use the sentence around them to work out their meaning, then check with your Vocabulary file.
captivated (paragraph 1) — What do you think this means? What clue words help you?
spartan (paragraph 4) — The text says "a spartan building with few resources." What does *spartan* suggest about the building?
discerning (paragraph 5) — The passage defines this word for you. What does a discerning viewer do?
revival (paragraph 6) — What is being revived? How do you know?
Deeper Thinking
These questions ask you to think beyond what the passage says directly. There is no single right answer, but you should explain your thinking clearly.
The passage compares ancient storytelling to modern filmmaking. Do you think anything is lost when stories move from spoken words to screens? Explain your answer.
The passage says commercials "use bright colours, catchy music, and exciting images to make us want to buy something." Think of a commercial you have seen. How did it try to persuade you?
Why do you think it matters that young East African filmmakers are telling their own stories, rather than having outsiders tell stories about them?
The author writes that a good story makes us "feel something real." What is the most moving or exciting story you have ever seen or heard? What made it powerful?
Grammar Focus: Participle Adjectives

In the reading passage, you met words like *excited*, *exciting*, *frightened*, *boring*, and *moved*. These are called participle adjectives. They come from verbs but work as describing words. Understanding participle adjectives is one of the most important grammar skills in this unit, because they help you write vivid, precise descriptions — especially when you are reviewing a film or describing your reaction to a story.
What are participle adjectives?
A participle adjective is a word that started as a verb and turned into an adjective. There are two forms:
The -ed form (past participle): describes how someone feels. Example: "I was excited about the film."
The -ing form (present participle): describes the thing that causes the feeling. Example: "The film was exciting."

This is one of the trickiest areas of English grammar for learners, but the rule is actually simple once you understand it. The -ed adjective always points inward, toward the person experiencing the feeling. The -ing adjective always points outward, toward the thing producing the feeling.
The key rule
Think of it this way:
-ed = the person who feels → "Jabari was fascinated by the scroll." (Jabari is the one feeling fascination.)
-ing = the thing that causes the feeling → "The scroll was fascinating." (The scroll causes the fascination.)
Here are more examples to make the pattern clear:
The documentary was boring. → I was bored by the documentary.
The news about the revival was surprising. → We were surprised by the news.
The commercial was annoying. → The viewers were annoyed by the commercial.
The story of the Monk of Nuru was inspiring. → Jabari felt inspired by the Monk’s story.
The scene in the cemetery was frightening. → The children were frightened by the scene.
Common participle adjective pairs
Here are ten pairs you should learn. Say each pair aloud and notice the difference:
Where to place participle adjectives
Participle adjectives can go in two places in a sentence:
Before the noun: "It was an exciting film." / "The bored audience left early."
After a linking verb (be, feel, seem, look, become): "The film was exciting." / "I felt bored."

Both positions are correct. Using the adjective before the noun is common in writing ("an inspiring documentary"), while using it after a linking verb is common in speech ("The documentary was inspiring").
A common mistake and how to avoid it
Many learners mix up the two forms. Here is the mistake and the fix:
Mistake: "I was boring during the lesson." ✘
This actually means you are the thing causing boredom — you are saying that you are a boring person! What you probably mean is:
Fix: "I was bored during the lesson." ✔
Similarly:
Mistake: "The film was very interested." ✘
Fix: "The film was very interesting." ✔ (The film causes interest.)
Memory trick: Ask yourself, "Am I describing a person who has a feeling, or a thing that creates a feeling?" Person = -ed. Thing = -ing.
Grammar Practice
Practice 1: Choose the correct form
Read each sentence and choose the correct participle adjective. Write the full sentence in your notebook.
The documentary about archeological sites was (fascinated / fascinating).
After watching the long film, the children felt (tired / tiring).
The (excited / exciting) audience cheered when the hero appeared.
We were (amazed / amazing) by the special effects in the movie.
The plot of the film was very (confused / confusing).
Jabari was (inspired / inspiring) by the Monk of Nuru's story.
The (disappointed / disappointing) fans left the cinema early.
Have you ever seen a truly (frightened / frightening) scene in a film?
Practice 2: Complete the sentences
Use the correct -ed or -ing form of the verb in brackets.
The new Somali film was very ______. I was completely ______. (move)
The commercial was so ______ that everyone felt ______ by it. (annoy)
My little sister was ______ by the cartoon. She said it was really ______. (amuse)
The story of the dynasty was ______. All the students were ______ in it. (interest)
I find horror films ______. I always feel ______ afterwards. (frighten)
Practice 3: Spot and fix the mistakes
Each sentence has an error with a participle adjective. Find the mistake and rewrite the sentence correctly.
The film was very bored. (The film causes boredom, so it should be …)
I was very interesting in the documentary. (You are the person feeling interest, so …)
The children looked very exciting before the show. (The children are the ones feeling excitement, so …)
It was a very tired journey across the desert. (The journey causes tiredness, so …)
Answers
Practice 1: 1. fascinating  2. tired  3. excited  4. amazed  5. confusing  6. inspired  7. disappointed  8. frightening
Practice 2: 1. moving / moved  2. annoying / annoyed  3. amused / amusing  4. interesting / interested  5. frightening / frightened
Practice 3: 1. The film was very boring.  2. I was very interested in the documentary.  3. The children looked very excited before the show.  4. It was a very tiring journey across the desert.
More Grammar for This Unit
Past Simple Passive — a quick reminder

In Unit 7 you met the past simple. In this unit, you will also use the past simple passive. The passive is useful when you want to focus on what happened rather than who did it. This is very common in film reviews and historical writing.
How to form it: subject + was/were + past participle
"The film was directed by a young Somali filmmaker."
"The ancient scrolls were discovered in a cemetery near the ridge."
"The Monk of Nuru was crucified by the Confederacy."
"The documentary was watched by millions of people around the world."

Use was for singular subjects (he, she, it, the film) and were for plural subjects (they, the scrolls, the actors). In a film review, you might write: "The film was released in 2024. It was produced in Nairobi and was shown at several international festivals."
You will practise the past simple passive in more detail in your Grammar file.
Adverbs of Time and Frequency — a quick reminder
Adverbs of frequency tell us how often something happens. They are useful for talking about media habits, such as how often you watch films or listen to music.
The most common adverbs of frequency, from most to least, are:
always (100%) → "I always watch the news with my family."
usually (about 80%) → "We usually watch a film on Fridays."
often (about 60%) → "She often reads before bed."
sometimes (about 40%) → "I sometimes listen to podcasts."
rarely (about 10%) → "He rarely watches cartoons now."
never (0%) → "We never eat during a good film!"

Where to put them: Place the adverb before the main verb ("I usually watch") but after the verb *to be* ("She is always late"). You can also put *sometimes* at the start of a sentence for emphasis: "Sometimes, we go to the cinema."
You will practise adverbs of frequency in more detail in your Grammar file.
Writing Workshop: Writing a Film Review

A film review is a piece of writing in which you share your opinion about a film. Good film reviews do not just say "I liked it" or "It was boring." They explain what the film is about, how it tells its story, and why you would or would not recommend it. Film reviews are an important part of media literacy because they help other people decide what to watch.
The structure of a film review
A well-organised film review usually has four parts:

Introduction: Give the film’s title, the director’s name (if you know it), the year it was made, and the genre (comedy, drama, documentary, animation, etc.). Write one sentence that gives your overall opinion.
Summary: Describe what happens in the film in 3–4 sentences. Do not give away the ending! Use the past simple passive where it fits ("The story was set in a small coastal town").

Your opinion: This is the most important part. Explain what you liked and what you did not like. Use participle adjectives to describe your feelings and the film’s qualities ("The acting was convincing", "I felt moved by the final scene", "Some parts were confusing").
Recommendation: Would you tell a friend to watch this film? Who would enjoy it most? End with a strong sentence.
Useful language for film reviews
Here are phrases you can use in each section of your review. Practise saying them aloud.
Introduction phrases:
"The film ______ was directed by ______ and released in ______."
"It is a [genre] film set in ______."
"Overall, I found this film [participle adjective: exciting / disappointing / inspiring]."
Summary phrases:
"The story follows [character] who ______."
"The film is set in ______ and takes place during ______."
"Without giving too much away, the main conflict is ______."
Opinion phrases:
"What I enjoyed most was ______ because ______."
"I was [moved / surprised / impressed] by ______."
"One weakness was that ______ felt [confusing / rushed / boring]."
"The acting was [convincing / powerful / disappointing]."
"The music added to the [exciting / tense / emotional] atmosphere."
Recommendation phrases:
"I would recommend this film to anyone who enjoys ______."
"This film is best for viewers who are interested in ______."
"I would not recommend this film because ______."
"If you liked [similar film], you will love this one."
Model film review
Read this example review. Notice how it uses the four-part structure, participle adjectives, and the past simple passive.
Your turn: Write your own film review
Choose a film, documentary, TV show, or even a story you know well. Write a review of 150–200 words using the four-part structure. Before you write, plan:
Choose your film. It can be any film, show, or documentary you have seen. If you have not seen many films, you may review the story "The Scroll of Nuru" as if it were a film.
Plan your four paragraphs. Use the phrases above as a starting guide.
Use at least three participle adjectives (e.g. *exciting*, *bored*, *inspired*, *moving*, *disappointed*).
Use at least one past simple passive sentence (e.g. "The film was released in...").
Use at least one adverb of frequency in your review (e.g. "I rarely watch documentaries, but this one...").
Read your review aloud and check: Does it make sense? Would a reader want to see this film?
Writing Extension: Create a storyboard

A storyboard is a series of drawings that shows the main scenes of a film or story. Filmmakers use storyboards to plan their films before they start filming. Each box in a storyboard contains a simple drawing and a short caption explaining what happens.
Choose a scene from "The Scroll of Nuru" and create a storyboard with six boxes. In each box:
Draw a simple sketch of what happens (stick figures are fine).
Write a one-sentence caption below each drawing using the past simple passive: e.g. "The scroll was discovered in a jar." or "Jabari was told to watch quietly."
Include at least one participle adjective in your captions: e.g. "The amazed team gathered around the jar."
Listening & Speaking
Listening: A conversation about screen time
Ask your AI tutor to read this conversation aloud, or read it with a partner. Then answer the questions below.
Halima: Yusuf, how much time do you usually spend watching things on a screen each day?
Yusuf: Hmm, I think about two hours. I usually watch something after school, and sometimes I watch a film with my family on Fridays.
Halima: That does not sound too bad. I always watch the news with my parents during dinner. But my little brother never stops watching cartoons! My mum is worried about his screen time.
Yusuf: My mum says screen time is fine if you also read books, play outside, and do your homework. She says the problem is when screens become the only entertainment.

Halima: I agree. I think the most important thing is balance. I was really interested in a documentary I watched last week about how films are made. It was so fascinating that I forgot I was learning! Not all screen time is the same.
Yusuf: Exactly. A boring cartoon is not the same as an inspiring documentary. It depends on what you watch, not just how long you watch.
Listening questions
How much screen time does Yusuf have each day?
What does Halima always do during dinner?
Why is Halima’s mum worried about her little brother?
What does Yusuf’s mum say about screen time?
What is the difference between a boring cartoon and an inspiring documentary, according to Yusuf?
Find two participle adjectives in the conversation and say whether each one is an -ed form or an -ing form.
Find two adverbs of frequency in the conversation. What do they tell you about how often something happens?
Speaking 1: Present your film review
Once you have written your film review from the Writing Workshop, practise presenting it aloud. Imagine you are on a radio show recommending films to listeners.
Stand or sit up straight. Speak clearly and at a steady pace.
Do not just read your review word for word. Look up and speak naturally.
Emphasise your opinion words: say "I was really moved" with feeling, not in a flat voice.
End with your recommendation and a strong closing sentence.
Record yourself if you can. Listen back and ask: Did I sound convincing? Would the listener want to watch this film?
Speaking 2: Debate — Is screen time good or bad?

With a partner, your AI tutor, or a family member, have a short debate. One person argues that screen time is mostly good for young people, and the other argues that it is mostly harmful. Each speaker should:
Give at least two reasons to support their opinion.
Use adverbs of frequency ("Children usually learn from documentaries", "Many young people rarely go outside because of screens").
Use participle adjectives to describe feelings and effects ("Watching too much can be tiring", "Educational programmes are inspiring").
Listen to the other person and respond to their points politely.
Speaking 3: Interview a filmmaker (role-play)
Imagine you are a journalist interviewing a filmmaker about their latest documentary. Work with your AI tutor or a partner. The journalist asks questions and the filmmaker answers.
Questions for the journalist to ask:
"What is your documentary about?"
"Where was it filmed?" (Use past simple passive: "It was filmed in…")
"What was the most challenging part of making the documentary?"
"Who would you recommend this documentary to?"
"What do you hope viewers will feel after watching it?" (Use a participle adjective: "I hope they feel inspired…")
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you at any time. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: "I do not understand participle adjectives. Can you explain them a different way?"
Practise grammar: "Can you give me ten sentences where I choose between -ed and -ing?"
Get writing feedback: "Can you read my film review and check my grammar and vocabulary?"
Prepare for speaking: "Can you listen to me present my review and tell me how to improve?"
Review the story: "Can you ask me questions about 'The Scroll of Nuru' to check I understood it?"
Practise vocabulary: "Can you test me on the Unit 8 words?"
A Simple Daily Routine
Check Your Learning
Look at each statement below. Tick "Yes" if you can do it confidently, or "Not yet" if you need more practice. For every "Not yet", ask your AI tutor for extra help.
I can read a passage about entertainment and media and answer comprehension questions.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use participle adjectives correctly, choosing between -ed (how I feel) and -ing (what causes the feeling).   Yes □   Not yet □
I can form sentences in the past simple passive (e.g. "The film was directed by…").   Yes □   Not yet □
I can use adverbs of frequency to describe how often something happens.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can write a film review with an introduction, summary, opinion, and recommendation.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can present a film review aloud with clear expression and confidence.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can discuss screen time and media habits using evidence and examples.   Yes □   Not yet □
I understand the story "The Scroll of Nuru" and can talk about its themes of legacy, belief, and memory.   Yes □   Not yet □
I can create a storyboard with captions that use passive voice and participle adjectives.   Yes □   Not yet □
Getting Ready for Unit 9: Amazing Arts
Make sure you know all the Unit 8 words on your Word Wall, especially the grammar terms (participle adjective, passive voice, adverb of frequency).
In Unit 9 you will explore art — public art, recycled instruments, poetry, and creative expression. Think about what art you see in your community.
You will learn about indefinite pronouns (someone, anything, nobody), adjective order, and revisit the present perfect.
Tell your AI tutor: "What will I learn in Unit 9? Can you get me ready?"

Well done for completing Unit 8. Entertainment and media surround us every day. Now you have the skills to think critically about the stories you watch and hear, and the tools to share your own opinions clearly and confidently. Keep reading, keep watching thoughtfully, and keep writing.

🎬  Model Review: "Light of the Gu"
"Light of the Gu" is a short drama that was produced in Hargeisa in 2023. It was directed by Fadumo Abdi, a young Somali filmmaker. Overall, I found this film deeply moving and beautifully made.
The story follows twelve-year-old Samira, who lives with her grandmother in a small town during the Gu rains. When the river floods, Samira must decide whether to save her grandmother's most treasured possession or help her neighbours. The film was shot entirely on location, and the rain scenes were incredibly realistic.
What I enjoyed most was the acting. The girl who played Samira gave a convincing and emotional performance. I was particularly moved by the final scene, where she stands in the rain and smiles. The music was also effective — it made the tense moments feel even more frightening.
One small weakness was the middle section, which felt a little slow and repetitive. Some viewers might find those scenes boring.
I would recommend this film to anyone who enjoys realistic stories about everyday courage. It is especially suited to young viewers who are interested in East African culture.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 8: Entertainment and Media
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "The Scroll of Nuru"
The Scroll of Nuru

In the dusty highlands of Umbara, beyond the modern suburbs and into the foothills of the great ridge, an unusual archeological site was uncovered. It began with a single shovel hitting stone, and ended with a full team arriving, their equipment buzzing with electrical hum.

Fourteen-year-old Jabari stood behind the caution tape, wide-eyed. He was not supposed to be there. But his uncle, a hospitable and generous site guard, had whispered, "Only if you watch quietly and don't touch anything."
"I promise," Jabari had said.
What they found would change the region's understanding of history.
The object was a scroll, sealed in a jar, hidden deep in what seemed to be a cemetery from an ancient dynasty. The symbols were strange — part pagan, part familiar. The experts argued.
"A lost monarchy, possibly pre-feudal," said one scholar.
"Maybe Catholic influence mixed with native rituals," said another.
"But it's been partially damaged," added a third. "The outer layer was churned by centuries of soil."
The writing team prepared a translation. "Some parts have been proclaimed, others must be reconstructed," the linguist explained.
They never expected the scroll to tell a story — with power, belief, and consequence.
A few lines had survived in full:

"In the Year of Eternal Fire, the Monk of Nuru led his disciples to the Valley of Memory. There, a ritual was performed daily beneath the Great Tree. But a revival led by the Confederacy sought to destroy them. The Monk was later crucified. Let the light remain."
Jabari read the lines again and again. Who was the Monk of Nuru? Why was he killed? Why did a group called the Confederacy fear him?
Back in town, the news spread quickly.
"The Monk's story could be proof of racial and spiritual conflict long before colonial rule," one historian said.
"A new legacy rises from the soil," wrote a reporter from Paris, where international universities tracked African finds closely.
Others warned of being too excited.
"Don't build utopias from old bones," warned an elder. "We must understand, not dream."
Jabari asked his history teacher, Ms. Bahati, "Was the Monk real?"
"We don't know," she said. "But history is often built from characteristics found in ruins, not full stories. That's why archeological work is so valuable."
"But he was crucified," Jabari whispered. "Why would someone do that to a monk?"
She looked at him kindly. "Sometimes, belief scares people. Especially if it threatens a system — like a feudal or royal one."
Weeks passed. A local exhibit was prepared.
"Show the dynasty timeline," the curator instructed. "Add captions to each symbol."
An artist painted the admiral of the old guard, towering over a scene of chaos. In the corner, the Monk stood calmly, eyes closed.
One placard read: "The Monk's teachings were never fully recorded. But his disciples preserved fragments of his rituals."
Another stated: "The revival was not just spiritual — it was political. The Confederacy opposed his influence."
Jabari stared at the map. One word whispered in his mind: terror.
He began writing in secret — building his own timeline of the Monk's last days.
Using adverbs like "usually" and "often", he described the rituals performed at dusk.
Using the past passive, he wrote: "The scroll was hidden during the Confederacy's march. The Monk was arrested. He was proclaimed a heretic. He was crucified."
He felt angry. But also fascinated.
Was the Monk punished because of his faith — or because his message threatened power?
Jabari shared his writing with his grandmother.
"This is strong," she said. "But be careful. Tell the story — but don't become partial."
"What do you mean?"
"Don't favour one side just because they seem gentle. Even peaceful leaders can make mistakes. That's what legacy means — it includes light and shadow."
The next week, Jabari and his classmates visited the site officially.
They were told: "You may not touch artefacts. Do not step inside the cemetery. Observe only."
A researcher gave instructions: "Write a public report. Use imperatives and historical tone."

Jabari's group wrote: "Document the Monk's characteristics. List the systems — monarchy, Confederacy, feudal rule. Compare spiritual and political causes of conflict. Include symbols from both Catholic and pagan tradition."
One student added, "Never forget what was done under orders."
Another added, "Include electrical sketches of the burial jars."
Jabari drew a map with branching arrows: belief → ritual → fear → terror → legacy.
Then came the debate.
"Was crucifixion ever justified?" the teacher asked.
"No!" one girl said. "It's cruel."
"But if the Monk was threatening the monarchy, they saw it as control," another said.
"But belief isn't a weapon," Jabari replied. "He was trying to help."
"Maybe," said another. "But we weren't there."
They were told to write essays using evidence.
Jabari's title: "Crucified Memory: Systems That Fear the Spirit."
In the final week, Jabari stood at the exhibit as a student guide. Tourists asked him questions.
"What makes this story special?" one woman asked.
Jabari answered, "Because we don't know everything. That makes it ours to explore."
He used clear verbs: proclaimed, crucified, discovered.
He pointed to the scroll. "That was partially burned — but its message lived."
One visitor from Merrimack, a teacher, told Jabari, "In our schools, we don't teach about African monks."
"You should," Jabari said. "The Monk was buried. But not forgotten."
On the last day, a candlelight vigil was held. It wasn't religious. Just respectful.
Students read aloud:
"This land remembers. The scroll returned. We are not disciples of history. We are its writers."
And under the fading baobab tree, Jabari whispered:
"Let the light remain."
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension questions
Where was the archeological site located?
What object was found at the site, and where was it hidden?
Why did the experts argue about the symbols on the scroll?
What did the surviving lines of the scroll describe?
Who was the Monk of Nuru, and what happened to him?
Why did the Confederacy oppose the Monk?
What did Jabari's grandmother mean when she said "Don't become partial"?
How did Jabari and his classmates contribute to the exhibit?
What was the title of Jabari's essay?
What did Jabari say when a visitor asked what made the story special?
Vocabulary in the story
Find these words in the story. For each one, write the sentence it appears in and explain what it means in that context.
archeological
dynasty
feudal
crucified
legacy
revival
partial
characteristics
ritual
eternal
Think and discuss
These questions ask you to think more deeply about the story. There is no single right answer. Explain your thinking carefully.
Jabari's grandmother says that legacy "includes light and shadow." What does she mean? Do you agree?
The scroll was damaged and only fragments survived. Why might an incomplete story be more powerful than a complete one?
The debate in the classroom shows students disagreeing respectfully. Why is it important to hear different opinions about history?
At the vigil, the students say: "We are not disciples of history. We are its writers." What does this mean? How can young people be "writers" of history?
How does this story connect to the theme of entertainment and media? Think about how stories are preserved and shared.
Grammar in the story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find examples of each:
Past simple passive: Find three sentences where the passive voice is used (e.g. "The scroll was hidden..."). Write them down.
Participle adjectives: Find at least two examples of participle adjectives in the story. Are they -ed or -ing forms?
Adverbs of frequency: Jabari uses "usually" and "often" in his writing. Find the sentence where these appear.
Imperatives: The researcher says "Write a public report. Use imperatives..." Find two more imperative sentences in the story.
Now You Try: Writing
Writing task 1: A diary entry
Imagine you are Jabari. Write a diary entry for the day you first saw the scroll at the archeological site. Include:
What you saw and how you felt (use participle adjectives: amazed, fascinated, frightened...).
At least one sentence in the past simple passive (e.g. "The jar was opened carefully").
At least one adverb of frequency (e.g. "I usually stay home after school, but today...").
Writing task 2: A public report
Jabari's class was told to "Write a public report. Use imperatives and historical tone." Write your own short public report (8–10 sentences) about the discovery. Use:
Imperatives to give instructions (e.g. "Examine the symbols carefully", "Do not touch the artefacts").
Past simple passive to describe what was found (e.g. "The scroll was discovered in a burial jar").
At least two vocabulary words from the unit (e.g. dynasty, monarchy, archeological, legacy).
tables:
📖  About this story
Genre: Historical mystery with speculative elements.
Theme: Belief, memory, legacy, and the echoes of lost civilisations.
Setting: A fictional East African region influenced by feudal monarchies and cultural revival.
Moral: A legacy is not only what is left behind, but what people choose to remember and learn from.
Read it slowly. When you meet a word from your Vocabulary list, notice it and say it aloud!

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor

"Can you check my public report and tell me if I used imperatives correctly?"

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 8: Entertainment and Media
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 8. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then try using the word in your own sentence. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Belief and Religion
Practise these words
Say each word three times. Then use it in a sentence of your own.
Which of these words might appear in a history book? Which might appear in a religious text?
The word eternal means "lasting forever". What things in your life feel eternal?
Group 2: History, Government and Conflict
Practise these words
Draw a timeline that shows: monarchy, dynasty, feudal system. Put them in the order you think they might appear in history.
Write a sentence using legacy about something your family has passed down to you.
The word admiral comes from the Arabic word "amir al-bahr" (commander of the sea). What does this tell you about the history of the word?
Group 3: Society and Places
Practise these words
Describe a place you know using at least two of these words (e.g. suburbs, spartan, hospitable).
The word spartan comes from the ancient Greek city of Sparta, where life was simple and tough. Can you think of a place that is spartan?
A commercial tries to persuade you to buy something. Think of a commercial you have seen. What made it memorable?
Group 4: Science, Discovery and Evidence
Practise these words
The word archeological is long. Break it into parts: ar-che-o-log-i-cal. Say each part, then say the whole word.
Write a sentence about an archeological discovery using at least one other word from this group.
What characteristics make a good documentary? Write three ideas.
Group 5: Places in the World
Practise these words
Find Paris on a map (or ask your AI tutor to describe where it is). Why do you think people call it "the City of Light"?
The name Merrimack comes from a Native American word. Ask your AI tutor what it means.
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!
ritual []   disciple []   eternal []   revival []   pagan []   Catholic []   crucified []
admiral []   dynasty []   monarchy []   feudal []   terror []   racial []   legacy []
suburbs []   partial []   commercial []   spartan []   hospitable []   utopia []
electrical []   archeological []   characteristics []   churn []   cemetery []   cartoonist []   cyanide []
Paris []   Merrimack []
tables: A special way of doing something that is done regularly or at certain times.
Every morning, my family has a ritual of eating breakfast together.
A person who follows and learns from a teacher or leader.
The disciple listened carefully to the wise words of his teacher.
The love between a parent and child is often seen as eternal.
A time when something becomes popular or important again.
The revival of the old film made many people want to watch it again.
A person who follows a religion that is not one of the main world religions, often believing in many gods.
In ancient times, some people were pagan and worshipped different gods.
A member of the Christian church that is led by the Pope.
My friend is Catholic, and she goes to church every Sunday.
To be killed by being nailed to a cross, a method used a long time ago.
In history class, we learned that some people were crucified for their beliefs.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you help me pronounce these words correctly?"
"Can you use 'ritual' and 'revival' in sentences about everyday life?"
"What is the difference between 'eternal' and 'everlasting'?"
A high-ranking officer in the navy who commands ships and sailors.
The admiral gave orders to the fleet before they set sail.
A series of rulers from the same family or group over a long time.
The Ming dynasty was a famous period in Chinese history.
A type of government where a king or queen rules the country.
In a monarchy, the king makes important decisions for the country.
Related to a system in the past where people worked for a lord in exchange for land and protection.
In a feudal system, farmers would give part of their crops to the lord.
A strong feeling of fear or being very scared.
The loud thunder filled the children with terror.
Related to a person's race or ethnic background.
The school celebrates racial diversity by learning about different cultures.
Something that is passed down from the past, like traditions, stories, or values.
My grandfather left me a legacy of stories about our family.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you explain the difference between a monarchy and a dynasty?"
"What is a feudal system? Can you explain it simply?"
"Can you tell me more about English words that come from Arabic?"
An area where people live that is outside of a big city, usually with houses and parks.
Many families choose to live in the suburbs because they are quieter than the city.
Not complete; only a part of something.
I only read a partial chapter of the book, so I do not know the whole story.
A short video or audio message that promotes a product or service.
I saw a funny commercial on TV about a new toy.
Simple and without comfort or luxury.
The spartan room had only a bed and a table, with no decorations.
Friendly and welcoming to guests or strangers.
The hospitable family invited us in for tea and biscuits.
A perfect place where everything is good and everyone is happy.
In her story, the main character dreams of a utopia where there is no war.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you explain what 'utopia' means and where the word comes from?"
"What is the opposite of 'spartan'?"
"Can you use 'partial' in two different sentences?"
Related to electricity or using electricity.
The electrical wires in the house help power the lights.
Related to the study of ancient cultures by looking at their buildings, tools, and objects.
The archeological team found old pottery while digging at the ancient site.
The special qualities or features that make someone or something different.
One of the characteristics of a good friend is being kind.
To mix or stir something very hard.
We need to churn the cream to make butter.
A place where people are buried after they die.
We visited the cemetery to pay our respects to my great-grandfather.
A person who creates cartoons, which are funny drawings or animations.
The cartoonist drew a funny picture of a cat wearing a hat.
A very poisonous chemical that can be harmful or deadly.
The scientist explained that cyanide is dangerous and should never be touched.

Many tourists visit Paris to see the Eiffel Tower and enjoy the delicious food.
The name of a river in the United States; it can also refer to a town or a school.
We went to the Merrimack River to have a picnic by the water.

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 9: Amazing Arts
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Indefinite Pronouns and more

This file has five grammar lessons for Unit 9. Work through one lesson at a time, try the practice exercises, then check your answers at the bottom. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Indefinite Pronouns — The Basics
What it means and why it matters

Indefinite pronouns are words that replace nouns without saying exactly who or what they refer to. They let us talk about people, things, or places in a general way. For example, instead of naming a specific person, you can say someone. Instead of listing every object, you can say everything. This is very useful in both speaking and writing.
The four groups
Indefinite pronouns come in four families. Each family has words for people, things, and places:
When to use each group
some- words are used in positive statements and offers: "Someone left a gift on the desk." / "Would you like something to drink?"
any- words are used in questions and negative sentences: "Does anyone have a pencil?" / "I do not have anything to wear."
every- words mean ALL people, things, or places: "Everyone enjoyed the show." / "She looked everywhere."
no- words mean ZERO — none at all: "Nobody was late today." / "There is nothing left to eat."
The singular verb rule
All indefinite pronouns that end in -one, -body, or -thing are singular. They take a singular verb:
Everyone is here. (not "Everyone are here.")
Nobody wants to leave. (not "Nobody want to leave.")
Something smells wonderful.
Nothing has changed.
A common mistake
Mistake: "Everyone are happy about the mural." ✗
Fix: "Everyone is happy about the mural." ✓
Remember: even though "everyone" means many people, the word itself is singular.
Another common mistake is confusing someone and anyone:
Mistake: "Anyone left their jacket in the classroom." ✗ (This sounds like a question about any random person.)
Fix: "Someone left their jacket in the classroom." ✓ (A specific but unknown person.)
Memory tip

Think of the word "indefinite" as "not definite" — not specific. These pronouns help you when you do not need to name exactly who or what. Some = a positive unknown. Any = a question or negative unknown. Every = all of them. No = none of them.
Practice 1
Fill in the blank with the correct indefinite pronoun from the box:
(someone, anyone, everyone, no one, something, anything, nothing, everything)
______ left a beautiful painting on the teacher's desk. We do not know who.
Is ______ interested in joining the art club?
I have ______ to say about the project — it was perfect.
______ in the class enjoyed the music performance.
There is ______ to worry about — we are all prepared.
Does ______ have a spare paintbrush I can borrow?
______ was at the gallery when I arrived — it was completely empty.
______ was covered in paint after the art lesson!
Lesson 2: Indefinite Pronouns — Advanced Uses
What it means and why it matters
Now that you know the four groups, let us look at some more advanced rules. These will help you use indefinite pronouns like a confident English speaker.
Using they/their/them with indefinite pronouns
In modern English, we often use they, their, and them to refer back to a singular indefinite pronoun when we do not know or do not want to specify the person's gender:
"Someone left their bag on the chair."
"Everyone should bring their own notebook."
"If anyone needs help, they can ask the teacher."
This is now accepted in standard English. It avoids the awkwardness of writing "his or her" every time.
Indefinite pronouns with adjectives
When an adjective describes an indefinite pronoun, the adjective comes after the pronoun (not before):
"I want to eat something delicious." (not "delicious something")
"Is there anywhere quiet where I can study?" (not "quiet anywhere")
"We need someone brave to speak first." (not "brave someone")
Indefinite pronouns with else
You can add else after an indefinite pronoun to mean "another" or "different":
"Is there anything else you need?"
"Someone else can take the lead today."
"Let us go somewhere else — this park is too crowded."
Double negatives — a trap to avoid
In English, we do not use two negative words in the same clause:
Mistake: "I do not have nothing." ✗ (Two negatives cancel out.)
Fix: "I do not have anything." ✓   OR   "I have nothing." ✓
Practice 2
Choose the correct option in each sentence:
I need (something cold / cold something) to drink on this hot day.
If (anyone / someone) calls, tell them I am busy.
(Everyone / No one) brought (their / his) art supplies today, so we are all ready.
She does not want (anything / nothing) for her birthday.
Let us try (somewhere else / else somewhere) for dinner tonight.
There is (anyone / no one) in the classroom — it is completely empty.
(Somebody / Anybody) should clean up the paint spills before we leave.
I have not seen (anyone / someone) I know at this exhibition.
Lesson 3: Adjective Order
What it means and why it matters

In English, when we use more than one adjective before a noun, there is a natural order. If we put the adjectives in the wrong order, the sentence sounds odd to native speakers — even if the grammar is technically correct. Learning this order will make your writing sound polished and natural.
The OSASCOMP order
Use the word OSASCOMP to remember the standard order:
O — Opinion: beautiful, ugly, amazing, terrible, lovely
S — Size: big, small, tiny, enormous, tall, short
A — Age: old, new, young, ancient, modern
S — Shape: round, square, flat, rectangular, circular
C — Colour: red, blue, green, bright, dark, colourful
O — Origin: Somali, East African, British, Chinese, Kenyan
M — Material: wooden, metal, plastic, stone, glass, recycled
P — Purpose: cooking, sleeping, painting, writing, farming
Step-by-step guide
When you have several adjectives, follow these steps:
Identify each adjective and its category (opinion, size, age, etc.).
Place them in the OSASCOMP order.
Check: does the phrase sound natural when you say it out loud?
Examples
"a beautiful small old round bowl" — opinion + size + age + shape
"a large rectangular wooden board" — size + shape + material
"a lovely bright green Somali cloth" — opinion + colour + origin
"three tiny new circular metal trays" — quantity + size + age + shape + material
"a beautiful tall colourful East African scene" — opinion + size + colour + origin
A common mistake
Mistake: "a wooden large rectangular board" ✗ — material (wooden) came before size (large).
Fix: "a large rectangular wooden board" ✓ — size then shape then material.
Memory tip
Think: Opinion first, purpose last. What you think about the object goes at the very beginning, and what the object is used for goes at the end, just before the noun.
Practice 3
Put the adjectives in the correct order before the noun:
a ______ ______ ______ table (round / old / beautiful)
a ______ ______ ______ flag (Somali / large / blue)
______ ______ ______ drums (recycled / big / three)
a ______ ______ ______ mural (colourful / amazing / East African)
a ______ ______ ______ house (new / stone / small)
some ______ ______ ______ brushes (painting / long / wooden)
Lesson 4: Present Perfect Tense (Review)
What it means and why it matters

The present perfect tense connects the past with the present. It tells us about actions that happened at some point in the past but are still relevant now. In the story "The Children of Wind Valley," the characters use the present perfect to show what they have already accomplished:
"We have measured changes in temperature."
"We have mapped the affected zones."
"We have spoken to local farmers."
How to form it
Subject + have / has + past participle.
I / you / we / they + have + past participle: "We have planted fifty trees."
he / she / it + has + past participle: "She has inspired many people."
Negative: add not after have/has: "They have not finished yet." (or "haven't")
Question: put have/has before the subject: "Have you seen the new mural?"
Regular vs. irregular past participles
Regular: add -ed to the base verb: plant → planted, measure → measured, clean → cleaned.
Irregular: these must be memorised: go → gone, see → seen, build → built, speak → spoken, write → written, give → given, take → taken, make → made.
When to use the present perfect
For experiences: "I have visited three countries."
For achievements: "She has written a beautiful poem."
For changes over time: "The neighbourhood has changed a lot."
For unfinished time: "We have planted 47 trees this term." (the term is not over yet)
A common mistake
Mistake: "They have flourish in the subtropics." ✗ (Forgot the past participle form.)
Fix: "They have flourished in the subtropics." ✓
Mistake: "She have completed her painting." ✗ (Wrong form of have.)
Fix: "She has completed her painting." ✓
Memory tip
Think of the present perfect as a bridge between the past and the present. "Have" is the bridge, and the past participle is the action that has crossed over to affect the now.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with the present perfect form of the verb in brackets:
She ______ (visit) the art gallery three times this year.
They ______ (complete) their group project.
We ______ (not see) the finished mural yet.
______ you ever ______ (build) something from recycled materials?
The garden ______ (grow) much bigger since last month.
I ______ (write) a poem about nature.
He ______ (not speak) to the teacher about his idea yet.
The school ______ (plant) over a hundred trees this year.
Lesson 5: Putting It All Together
What it means and why it matters

In real English writing and speaking, we combine many grammar features in the same text. This lesson helps you practise using indefinite pronouns, adjective order, and the present perfect tense together — just as you will in the Writing Workshop.
Example paragraph
Read this paragraph and notice the grammar features:

"Our school has started a recycling project this term. Everyone in Year 6 has collected plastic bottles and old newspapers. Someone donated a large beautiful wooden box to store the materials. We have already made three small colourful recycled sculptures for the school garden. Nobody expected the project to grow so quickly. Now anyone who visits our school can see something amazing — art made from things that people once threw away."
In this paragraph, find:
Three indefinite pronouns and their group (some-, any-, every-, no-).
Two examples of adjective order (list the adjectives and their categories).
Two sentences using the present perfect tense.
Mixed Practice
Write your own paragraph (six to eight sentences) about an imaginary school project. Your paragraph must include:
At least three different indefinite pronouns.
At least two phrases with adjectives in the correct order.
At least two sentences using the present perfect tense.
At least three vocabulary words from Unit 9.
Answer Key
Practice 1 (Indefinite Pronouns — Basics)
1. Someone   2. anyone   3. nothing   4. Everyone   5. nothing   6. anyone   7. No one   8. Everything
Practice 2 (Indefinite Pronouns — Advanced)
1. something cold   2. anyone   3. Everyone, their   4. anything   5. somewhere else   6. no one   7. Somebody   8. anyone
Practice 3 (Adjective Order)

1. a beautiful old round table   2. a large blue Somali flag   3. three big recycled drums   4. an amazing colourful East African mural   5. a small new stone house   6. some long wooden painting brushes
Practice 4 (Present Perfect)
1. has visited   2. have completed   3. have not seen   4. Have ... built   5. has grown   6. have written   7. has not spoken   8. has planted

Why do we say everyone is instead of everyone are?
Can you give me five more adjective order phrases to practise?
