paragraphs: Unit 9: Amazing Arts
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
What You Will Learn

Welcome to Unit 9: Amazing Arts — the final unit of Year 6! In this unit you will explore how art, music, and creative expression can transform communities and inspire change. You will read about children who turn broken objects into beautiful instruments, empty walls into colourful murals, and dusty valleys into thriving green spaces. Through these stories and activities, you will sharpen your English skills and discover the power of creativity.

This unit connects reading, grammar, vocabulary, writing and speaking into one rich topic. By the end, you will be able to talk confidently about art, the environment, and community projects — all while using more advanced grammar like indefinite pronouns and adjective order.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a creative-arts passage and discuss its ideas in English.
Use indefinite pronouns (someone, anyone, everyone, nothing, etc.) correctly in sentences.
Order multiple adjectives properly before a noun (opinion → size → age → colour → origin → material).
Review the present perfect tense to describe what has already been done.
Write a detailed art project description using precise vocabulary.
Present your creative ideas clearly in a short spoken presentation.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Indefinite Pronouns, Adjective Order, Present Perfect review).
Read the Story file, “The Children of Wind Valley”, and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary

Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
Arts and Expression
transform — to change something into a different form or appearance.
cultivate — to prepare and care for something so it can grow.
flourished — grew or developed in a healthy, successful way.
landscape — the visible features of an area of land.
cursive — a style of writing where the letters are connected together.
pluralistic — having many different ideas, cultures, or groups living together.
Environment and Change
deforestation — the process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest.
preserve — to keep something in its original state so it is not damaged.
sacrifice — giving up something important for the sake of something else.
reform — to make changes to something to improve it.
activism — the act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause.
turbine — a machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy.
People and Society
collide — to hit something with a lot of force; to come into conflict.
relocate — to move to a different place.
overthrow — to remove a leader or government from power.
disgrace — a feeling of shame or loss of respect.
altitude — the height of something above sea level.
access — the ability to enter or use something.
Reading: The Mural Makers of Mogadishu
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.

In many cities around the world, art has the power to transform empty spaces into places of hope and beauty. One of the most exciting examples of this comes from communities where young people pick up paintbrushes instead of waiting for change to arrive. They decide that everyone deserves to see colour and creativity in their daily lives — and they make it happen themselves.

In a neighbourhood on the outskirts of a large East African city, a group of teenagers noticed that the walls along the main road were cracked, grey, and covered in dust. Nothing about the street felt welcoming. Families walked past quickly, and children had nowhere special to play. The teenagers decided to change that. They formed a group called the Mural Makers.

The Mural Makers began by collecting leftover paint from local shops and workshops. Someone donated old brushes. Anyone who wanted to help was welcome, and soon the group grew to more than thirty young artists. They did not need expensive equipment or access to a fancy studio. All they needed was a wall, a plan, and the commitment to finish what they started.

Their first project was a mural on the side of a community hall. Amina, the group’s leader, sketched the design in her notebook using neat cursive handwriting. The mural showed a landscape of green hills, a bright sun, and children planting trees together. “We want people to see what this place could look like,” Amina explained. “Art helps us imagine a better future.”

The teenagers learned to organise their adjectives carefully when describing their supplies. They asked for “a large rectangular wooden board” to mix paint on, and they described their finished mural as “a beautiful tall colourful East African scene.” This kind of careful adjective order made their English writing sound natural and polished.

Slowly, the neighbourhood began to change. Where there had been bare walls, there were now bright images of animals, flowers, and famous East African landscapes. An old man who had lived on the street for decades said, “Everything looks different now. I feel proud of where I live.”

But the Mural Makers did not stop at painting. They also started a campaign to preserve the few remaining trees on their street. Deforestation in the surrounding area had removed much of the shade, and the altitude of the neighbourhood meant hot winds blew dust through the streets. The group planted thirty new seedlings, each one painted with a small number in bright green.

One member of the group, Yusuf, was interested in music. He began building simple instruments from recycled materials — a drum from an old plastic container, a guitar from scrap wood and wire. “Something beautiful can come from anything,” he told the younger children who gathered to watch. He taught them simple rhythms and songs about caring for the environment.

Word about the Mural Makers spread to other parts of the city. A local newspaper wrote about their work. A school in another district invited them to paint a mural in the playground. Somebody from a community radio station interviewed Amina. “Art is activism,” she said. “When we paint, we are saying that this place matters. When we plant a tree, we are saying the future matters.”

Not everyone understood at first. Some adults thought the murals were a waste of time. One shopkeeper said, “Paint does not feed families.” But when more visitors started coming to the neighbourhood to see the murals, and when a small market opened next to the community hall, even the doubters began to see the value. Art had not only made the street beautiful — it had helped the local economy flourish.

The Mural Makers have now completed twelve murals, planted over a hundred trees, and built a small music workshop where children learn to play recycled instruments every Saturday. Their motto, painted in large cursive letters on the community hall, reads: “Nothing is wasted. Everything is art. Everyone belongs.”

Amina says she hopes the project will transform not just their neighbourhood but also how people think about young artists. “We have shown that anyone can create change,” she said. “You do not need permission to make the world more beautiful. You just need to start.”
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What problem did the teenagers notice about their street at the beginning of the passage?
How did the Mural Makers get the materials they needed to paint? Mention at least two ways.
What did Amina’s first mural show, and what message did she want it to send?
How did Yusuf contribute to the group in a different way from the other members?
The passage says “Art is activism.” In your own words, explain what Amina meant by this.
How did the murals help the local economy? Find the evidence in the text.
What is the Mural Makers’ motto? Why do you think they chose those three sentences?
Find three indefinite pronouns used in the passage and write the full sentence for each one.
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
The shopkeeper said, “Paint does not feed families.” Do you agree or disagree? Give your reasons.
Amina says, “You do not need permission to make the world more beautiful.” What does this mean for young people in your community?
If you could paint a mural on a wall in your town or neighbourhood, what would you paint and why?
How is what the Mural Makers did similar to what the children do in this unit’s story, “The Children of Wind Valley”?
Vocabulary in Context
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
transform
commitment
preserve
deforestation
activism
flourish
Grammar Focus: Indefinite Pronouns
This section introduces a key grammar topic for Unit 9. You will study it in more detail in your Grammar companion file, but here is a summary to get you started.
What are indefinite pronouns?
Indefinite pronouns are words that refer to people or things without saying exactly who or what they are. They are very useful when you want to talk about people or things in a general way.
There are four main groups:
some- words (for positive statements): someone, somebody, something, somewhere.
any- words (for questions and negatives): anyone, anybody, anything, anywhere.
every- words (for all people or things): everyone, everybody, everything, everywhere.
no- words (for zero, none): no one, nobody, nothing, nowhere.
Important grammar rules
Indefinite pronouns that end in -one, -body, or -thing are always singular. This means they take a singular verb:
Everyone is welcome. (not “Everyone are welcome.”)
Nobody knows the answer. (not “Nobody know the answer.”)
Something smells wonderful in the kitchen.
However, in modern English, we often use they/their/them to refer back to an indefinite pronoun when we do not know the person’s gender:
Someone left their bag in the classroom.
Everyone should bring their own notebook.
Using indefinite pronouns in different sentence types
Look at how we choose the right pronoun depending on the type of sentence:
Positive statement: Someone donated old paintbrushes to the group.
Question: Did anyone bring extra paint today?
Negative meaning: No one wanted to miss the mural unveiling.
Universal: Everything in the workshop was made from recycled materials.
Indefinite pronouns in the reading passage
Go back to the reading passage and find at least five indefinite pronouns. For each one, write:
The full sentence it appears in.
Whether it is a some-, any-, every-, or no- pronoun.
Whether it refers to a person, a thing, or a place.
Quick Practice
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun to complete each sentence:
______ (Someone / Anyone) left a beautiful drawing on the teacher’s desk.
Is there ______ (something / anything) we can use to clean these brushes?
______ (Everyone / No one) in the class enjoyed the art exhibition.
There is ______ (nothing / anything) wrong with making mistakes — that is how we learn.
She looked ______ (everywhere / somewhere) for her paintbrush but could not find it.
______ (Nobody / Somebody) told me the concert was cancelled, so I went anyway.
Answers: 1. Someone   2. anything   3. Everyone   4. nothing   5. everywhere   6. Nobody
Grammar Focus: Adjective Order
When we use more than one adjective before a noun, English has a natural order. If we get the order wrong, the sentence sounds strange, even if the grammar is technically correct.
The standard adjective order
Remember the order using the letters OSASCOMP:
O — Opinion (beautiful, ugly, amazing, terrible)
S — Size (big, small, tiny, enormous, tall)
A — Age (old, new, young, ancient)
S — Shape (round, square, flat, rectangular)
C — Colour (red, blue, green, bright, dark)
O — Origin (Somali, East African, British, Chinese)
M — Material (wooden, metal, plastic, stone)
P — Purpose (cooking, sleeping, painting, writing)
Examples from the passage
Notice how adjectives are ordered naturally in these phrases from the reading:
“a large rectangular wooden board” — size (large) + shape (rectangular) + material (wooden)
“a beautiful tall colourful East African scene” — opinion (beautiful) + size (tall) + colour (colourful) + origin (East African)
More examples
“a small old round table” — size + age + shape
“a lovely bright green Somali cloth” — opinion + colour + origin
“a tiny new circular metal tray” — size + age + shape + material
A common mistake
Mistake: “a wooden large rectangular board” ✔ This sounds wrong because material (wooden) comes before size (large).
Fix: “a large rectangular wooden board” ✔ Size → shape → material.
Quick Practice
Put the adjectives in the correct order before the noun:
a ______ ______ ______ drum (old / big / plastic)
a ______ ______ ______ mural (East African / colourful / beautiful)
______ ______ ______ paintbrushes (wooden / new / small)
a ______ ______ ______ building (stone / ancient / enormous)
Answers: 1. a big old plastic drum   2. a beautiful colourful East African mural   3. small new wooden paintbrushes   4. an enormous ancient stone building
Grammar Practice
These exercises bring together indefinite pronouns and adjective order. Complete them in your notebook, then check your answers at the bottom or ask your AI tutor.
Exercise A: Indefinite Pronouns in Context
Complete each sentence with the best indefinite pronoun from the box.
(someone, anyone, everyone, no one, something, anything, nothing, everything, somewhere, nowhere)
______ brought recycled bottles to the art class — we do not know who it was.
Is there ______ in this room who can play a musical instrument?
After the clean-up, ______ was left on the ground — it was spotless!
______ in our school believes that creativity is important.
I looked ______ for my sketchbook, but I cannot find it.
We need ______ strong to help carry the paint cans to the hall.
______ in her art portfolio was made from recycled materials.
There was ______ to sit in the crowded gallery, so we stood near the back.
Answers: 1. Someone   2. anyone   3. nothing   4. Everyone   5. everywhere   6. someone   7. Everything   8. nowhere
Exercise B: Adjective Order Challenge
Rewrite each phrase with the adjectives in the correct order:
a (Somali / traditional / beautiful) rug
(recycled / small / three) drums
a (rectangular / new / large) canvas
an (stone / amazing / ancient / East African) carving
some (metal / shiny / tiny) beads
Answers: 1. a beautiful traditional Somali rug   2. three small recycled drums   3. a large new rectangular canvas   4. an amazing ancient East African stone carving   5. some tiny shiny metal beads
Exercise C: Present Perfect Review
The present perfect tense (have/has + past participle) is used throughout the story and reading passage to show actions that started in the past and connect to the present. Complete these sentences:
The Mural Makers ______ (complete) twelve murals so far.
Amina ______ (inspire) many young artists in her community.
We ______ (plant) over a hundred trees this year.
Yusuf ______ (build) instruments from recycled materials.
The children ______ (show) that anyone can create change.
I ______ (never / visit) an art gallery before.
Answers: 1. have completed   2. has inspired   3. have planted   4. has built   5. have shown   6. have never visited
Exercise D: Mixed Grammar — Write Your Own
Write six sentences about your community or school. Each sentence must include:
Two sentences with an indefinite pronoun (e.g., “Everyone in my neighbourhood enjoys…”).
Two sentences with two or more adjectives in the correct order (e.g., “We have a large old wooden gate.”).
Two sentences using the present perfect tense (e.g., “Our school has planted new trees.”).
Writing Workshop: My Art Project Description

In this section, you will plan and write a detailed description of an art project. This could be a real project you have done, or one you imagine. The goal is to practise your vocabulary, grammar, and descriptive writing skills.
Step 1: Choose Your Project
Pick one of these art project ideas, or create your own:
A mural for a wall in your school or neighbourhood.
A set of musical instruments made from recycled materials.
A poetry and art exhibition about protecting the environment.
A community garden with painted signs and sculptures.
A photography project showing the beauty of your local landscape.
Step 2: Plan Your Description
Before you write, answer these planning questions in your notebook:
What is your project called?
Where will it take place? Describe the location.
What materials do you need? Use correct adjective order to describe them (e.g., “ten small recycled plastic bottles”).
Who will help you? Use indefinite pronouns (e.g., “Anyone who wants to help is welcome.”).
What will the finished project look like? Use vivid adjectives.
Why is this project important? How will it transform the community?
What have you already done to prepare? Use the present perfect (e.g., “I have already collected paint.”).
Step 3: Write Your Description
Write four to five paragraphs. Follow this structure:
Paragraph 1 — Introduction: Name your project, say where it will happen, and explain why it matters.
Paragraph 2 — Materials and Team: Describe what you need and who will help. Use adjective order and indefinite pronouns.
Paragraph 3 — The Process: Explain what you will do, step by step. Use sequence words like first, next, then, finally.
Paragraph 4 — The Vision: Describe what the finished project will look like and how it will help the community. Use the present perfect to say what you have already accomplished.
Paragraph 5 — Conclusion: End with a strong statement about why art and creativity matter. You may use a quote or a motto, like the Mural Makers’ motto from the reading passage.
Step 4: Edit and Improve
Use this checklist to review your writing:
Have I used at least two indefinite pronouns correctly?   ☐
Have I described at least two items using correct adjective order?   ☐
Have I used the present perfect tense at least once?   ☐
Have I used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?   ☐
Does every sentence start with a capital letter and end with punctuation?   ☐
Have I read my work out loud to check that it sounds natural?   ☐
Model Paragraph
Here is an example of a well-written first paragraph to guide you:

“Our project is called Colours of Hope. We plan to paint a large beautiful colourful mural on the wall of our community hall. The wall has been grey and empty for years, and everyone in the neighbourhood walks past it every day. We believe that art can transform a dull space into something that brings people together. We have already collected paint and brushes from local shops, and someone has donated a tall wooden ladder for us to reach the top of the wall.”
Listening & Speaking
Listening: An Interview with a Young Artist
Ask your AI tutor to read this interview aloud while you listen. Then read it yourself.
Interviewer: Tell us about your art group.
Layla: We started last year with just five people. Now everyone in our class wants to join! We paint murals on school walls and make instruments from recycled things.
Interviewer: What materials do you use?
Layla: We use anything we can find. Old cans become drums. Cardboard becomes a canvas. Nothing goes to waste. We even made a beautiful small round Somali drum from a cooking-oil tin.
Interviewer: What has been your biggest challenge?
Layla: At first, nobody took us seriously. Some people thought we were just playing. But when they saw our first mural, everything changed. Now the whole community supports us.
Interviewer: What advice do you have for other young artists?
Layla: Start with what you have. You do not need expensive materials. Anyone can create something beautiful. Just believe in yourself and keep going.
After listening, answer these questions:
How many people were in Layla’s group when it started?
Name two recycled materials the group uses.
What was the group’s biggest challenge?
What advice does Layla give to other young artists?
Find four indefinite pronouns in the interview and say which group they belong to (some-, any-, every-, no-).
Speaking 1: Present Your Art Project
Prepare a short spoken presentation (about two minutes) about the art project you described in the Writing Workshop. Follow this structure:
Introduction: Say the name of your project and where it will happen.
Description: Explain what you will create and what materials you need.
Importance: Say why this project matters for your community.
Call to action: Invite your audience to help. Use an indefinite pronoun: “Anyone who wants to join is welcome.”
Record yourself and listen back. Check that you spoke clearly and used vocabulary from this unit.
Speaking 2: Debate — Does Art Really Change Communities?
In pairs or with your AI tutor, debate this question: Does art really change communities, or is it just decoration?
One person argues for (art does change communities) and the other argues against (art is just decoration). Use evidence from the reading passage and the story to support your arguments.
For: “Art transformed the neighbourhood because…” / “The Mural Makers have shown that…”
Against: “The shopkeeper said that paint does not feed families, and…” / “Some people think…”
After the debate, write one paragraph explaining which side you agree with and why.
Speaking Game: Who Am I?
Play this game with your AI tutor or a classmate:
Think of a person from the reading passage or the story (for example, Amina, Yusuf, Achieng, Omondi).
Give three clues about the person without saying their name. Use indefinite pronouns and adjective order.
Example: “This person started something special. They painted a beautiful tall colourful mural. Everyone admires their work.”
The other person guesses who it is. Then swap.
Check Your Learning

You have now completed Unit 9: Amazing Arts. This is the final unit of Year 6 — well done for making it this far! Use the checklist below to see what you have learned and what you might want to review.
Self-Assessment
Tick “Yes” or “Not yet”. For every “Not yet”, ask your AI tutor for more practice.
I can read and understand a passage about art and community change.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use indefinite pronouns (someone, anyone, everyone, nothing, etc.) correctly.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can put adjectives in the correct order before a noun.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can use the present perfect tense to describe completed actions.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can write a detailed art project description using vocabulary from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can speak clearly about creative ideas in a short presentation.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I know at least fifteen vocabulary words from this unit.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
I can explain why art and creativity matter for communities.   Yes ☐   Not yet ☐
Reflection
In your notebook, write a short paragraph (three to five sentences) answering this question:
What is the most important thing you learned in Unit 9, and how will you use it outside of school?
End-of-Year Celebration

Congratulations! You have completed all nine units of Year 6 English. Look back at how far you have come since Unit 1. You have learned to read stories, write descriptions, debate ideas, and express yourself clearly in English.
Here are some things to be proud of:
You can discuss complex topics like the environment, media, money, and now the arts.
You know hundreds of English vocabulary words and can use them in sentences.
You can use grammar confidently: modal verbs, conditionals, relative clauses, passive voice, indefinite pronouns, and more.
You have read and understood nine stories set in East Africa and beyond.
You have become a more confident reader, writer, speaker, and thinker in English.
Keep reading, keep writing, keep speaking. English is a skill that grows with practice. Ask your AI tutor: “What should I do over the holidays to keep my English strong?”
Self-Study Tips
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my presentation and give me feedback?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more questions about indefinite pronouns?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
A Simple Daily Routine
Well done — you have completed the final unit of Year 6! Be proud of every word you have learned, every sentence you have written, and every idea you have shared. Keep creating! 🌟

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 9: Amazing Arts
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "The Children of Wind Valley"
The Children of Wind Valley

The village of Lusinga rested in a wide valley surrounded by high ridges and evergreen trees. It was once famous for its cool climate, wildflowers, and strong winds that whispered through the hills. But recently, the land had begun to suffer.
"There used to be so many trees here," said Achieng, age 13, as she climbed a slope with her twin brother, Omondi. "Now look at this mess."

The mountain landscape was dry. Broken stumps replaced old forest trails. Empty cans and plastic hung on fallen branches. People had cleared much of the forest for firewood and farming. It was called deforestation — and it had changed everything.
"Even the wind feels wrong," said Omondi. "It's hotter now. The streams have dried. And the peppermint plants are gone."
They sat on a rock overlooking the valley.
"We need to do something," Achieng said.
At school the next day, their science teacher announced a project.
"You will design a plan to help the environment," she said. "This region has suffered. Now it's your turn to help it recover."
"What can we do?" asked a boy.
"That's for you to analyze," she replied. "It may involve new tools, new rules — or even a new way of thinking."
The students were excited. Achieng and Omondi formed a group with their classmates Jamila and Kiptoo. They named their group The Children of Wind Valley.
The first thing they did was research.
"We've already lost 60% of our forest," said Jamila, reading aloud. "Many animals have relocated. The soil is dry. And we've had more floods this year than ever before."
Omondi pointed at a graph. "Temperatures have flourished, too. This valley used to be cooler. Now it's close to the subtropics."
Kiptoo nodded. "My grandmother says the wind used to sing. Now it screams."
They decided on a goal: to preserve what was left and replant what had been lost.
They made posters and maps. Kiptoo wrote all their titles in neat cursive. One said:
"Plant. Protect. Pass it on."
Jamila added captions:

"Wind Valley is our heritage.
Nature is not just space — it's memory."
They visited a local farm with a wind turbine. The farmer explained how it worked.
"The turbine collects the wind's power and turns it into electricity," he said.
"Could we build a small one at school?" Omondi asked.
"With the right support," the farmer replied. "You'd need planning, commitment, and money."
The team met under the big fig tree after school. Jamila held up a drawing.
"Imagine it," she said. "A small wind farm. A tree nursery. A learning centre."
"It'll take real sacrifice," said Achieng. "Time, energy... Maybe people won't like it."
"But it could be a model for other schools," said Kiptoo. "A thriving space for nature and learning."
They called it the Ridge Replanting Project.
They wrote a proposal using the present perfect:

"We have measured changes in temperature and soil quality.
We have mapped the affected zones.
We have spoken to local farmers and elders."
They practised future tense:

"We will plant 100 trees.
We will build a wind model.
We will create a new environmental club."
They used clear adjective order too: "a small wooden solar-powered nursery," and "a wide circular concrete platform."
Not everyone supported them.
"This is just school talk," said a shopkeeper. "It's not real."
One man shouted, "Stop blaming us for cutting trees! You want us to starve?"
Some accused them of trying to overthrow tradition.
"That's not our goal," Achieng said in a public meeting. "We're not blaming. We're planting. Not to destroy the past — but to protect the future."
The district officer, Ms. Nabwire, came to their school.
"I've read your proposal," she said. "It's detailed. And powerful. Especially your section on reform."
She showed them her notes. "You've used real data. Emotional writing. And vision."
"Will we get support?" Achieng asked.
"You've earned a pilot grant," she said. "Enough for 50 seedlings, and a model turbine. But you must monitor everything — and report weekly."
They were shocked. "We did it!" said Jamila.
That weekend, they began the planting. Students dug holes. Elders helped carry compost. A local artist painted signs:
"This ridge is a pluralistic place — where animals, trees, and children all belong."
They cleaned an old storehouse and turned it into a learning room.
They called it The Green Room.
Inside, Achieng taped a sign on the wall:

"We have sacrificed comfort for hope. We have collided with doubt.
But this is our space of transformation."
By the end of the term, 47 trees had survived. Bees returned. Butterflies fluttered near the new flowers. Some peppermint bushes sprouted again.
The school held an open day. Visitors toured the ridge and the Green Room. Many said it was beautiful.
Occasionally, someone called it "just a school project."
But then came a letter — from a school in Rwanda.

"We heard about your work.
Could you send us your report?
We want to start something too."
In the final assembly, the principal read from their original submission.

"We believe in environmental action rooted in education and respect.
The wind has shaped our stories. Now we shape the wind."
The entire hall applauded.
Outside, the wind turned again — this time softer. The trees moved gently, and the ridge whispered.
Achieng smiled.
"They said we couldn't change anything."
"And yet," said Omondi, "we've changed everything."
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the story.
What was the village of Lusinga once famous for?
What had happened to the forest and why? Use the word deforestation in your answer.
Who were the four members of the group, and what did they call themselves?
What did the group learn from their research? Name at least two facts.
Describe the Ridge Replanting Project. What did the group plan to do?
How did the group use the present perfect tense in their proposal? Give one example.
Not everyone supported the children. What did the shopkeeper and one man say?
How did Achieng respond to the criticism at the public meeting?
What did the district officer, Ms. Nabwire, think of their proposal?
What happened by the end of the term? How did the environment begin to change?
Why was the letter from Rwanda important?
What does Omondi mean when he says, "we've changed everything"?
Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
The shopkeeper said the project was "not real." Do you think he was right? Why or why not?
Achieng said, "We're not blaming. We're planting." What does this tell us about her approach to change?
The sign in the Green Room says, "We have sacrificed comfort for hope." What sacrifices did the children make?
The story ends with the image of the wind turning softer. What do you think this symbolises?
How is this story connected to the reading passage about the Mural Makers?
If you could start a project like this in your own community, what would you do?
Find the Vocabulary Words
Read the story again. Find these Unit 9 words and read out the sentence they appear in:
landscape
deforestation
peppermint
analyze
classmates
relocated
flourished
subtropics
preserve
cursive
turbine
sacrifice
thriving
overthrow
reform
pluralistic
collided
occasionally
transform
Grammar in the Story
The story uses several grammar features from this unit. Find examples of:
Present perfect tense: Find at least three sentences that use "have" or "has" + past participle.
Future tense: Find two sentences that use "will" to talk about plans.
Adjective order: Find the two phrases where adjectives appear in a specific order before a noun.
Reported speech: Find one example where someone's words are reported rather than quoted directly.
Now You Try: Writing
Writing Activities
Retell a scene: Choose your favourite part of the story and retell it in your own words. Write at least five sentences. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit.

Write a letter: Imagine you are the students in Rwanda who wrote to the Children of Wind Valley. Write a short letter (six to eight sentences) asking about their project. Use at least two indefinite pronouns.
Create a motto: The group's motto was "Plant. Protect. Pass it on." Create your own three-word motto for an environmental or art project. Then write a paragraph explaining what it means.

Art project plan: Using the skills from the Writing Workshop in your Lesson file, write a short description (one paragraph) of a project similar to the Ridge Replanting Project that you could start at your school.

Theme: Environmental justice, sacrifice, and the power of young people to create change.
Setting: A mountain town near Mount Elgon, East Africa.

Read it slowly. When you meet a vocabulary word from your list (shown in bold), say it out loud!

paragraphs: Vocabulary  —  Unit 9: Amazing Arts
Year 6 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-study word book

These are the important words for Unit 9. Learn a few words each day. For every word, read the meaning, say the example out loud, and then try the practice activities. Your AI tutor can check your sentences and help you say the words correctly.
Group 1: Nature and Geography
Practise these words
Use the word landscape to describe a place you have visited or seen in a picture.
Explain in one sentence why deforestation is harmful to the environment.
Finish the sentence: "The turbine works by using the power of ______ to create ______."
Group 2: Activism and Social Change
Practise these words
Write a sentence using activism to describe something young people do to help their community.
Explain the difference between reform and overthrow in your own words.
Use sacrifice and thriving in the same sentence about a school project.
Group 3: People, Action, and Abstract Ideas
Practise these words
Use the word transform to describe a change you have seen in your school or neighbourhood.
Write a sentence using commitment about something you are dedicated to.
Explain what cultivate means. Can it be used for things other than plants? Give an example.
Use collide in a sentence about two different ideas meeting.
My Word Wall
Tick each word when you can read it, say it, and use it in a sentence. Come back at the end of the unit and check again!
Nature and Geography
deforestation ☐   landscape ☐   altitude ☐   subtropics ☐   peppermint ☐   turbine ☐
Activism and Social Change
reform ☐   activism ☐   preserve ☐   sacrifice ☐   overthrow ☐   thriving ☐
flourished ☐   pluralistic ☐   disgrace ☐
People, Action, and Abstract Ideas

End-of-Unit Vocabulary Challenge
When you have learned all the words, try this challenge:
Choose any five words from your Word Wall. Write one sentence for each that connects to the theme of Amazing Arts.
Write a short paragraph (four to six sentences) using at least six vocabulary words from this unit. The paragraph should be about an art project, an environmental project, or a community change.
Ask your AI tutor to quiz you on all 29 words. Try to get at least 25 correct!
tables: The process of cutting down or removing trees from a forest or area of land.
Deforestation can harm animals that live in the trees and cause floods.
The visible features of an area of land, including mountains, trees, and water.
The landscape was beautiful, with tall mountains and green fields.
The height of something above sea level or ground level.
The airplane flew at a high altitude, far above the clouds.
The area of land just below the tropics, where the weather is warm and sometimes dry.
Many plants that grow in the subtropics need lots of sunshine and warm temperatures.
A type of plant with a strong, fresh smell and taste, often used in teas and sweets.
I love to drink peppermint tea when I feel cold.
A machine that uses the movement of air or water to create energy or power.
The wind turbine on the hill helps to produce electricity for our town.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the nature and geography words?
What is the difference between altitude and subtropics?
Can you help me pronounce deforestation correctly?
To make changes to something in order to improve it.
The school wants to reform the rules to make them fairer for everyone.
The act of working to make a change in society or to help a cause.
Many young people join activism to help protect the environment.
To keep something in its original state or condition so it is not damaged or spoiled.
The scientists preserved the ancient artefacts in special containers.
Giving up something important or valuable for the sake of something else.
She made a sacrifice by giving her lunch to a friend who forgot theirs.
To remove a leader or government from power, usually by force.
The people wanted to overthrow the unfair ruler and create a better government.
Doing very well and growing strong or healthy.
The plants in the garden are thriving because they get plenty of sunlight.
To grow or develop in a healthy way; to be successful.

Having many different ideas, cultures, or groups living together.
